init1_name1: Manastirliu 650 schools will be equipped with intelligent laboratories to increase students digital skills
init1_justif1: Led by the Minister of Education and Sports Ogerta Manastirliu in cooperation with the National Agency for the Information Society this initiative advances AI literacy by expanding intelligent laboratories to 650 schools nationwide. The SmartLabs infrastructure enables students starting from the first grade to develop coding cybersecurity and broader digital competencies that form the foundation for AI-related skills. By embedding technology-based learning across primary and secondary education and coupling infrastructure investment with curriculum support in STEM and digital subjects the government institutionalizes early nationwide AI readiness and digital skill development.
init1_name2: Preparations for the new school year Manastirliu For the first time knowledge about AI is included in the curriculum
init1_justif2: This initiative lead by the ministry of education addresses AI literacy by introducing basic knowledge about artificial intelligence within the formal pre-university curriculum at an early stage of education. It supports student awareness of what AI is and how it is used framed within digital learning objectives.

init1_name1: Establishment of the Special Parliamentary Committee to Coordinate and Oversee All Institutional Actions to Combat Disinformation and Other Forms of Foreign Interference in Democratic ProcessesEstablishment of the Special Parliamentary Committee to Coordinate and Oversee All Institutional Actions to Combat Disinformation and Other Forms of Foreign Interference in Democratic Processes
init1_justif1: The initiative addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by establishing a Special Parliamentary Committee with a mandate to coordinate and oversee institutional actions against disinformation and foreign interference in democratic processes. It explicitly targets content disseminated through bots algorithmic amplification and coordinated inauthentic behavior and calls for reviewing algorithms used to display prioritize distribute or remove content. The committee is tasked with proposing administrative or legislative measures to increase platform accountability and counter hate speech and manipulation while safeguarding freedom of expression (Section II page 1).

init1_name1: ON THE APPROVAL OF THE METHODOLOGY DOCUMENT AND TECHNICAL STANDARDS FOR THE USE OF AI IN THE REPUBLIC OF ALBANIA
init1_justif1: The standards emphasize under Principle 2.4 large-scale human capacity building to prepare for AI-driven labor market transformation. It highlights the need to equip AI practitioners with advanced technical skills while also training sector-specific professionals such as doctors to effectively use AI as an augmentative tool.
init1_name2: Government AI Campus
init1_justif2: The Government of Albania through the Minister of State for Public Administration and Anti-Corruption and the National Agency for Information Society (AKSHI) is implementing AI skill development in the public sector. By launching the “Government AI Campus” in partnership with the Tony Blair Institute and Apolitical it provides structured AI training (Awareness Practitioner Leadership) for civil servants and managers. The initiative equips public employees with practical AI competencies for governance constituting concrete capacity-building action.

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
imp1_body_name: Commissioner for the Right to Information and Protection of Personal Data
init1_name1: Commissioner for the Right to Information and Protection of Personal Data
init1_justif1: The Commissioner for the Right to Information and Protection of Personal Data oversees compliance with Law No. 124/2024 On the Protection of Personal Data including rules on automated decision-making. Under Art. 14–15 (pg 11–13) and Art. 22 (pg 18–19) controllers must provide meaningful information about the logic involved and enable human intervention. The Commissioner investigates complaints conducts inspections and orders corrective measures ensuring AI-driven decisions affecting individuals are explainable and reviewable.
init1_name2: Decision No 01 dated 3004-2025 On Determination of Countries Which Ensure an Adequate Level of Personal Data Protection
init1_justif2: Decision No. 01 (30.04.2025) operationalizes cross-border data transfer rules under Law No. 124/2024 On the Protection of Personal Data. It defines adequacy criteria including the existence of independent supervisory authorities and guarantees of transparency in personal data processing. By listing countries considered adequate it clarifies lawful international data flows supporting accountability where AI systems rely on cross-border data transfers.

init1_name1: Algerias participation in the International Youth Robotics and AI Competition
init1_justif1: National-level executive authority (Ministry of National Education) leading and representing Algeria in an international competition. This initiative supports AI literacy by exposing school-age students to core AI-related concepts such as algorithms automation robotics programming and problem-solving through a government-endorsed educational activity. By formally supporting participation in an international robotics and AI competition the Ministry of National Education promotes early understanding and practical engagement with AI technologies helping students develop foundational knowledge and skills relevant to AI in an age-appropriate applied learning context even though it remains extracurricular rather than curriculum-wide.

init1_name1: Skills Center
init1_justif1: The Skills Centers initiative addresses AI reskilling and upskilling by providing government-led free and structured training in artificial intelligence and related advanced digital technologies (such as cloud computing cybersecurity and robotics). By targeting youth and future workers the initiative directly builds AI-relevant technical skills needed for an evolving labour market and supports workforce adaptation to AI-driven digital transformation even in the absence of a binding national reskilling framework.

init1_name1: Secretary of State defends continued investment in digital literacy in schools
init1_justif1: At the 2024 Technology and Education Forum (FTE) the Secretary of State for Secondary Education emphasized the need to continue investing in digital literacy in schools including the use of emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence. She highlighted government efforts to equip schools with technology and improve teacher training to expand access and quality of education. The statement links digital literacy and AI skills to preparing students for Angola’s technological development.

It contributes to Public and Private Sector Skills Development by engaging civil servants and different stakeholders in AI governance ethics innovation and applications enhancing competencies and fostering responsible AI integration in public administration.

init1_name1: The White Paper on Information and Communication Technologies (2023-2027)
init1_type1: Commissioning concept notes white papers or outlines that serve as inputs for drafting.
init1_justif1: The White Paper on Information and Communication Technologies (2023–2027) demonstrates Angola’s commitment to developing national capacity in AI. Although the National AI Strategy has not yet been created according to the White Paper the Executive through the ENIA aims to set a policy direction to prepare the workforce for AI by prioritizing future initiatives for upskilling and reskilling. These initiatives include plans to develop AI and data skills through a National Training Plan youth talent development STEM-aligned curriculum reform and teacher training to build early AI literacy (pp. 99–101).
init1_name2: Partnership between Angola and the UAE to modernize AI systems support AI research and upskill the workforce by training Angolan professionals
init1_justif2: The initiative strengthens AI skills and workforce capacity through international cooperation and research networks. The Memorandum of Understanding with the UAE includes developing algorithms for smart public services joint research projects training Angolan public-sector professionals in AI and participation in international knowledge networks and events. Engagements with Huawei and visits to AI research centres in China further support exposure to advanced AI systems and practical applications.

init1_name1: Training cycle in AI and Public Policy for members of the National Public Administration
init1_justif1: The National Institute of Public Administration (INAP) provides government staff with structured training on artificial intelligence data governance and automated systems. Specifically training activities are promoted based on an articulating axis aimed at raising awareness and strengthening the capacities of public agents in ICT with special emphasis on Artificial Intelligence. The virtual course is available to public employees with responsibilities at some stage of the process of a substantive public policy in their jurisdiction with or without personnel in charge.

init1_justif1: A partnership between Amazon Web Service and The Ministry of Economy provides massive free online training in cloud technologies and artificial intelligence aiming to train over 100000 people by 2025. It builds workforce capacities to manage AI-enabled systems fosters talent development for emerging AI jobs and reduces vulnerability in labor markets undergoing digital transformation.

init1_name1: STEPai AI educational programs will be introduced in high schools
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Education Science Culture and Sports of Armenia approved the “STEP.ai – Artificial Intelligence” educational program for 10th grade. Developed since 2019 by Synopsys Armenia UEICT and AGBU Armenian Virtual College with input from international experts the three-year curriculum introduces AI fundamentals to high school students (grades 10–12). Offered in hybrid and online formats it promotes AI literacy logical thinking and problem-solving through project-based learning. Supported by leading tech companies the program links education with industry needs provides internships and mentorship and aims to foster a new generation of AI specialists to drive Armenia’s digital transformation.

init1_justif1: The Council of Europe and the National Assembly of Armenia held a discussion on AI in healthcare addressing ethical and human rights implications. The event examined amendments needed in Armenia’s legal framework human rights challenges and European standards from the CDBIO report on AI’s impact on the patient-doctor relationship. Topics included patient autonomy health data equitable access and professional standards -underscoring the importance of human oversight.

init1_name1: Factsheet for police Non-consensual sharing of sexual material
init1_justif1: This factsheet explains the offences introduced by the Criminal Code Amendment (Deepfake Sexual Material) Act 2024 and clarifies that the offence applies to sexual material created or altered using digital technology including AI-generated deepfakes (pp. 1-3). It guides police on identifying investigating and prosecuting cases where AI-generated or manipulated images are shared without consent. By clarifying that such synthetic material falls within the offence the factsheet supports law-enforcement responses to AI-facilitated image-based abuse and technology-enabled violence.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
init1_name1: Review of AI and the Australian Consumer Law
init1_justif1: The Government Treasury’s discussion paper Review of AI and the Australian Consumer Law constitutes a governmental initiative addressing access to redress and remedies by clarifying how existing ACL protections apply to AI-enabled goods and services. It discusses the remedies available to consumers in this context (p. 2) confirms that the ACL’s technology-neutral framework remains applicable without requiring reform (p. 6) and explains that consumers may be entitled to compensation where a failure to meet a consumer guarantee causes loss or damage (p. 11) while also noting that AI systems may create additional challenges for accessing remedies (p. 12).

init1_name1: eSafety Annual Report Program Monitoring Implementation of Online Safety Frameworks
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Australian Communications and Media Authority ACMA-eSafety Annual Report 2023-24 includes performance statements regulatory outcomes and data on compliance schemes under the Online Safety Act 2021 (which underpins the Online Safety (Basic Online Safety Expectations) Amendment Determination 2024). The report provides metrics and narrative about how online service providers address harms — including through algorithmic systems age-assurance (pp. 197-198). and moderation — thus supporting children’s rights to online safety and protection from AI-enabled risks (p. 216).
init1_name2: AustraliaUS Joint Council Safety by Design Workshop (Dec 2024)
init1_justif2: A two-day Safety by Design workshop co-led by the eSafety Commissioner and U.S. DHS gathered industry and global experts to develop a toolkit ensuring child safety is embedded throughout product development including in new and emerging AI technologies. The Joint Council will build on the outputs of this workshop and continue to work with industry to develop a Safety by Design toolkit to ensure that child safety is prioritized at every stage in the development of online products and services including in new and emerging artificial intelligence technologies. The toolkit is expected to collate best practices and share innovative approaches for companies to tackle online CSEA while also exploring the challenges and limitations of adopting.

init1_name1: Safe and responsible AI in Australia consultation - Australian Governments interim response
init1_justif1: The Safe and responsible AI in Australia consultation – Australian Government’s interim response by the Australian Government Department of Industry Science and Resources (p.2) notes that part of the discussion were many submissions that emphasised the importance of embedding national and cyber security measures in AI development and deployment a number of submissions called for AI development to incorporate secure-by-design practices to ensure security is a core business goal from the start of development.(p. 15) and suggests introducing obligations for accountability (for example requiring training for developers and deployers and clearer obligations to make organisations accountable and liable for AI safety risks) (p. 4).
init1_name2: National AI Centre
init1_justif2: The National Artificial Intelligence Centre (NAIC) promotes inclusive and responsible AI adoption across Australia by supporting SMEs industry and government to build trustworthy AI systems. Its mandate emphasises responsible AI diversity in AI capability development and engagement across sectors and communities. Through national programs partnerships and ecosystem-building activities NAIC helps ensure AI systems reflect Australian societal values and diverse user needs including underrepresented communities.
init1_name3: Responsible AI Network
init1_justif3: A national research and policy network advancing AI ethics diversity and transparency. Produces reports practical frameworks and sector-specific guidance to operationalise recommendations 4–5 of the Committee’s report.

imp1_body: n/a
init1_name1: Senate Select Committee Report on Adopting AI (AI)
init1_justif1: The report by the Senate Committee examines the environmental impacts of AI systems and infrastructure noting that AI development and deployment can significantly increase energy consumption greenhouse gas emissions water use and pressure on land and resources (Chapter 6 pp.143-165). It highlights the environmental costs of AI model training and data centre operations and discusses approaches for capturing reducing and regulating these impacts (pp. 148 to 158). The committee recommends that the Australian Government take a coordinated holistic approach to managing the growth of AI infrastructure to ensure development is environmentally sustainable (Recommendation 13 p.165).

init1_justif1: These consultations address gender equality because Version 2 (V2) of the VAISS builds on the ten guardrails established in the first version (V1). One of these guardrails emphasises engaging stakeholders and evaluating their needs and circumstances with a focus on safety diversity inclusion and fairness. Through the consultation process diverse stakeholders such as civil society organisations AI companies government agencies not-for-profits (NFPs) and academia can provide input on issues related to bias and discrimination to strengthen the guardrail in the next Version of the VAISS.

init1_name1: Pilot AI assurance framework
init1_justif1: In September 2024 the Digital Transformation Agency (DTA) launched a pilot of a draft AI impact assessment tool known as the Pilot Australian Government AI assurance framework. The tool helps agencies assess potential risks and impacts of AI systems including risks of bias and discrimination. One of the key principles applied in the assessment is fairness which requires AI systems to be inclusive and accessible and not result in unfair discrimination against individuals or groups. This includes impacts on individuals with diverse genders and other protected attributes (https://web.archive.org/web/20260124113511/https://www.digital.gov.au/policy/ai/pilot-ai-assurance-framework/step-4-fairness).
init1_name2: Voluntary AI Safety Standard v2 Consultations Expression of Interest
init1_justif2: These consultations address gender equality because Version 2 (V2) of the VAISS builds on the ten guardrails established in the first version (V1). One of these guardrails emphasises engaging stakeholders and evaluating their needs and circumstances with a focus on safety diversity inclusion and fairness which includes gender considerations and gender-related discrimination due to biased datasets. Through the consultation process diverse stakeholders such as civil society organisations AI companies government agencies not-for-profits (NFPs) and academia can provide input on issues related to bias including gender-related discrimination.

init1_name1: Voluntary AI Safety Standard v2 Consultations Expression of Interest
init1_justif1: The VAISS Version 2 (V2) consultation process supports human oversight and determination by inviting stakeholders to help refine guardrails ensuring human accountability governance and intervention mechanisms in AI systems. The consultation seeks feedback on strengthening requirements for organisations to oversee AI deployment manage risks and maintain human responsibility across the AI lifecycle. These discussions build on the existing VAISS guardrails particularly those requiring human control monitoring and accountability for AI outcomes ensuring AI systems remain tools that support human decision making rather than operating autonomously without oversight.

init2_name1: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) Independent Oversight of AI-related Privacy Compliance
init2_justif1: The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner OAIC as Australia’s independent privacy regulator oversees organisations’ use of commercially available AI systems and ensures compliance with privacy obligations. They published additional guidance for the public on how to assess AI's impact on privacy providing tools and steps to conduct Privacy impact assessments on their website.

init1_name1: Consultation on Mandatory Guardrails for High-Risk AI Systems
init1_type1: Launching consultations or stakeholder workshops to gather input for a future draft.
init1_justif1: The Australian Government launched a public consultation on mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI systems through the Safe and Responsible AI in Australia – Proposals Paper (2024). The consultation sought stakeholder input to inform future AI regulation. The document identifies AI in employment and workplace settings as a potential high-risk use case noting that AI used in recruitment hiring promotion pay transfers and termination decisions may significantly affect individuals’ opportunities and may produce discriminatory impacts. See discussion of employment and workplace settings (pp. 26-27) within the section on high-risk AI contexts.
init1_name2: Our Gen AI Transition
init1_justif2: The Jobs and Skills (under the Australian Government) Generative AI Capacity Study (2025) examines the potential impacts of generative AI on the Australian labour market including workforce transformation job augmentation and emerging skill demands. The report assesses how AI may change work tasks across industries and occupations and identifies implications for workforce planning and labour policy. It provides evidence to inform policy responses addressing workforce transitions and labour protections related to AI-driven changes in employment. See discussion on labour market impacts workforce transitions and occupational exposure to AI (pp. 7–15 23–30).

init1_name1: National Skills Agreement Operational Programs
init1_justif1: The NSA implements several nationwide operational programs that expand reskilling and upskilling capacity in areas affected by technological change including AI. It establishes TAFE Centres of Excellence to build skills for high-technology and emerging industries (A112–A116) ensures access to digital literacy and foundation skills training (A108) and strengthens VET workforce capability (A124–A129). These programs support Australia’s transition by preparing workers with the digital and technology competencies required in an AI-enabled economy.
init1_name2: National Skills Agreement Monitoring & Evaluation System
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The NSA establishes a national M&E system to track skills outcomes essential for adapting to technological change including AI. It mandates ongoing monitoring of VET and labour-market performance (A22–A26) annual public reporting (A151–A154) and an independent review of the stewardship model (A37–A39). These mechanisms help governments assess whether training systems—including digital and technology capability initiatives—are effectively supporting reskilling and upskilling needs in an AI-driven economy.
init1_name3: National Skills Agreement National Training and Skills Development Initiatives
init1_justif3: The NSA delivers large-scale training initiatives that build Australia’s workforce capability in areas affected by digital transformation including AI. It guarantees access to foundation skills training including digital literacy (A108) and expands high-level training through TAFE Centres of Excellence aligned with technological and emerging industry needs (A112–A116). The National TAFE Network also develops educator capability and innovative training practices (A121). These initiatives strengthen national reskilling and upskilling capacity.

init1_name1: Voluntary AI Safety Standard v2 Consultations Expression of Interest
init1_justif1: The VAISS Version 2 (V2) consultation process addresses AI safety and security by seeking stakeholder input to refine and strengthen the existing guardrails that manage AI risks across the system lifecycle. The consultation aims to improve practices for identifying and mitigating AI-related harms including stronger guidance for developers and deployers on secure design risk management and monitoring of AI systems. It also explores enhancements such as improved content provenance labelling or watermarking and clearer technical practices to reduce misuse and manipulation of AI systems. These updates aim to strengthen safeguards and reliability in AI deployment.

init1_justif1: The Wallonia-Brussels Federation’s “Artificial Intelligence & Teaching: Principles of Application” (August 2024) establishes a system-wide principle for the responsible use of AI across compulsory adult and higher education. imp1_body_name: Belgian Institute for Postal Services and Telecommunications (BIPT) as the Digital Services Coordinator (DSC)
init1_name1: Designation of the Belgian Institute for Postal Services and Telecommunications (BIPT) as the Digital Services Coordinator (DSC)
init1_justif1: The BIPT's designation as the Independent Oversight Body (DSC) via the Act of April 21 2024 is the crucial implementation step for enforcing the DSA in Belgium. The DSA requires Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) to implement effective measures to mitigate systemic risks to public security and democratic processes (Art. 34) which covers algorithmic amplification of misinformation and violence. The DSC's role is to monitor investigate and sanction platforms for non-compliance with these risk mitigation duties thereby directly providing the necessary enforcement mechanism to counter AI-facilitated harms at the national level
init1_name2: BIPTs Operationalization of the Trusted Flaggers and Out-of-Court Dispute Settlement (ODS) Certification Mechanism
init1_justif2: The BIPT's operationalization of the certification mechanisms (starting in April/May 2024) is a core Multistakeholder Consultation and Capacity Building effort (Source 1.2). The DSA requires the DSC (BIPT) to certify 'Trusted Flaggers' (Art. 22) and Out-of-court Dispute Settlement (ODS) bodies (Art. 21) (Source 1.1). Trusted Flaggers are CSOs/NGOs with expertise in illegal content (like hate speech incitement to violence) whose reports must be prioritized by platforms (Source 1.7). This action directly integrates non-state expertise into the framework enabling faster more accurate identification and removal of AI-facilitated misinformation and violence and providing victims with a non-judicial redress mechanism (ODS)

init1_name1: VITOs use case - Living Environment
init1_justif1: This is continuous Knowledge Generation through publicly-funded research. Projects within the FAIR consortium (like those at VITO) are actively working on developing 'AI Surrogate Models' which are machine learning models trained on the outputs of complex simulations (Source 1.3). These surrogates enable accurate predictions for environmental models (e.g. air quality) while significantly reducing computational cost. By accelerating scenario analysis and operational deployment while simultaneously reducing energy consumption and compute power needed this research directly generates the technical knowledge necessary to achieve the framework's goal of developing frugal and energy-efficient AI.

init1_name1: Hybrid Training Course on AI (AI) and Anti-Discrimination
init1_justif1: This initiative was a government-led training course (co-organized by the Belgian Ministry of Justice FPS BOSA/AI4Belgium and Unia)  Its purpose was to build the capacity of relevant actors including policymakers regulators and civil society to contribute to the fight against algorithmic discrimination . The four-module online training directly focused on: combating algorithm discrimination and providing 'illustrations in several areas'  By equipping public servants and regulators with the knowledge to identify and respond to discrimination risks in AI systems this training directly implements the national framework's commitment
init1_name2: As-is and gap analysis Report on Upholding Equality and Non-discrimination in the Use of AI by Public Administrations (as part of the European Commission Technical Support Instrument Project)
init1_justif2: The 'As-is and gap analysis' report published in March 2025 provides critical information on the challenges and gaps in Belgium's current legal and institutional framework regarding AI Fairness and Non-discrimination in public administration  The report's findings directly inform and prepare government entities and Unia itself on how to better detect prevent and respond to algorithmic discrimination through strengthened legal advice policy recommendations and procedural guidance for the impending EU AI Act implementation

init1_name1: Guidelines of the Belgian Data Protection Authority on AI Systems and the GDPR
init1_justif1: The brochure describes the complex interaction between the General Data Protection Regulation and the Artificial Intelligence Act in the context of AI systems development. It uses 'use cases' to explain the applicability of the two frameworks. Data Protection Impact Assessments are required in some cases: they are mandatory in the processing of high-risk data or in the implementation of new technologies (p. 14); the AI ??Act requires a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (p. 14-15). For high-risk systems a more thorough risk assessment is required in some cases. This analysis examines the specific risks and identifies the potential harms associated with the AI ??system. This obligation complements the GDPR.

init1_name1: Dzongkha Natural Language Processing (NLP) Project
init1_justif1: The Dzongkha NLP project is an ongoing programmatic initiative led by the Department of Culture and Dzongkha Development (DCDD) and the GovTech Agency. It utilizes AI to develop Natural Language Processing tools for Bhutan's national language. The project includes AI-driven machine translation Automatic Speech Recognition and Text-to-Speech systems for Dzongkha. As mandated in the GovTech Digital Strategy 2024 (e.g. under digital service accessibility) this initiative ensures public systems and digital services are available in the local language directly leveraging AI to preserve Bhutanese linguistic heritage prevent digital language exclusion and promote cultural diversity in the technology ecosystem.

init1_name1: Women Digital Champions Training
init1_justif1: The program directly addresses gaps identified in Bhutan's 2024 AI Readiness Assessment regarding limited women's participation in AI planning and decision-making. Training content includes: AI benefits and risks from gender perspectives; technology-facilitated gender-based violence; barriers to gender-inclusive digital policies; strategies for equitable digital access; AI applications to combat online gender-based violence; and women's leadership in digital innovation. The initiative builds capacity among 30 government officials to integrate gender considerations into AI policy development and implementation ensuring women's meaningful participation in Bhutan's digital transformation and AI governance.

init1_name1: Education Working Group identifies best practices for the use of digital resources
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Education participated in the G20 Education Working Group and discussed the importance of involving educators in the development of educational content platforms; the need for digital solutions to address equity and inclusion; and a call for artificial intelligence tools to be based on content that has undergone fact-checking processes. These were the three main conclusions of the meeting. The working group established guidelines reached after two days of deliberations among 31 delegations. The MECRED platform (Brazil) was cited as an example because it provides digital educational resources created by teachers from across the country preventing misinformation from circulating on social media.
init1_name2: Ministry of Education and Federal University of Rio Grande do Norte launch AI course for educators
init1_justif2: Free course provided by the Institute of the Federal University of Rio Grande do Norte in partnership with the Ministry of Education through the Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel aims at Artificial Intelligence for Basic Education Educators: Digital Skills and Pedagogical Innovation. The training is aimed at teachers and undergraduate students from across the country aiming to promote education for the responsible ethical and creative use of Artificial Intelligence in the classroom. The course will cover five topics: introduction to artificial intelligence; identification and recognition of educational problems; fluency in educational data; educational problem-solving; and AI tools in education.
init1_name3: Ministry of Education holds a seminar on AI in basic education
init1_justif3: The Brazilian Ministry of Education event aims to foster dialogue between various leading institutions and academics on pedagogical practices and school management processes related to the use of AI; opportunities and challenges for the use of digital educational resources; and most importantly the paths for the ethical critical and pedagogical integration of AI in Brazilian basic education.

init1_name1: Public hearing on social media regulation and regular meeting
init1_justif1: The Social Communication Council of the National Congress held a debate on social media regulation addressing challenges related to disinformation hate speech and privacy. The goal is to gather contributions from society to balance freedom of expression and digital security. The debate supports Bill 2630/20 approved by the Senate and currently under discussion in the Chamber of Deputies which bans fake accounts and the use of bots as well as combating the spread of fake news and attacks on democracy. Due to ongoing discussions on the bill the Social Communication Council of the National Congress is still participating in the discussions.
init1_name2: Ministry of Human Rights and Citizenship launches study on AI democracy and tackling LGBTQIA+ political violence
init1_justif2: The study presents diagnoses analyses and recommendations for addressing LGBTQIAphobic political violence in digital environments and promoting responsible and inclusive use of artificial intelligence reaffirming the federal government's commitment to promoting human rights and combating this type of violence. The publication brings together a set of recommendations aimed at the government technology companies and civil society. The study is available at https://web.archive.org/save/https://www.votelgbt.org/inteligencia
init1_name3: Hackathon Digital Rights AI Platform Regulation and Information Integrity
init1_justif3: The Hackathon is organized by the Coordination of Treatment Analysis and Dissemination of Scientific Information (and the Coordination of Information Technologies of the Brazilian Institute of Information in Science and Technology. It aims to propose creative and innovative solutions based on a collaborative interdisciplinary and open perspective on the following topics: Digital government and the digital transformation of the State; Ensuring privacy on digital platforms; Regulation of digital platforms; and Information integrity and the use of AI. Students will be able to develop proposals (online and in person) with experts to combat misinformation via platforms especially in the use of AI.

init1_name1: The Federal Government structures ANPD to assume the responsibilities of ECA Digital
init1_justif1: Designation of the National Data Protection Agency as the autonomous administrative authority for the protection of children and adolescents in digital environments and establishing jurisdiction to receive court blocking orders. The court orders refer to the use of profiling techniques (based on AI) virtual augmented or extended reality for the exploitation of children and adolescents and monetization. The orders also include the possibility of disabling systems that use AI from providers of products and services in digital environments whose systems have been evaluated and found to pose a security risk or are not suitable for children and adolescents.
init1_name2: Absolute Priority Dialogues for Safe Children and Adolescents in the Digital Environment
init1_justif2: A seminar hosted by the Federal Supreme Court on the protection of children and adolescents in the digital environment was held on the same day as the enactment of the Law on the Statute of Children and Adolescents in the Digital Environment which establishes protective clauses for AI-based systems and AI-based profiling for monetization. During the event the court launched the Desinfoteca—an online collection of research on digital phenomena such as disinformation and their impact on fundamental rights. The format sought to stimulate reflection on existing best practices regulatory gaps and paths for future normative innovations fostering a space for listening and inter-institutional dialogue.

init1_name1: The Ministry of Development Industry Commerce and Services has opened a consultation to define the list of data center equipment eligible for Redata
init1_justif1: This is a binding and mandatory regulatory act but it must be approved later to become a definitive law. It creates the Special Taxation Regime for Datacenter Services in Brazil Redata (art.1 pg.1 chapter I p.3-7). The act is part of the National Datacenter Policy (p.8). The following conditions are established (art. 11.B) for organizations that establish (or expand) data centers in the country: make available to the domestic market a percentage of processing capacitystorage and data handling prohibiting its allocation forexport or own use in the absence of domestic demand; meet sustainability criteria and indicators: water energy etc.

init1_justif1: The National Secretary of Digital Rights representing the Ministry of Public Security and Justice participated in the Inclusive Feminist Foreign Policy Observatory such as multistakeholder consultations to explain that artificial intelligence presents technological patterns that involve political choices that can reproduce historical inequalities. The secretary highlighted the importance of a gender-sensitive approach to AI which requires conscious decisions to avoid bias and discriminatory content ensuring that technology promotes inclusion and non-violence.

init1_name1: Generative AI in Public Service - Definitions Uses and Best Practices
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The federal government has released a guide with recommendations and best practices for Generative Artificial Intelligence in the Public Service. The guide includes an approach to high-risk AI use and the need for human oversight (p.6). Fundamental Principles for the Use of Generative AI (p.11) were defined Risk with identification of specific vulnerabilities and implementation of mitigation measures (p.12-16) which include explainability and human supervision for high risks (p.18) risk classification (p.12-22) recommendations and practices (22-30).

init1_name1: The Brazilian Institute of Information in Science and Technology participates in a public hearing on the impacts of AI on the job market
init1_justif1: The main objective of the hearing was to analyze the transformations that the adoption of AI has brought to professional routines including the automation of tasks the reconfiguration of professions and the emergence of new roles. The event brought together representatives from public institutions unions employers' associations and academic experts. The institute believes it is necessary to rethink investment in Science and Technology adopting a stance that promotes the use of the National Fund for Scientific and Technological Development (FNDCT) to support research units and the S&T system.
init1_name2: Ministry discusses research on the effects of AI on the world of work
init1_justif2: The Minister of Labor and Employment discusses research projects analyzing the impacts of artificial intelligence on the world of work developed in several countries including Brazil with a representative of the International Observatory on Artificial Intelligence and members of the Working Group on Artificial Intelligence. The ministry's initiative aims to gather contributions to enrich the working group's discussion agenda. Research indicates that 'AI shop floor' workers face regulatory and quantitative invisibility as they are not adequately included in labor and care policies despite their high qualifications often including master's and doctoral degrees.

It utilizes the risk classification system from the AI ??regulation bill. Publicity is a fundamental principle (p. 11). The proposal is that it be continuously updated to reflect changes in regulations and technological advancements.

init1_justif1: The National Institute of Metrology Quality and Technology (Inmetro) held the event with the goal of presenting the potential of artificial intelligence (AI) in a practical and inspiring way to all the institution's employees. Participants attended presentations on AI trends in the public sector and practical applications of the technology in Inmetro's internal routines and those of other institutions. The event reinforces Inmetro's commitment to promoting the exchange of experiences and encouraging the conscious use of artificial intelligence in the public sector.

init1_justif1: The lecture 'Artificial Intelligence in Brazil: Regulation and the Labor Market' is part of the program for the 21st Meeting of the Labor Observatory Network (formed by local centers that produce studies and analyses on the labor market). The lecture addressed the regulatory challenges of artificial intelligence in the country as well as its impacts on the labor market and public employment policies.
init1_name2: Escola do Trabalhador 40 launches new free courses in AI
init1_justif2: Escola do Trabalhador 4.0 has launched new free online courses in Artificial Intelligence such as the AI ??learning path for NGOs and the Fluency for Students Fluency for Educators and Employability with Microsoft Copilot courses. The platform now offers 97 courses across 23 learning paths all aligned with current job market demands and trends.
init1_name3: Ministry of Labor and Employment establishes Working Group on AI
init1_justif3: The Working Group on Artificial Intelligence is responsible for conducting studies and research on the impact of AI on both the labor market and public services with a focus on identifying trends and future prospects. The group will also focus on identifying opportunities for the development of AI-based products and services that promote social inclusion and create new jobs.

init1_justif1: The Regulatory Sandbox of the National Data Protection Authority constitutes a controlled testing environment that allows public and private entities to develop and test technological innovations based on artificial intelligence systems that involve the processing of personal data under structured regulatory supervision. The program is currently in its second edition.

In his lecture on 'AI Regulation in Brazil from a Governance Perspective' he addressed the principles and objectives of AI regulation which include the security transparency and explainability of systems as well as the protection of fundamental rights and algorithmic non-discrimination.

init1_name1: Program for supporting Bulgarian communities organizations and media abroad
init1_justif1: The program focuses on supporting projects by Bulgarian community organizations and media outlets in neighboring countries (Western Balkans) and Eastern Europe with traditional Bulgarian communities (Ukraine and Moldova) aiming to build capacity to combat disinformation and fake news improve media literacy and build public resilience against external interference and information manipulation. The program provides funding for activities related to: Fact-checking; Combating disinformation and fake news; Initiatives aimed at increasing media literacy and public resilience; Capacity building to identify and combat fake news disinformation external interference and information manipulation.
init1_name2: International Conference Education 40 - AI Cybersecurity and Digital Future with Erasmus
init1_justif2: The event was organized by the Center for Human Resources Development. The Center is a legal entity under Article 60 of the Administration Law—a secondary budgetary expenditure authority under the Minister of Education and Science. During the event the topic of security was addressed by the Deputy Minister of the Interior who emphasized that Bulgaria is actively working to establish itself as a digital security creator in partnership with the EU and NATO. Commissioner Svetlin Lazarov (GDBOP) had a competent participation in cybersecurity issues. Together with Krasimir Kotsev (CoCyber) he conducted a demonstration on fake news and relevant digital protection strategies.

init1_name1: Guidelines for using AI in the education system issued by the Ministry of Education
init1_justif1: The initiative supports cultural and linguistic diversity by committing the system to monitor and evaluate AI tools to ensure their effectiveness and adequacy for diverse learning needs abilities learning styles language differences and cultural characteristics. This ensures that both students and teachers benefit from AI systems that adapt to varied linguistic and cultural contexts rather than reinforcing a single dominant model. By requiring this level of oversight the initiative helps guarantee that AI-supported resources remain equitable and accessible across different groups. In doing so it positions AI as a tool that actively reduces disparities in learning experiences for all students and teachers (pp.10-11 Principle 1).

One of the modules of this event was 'New technologies to help people with disabilities' with priority theme theme 'Social Inclusion and Equal Opportunities' and different speakers including those from the Ministry discussed how AI-technologies can help people with disabilities be better integrated into the society and on equal foot by using more ways to communicate. See page 6 of teh translated document.
init1_name2: Opinion of the Bulgarian Personal Data Protection Commission on the use of a video surveillance system with facial recognition functionality in retail outlets (stores)
init1_justif2: In this opinion the use of facial recognition video surveillance system in retail outlets for prevention of theft leading to individual’s biometric categorization is analyzed. In paragraph 4 Section 'What exactly is facial recognition?' the Commission accepts that technical inaccuracies of such AI systems may lead to biased results and discrimination. It is stated that the risk of such biased results and discrimination is of particular importance in relation to age ethnicity race gender or disability. In the last paragraph of the legal analysis section it opines the algorithms can be used in relation to different demographic groups thus the presence of bias or deviations in facial recognition can deepen prejudice in society.

init1_name1: Measure Development of Standards in the Use of AI (BG0082) as part of the Action Plan Bulgaria Action Plan 2022-2024 (December)
init1_justif1: The measure Development of Standards in the Use of Artificial Intelligence (BG0082) was Part of Bulgaria’s Open Government Partnership Action Plan (12.2022-12.2024). It addresses human rights impact assessments safeguards for AI in public decision-making and regulatory frameworks for ethical AI use in government (see Description). E-Government Ministry led and focus areas: Automated decision-making AI ethics human rights safeguards and digital governance (see section Overview). Among others the measure consists of development of common standards in the AI use in the digitization process in order to ensure guarantees of equal access and respect for human rights (e.g. AI-based systems to beunbiased and non-discriminatory p.15 of Plan)
init1_name2: AI Readiness Index in Public Administration in the Republic of Bulgaria issued by the Institute of Public Administration
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: In Section Preparation of legislation internal rules and the ethical application of AI (p.3) it is concluded that Bulgaria must  update its legislation to be harmonized with the European one. In the period 2025–2027 internal mechanisms for risk assessment when using AI systems must be introduced in accordance with the riskcategories defined in the regulation. In S.7.2 'What topics would you suggest for training in the field of working with AI?' the interviewed civil servants specified that they would appreciate as additional training topics impact of AI on the quality of life and society.

init1_name1: Sofia Municipality tests AI assistant for more transparent public procurement
init1_justif1: One of the commitments made by Sofia Mayor Vasil Terziev is to make the public procurement process in the capital as transparent and impartial as possible. In fulfillment of this commitment the Sofia Municipality is already testing an experimental artificial intelligence assistant that assists in the verification of public procurement.
init1_name2: TABLE for reflecting objections and suggestions on the draft Guidelines for application under the procedureDigital Transformation of School Education created by the EXECUTIVE AGENCYPROGRAMME EDUCATION
init1_justif2: The table reflects the objections and suggestions submitted by different ministries on the draft Guidelines for application under the procedure: 'Digital Transformation of School Education' and was created by EXECUTIVE AGENCY “PROGRAMME EDUCATION”. The Ministry of Education stated that the requirements for use of Bulgarian AI models and use of cloud services or infrastructure outside of Bulgaria may be challenged because unclear potentially restrictive and could create legal and financial risks if applied in public procurement procedures. It noted that including such requirements in future public procurements may be challenged as discriminatory or competition-restricting especially against bidders who rely on non-BG models (proposal 4).

init1_type1: Dedicated budget lines or public tenders
init1_justif1: The subject of the order covers the development of a comprehensive platform with adaptive-analytical software with the ability for self-learning data processing and analysis (Artificial Intelligence) of the social and solidarity economy together with the delivery of the hardware equipment necessary for its implementation. It was commissioned by the Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs. The focus is on a AI-driven platform to assist for the social and solidarity economy. The bid explicitly mentiones the delivery of thehardware equipment necessary for the implementation of the AI platform.
init1_name2: AI in Law Training for Appellate District Judges
init1_justif2: 49 magistrates from the Veliko Tarnovo Appellate District underwent a training organized by the District Court - Ruse jointly with the National Institute of Justice. The focus of the event was 'Application of Artificial Intelligence in Law. Electronic Evidence. Evidence in Social Networks and Cryptocurrencies'. The training provided an opportunity for discussion and in-depth study in an area that is becoming increasingly important for the administration of justice in the digital age. Key aspects were examined such as: the application of artificial intelligence in the legal sphere and its potential benefits and challenges; the specifics of electronic evidence and its admissibility in the judicial process; how evidence from social networks.
init1_name3: AI Readiness Index in Public Administration in the Republic of Bulgaria
init1_justif3: This initiative supports public sector skills development by identifying knowledge gaps in AI understanding mapping priority application areas such as process automation citizen services and data analysis and structuring training needs across ethics technical foundations data work practical case studies and communication ethics legal and social issuese.g. legal aspects of applying AI in the public sector. By combining formal training proposals with recognition of informal learning methods like self-study peer exchange and experimentation it creates a comprehensive roadmap for building AI literacy and competence among civil servantsenabling them to responsibly adopt AI improve efficiency and maintain public trust (section 7)

init1_name1: Annex II to the National Strategic Document Digital Transformation of Bulgaria for the Period 2024 2030 Digital Decade Programme until 2030 Roadmap of Bulgaria
init1_justif1: Measure 32 addresses Safety and Security in the context of AI by explicitly requiring the implementation of cybersecurity and data privacy processes in SMEs alongside the adoption of Industry 4.0 technologies such as IIoT autonomous robots cloud computing and big data. Measure 37 addresses Safety and Security in the context of AI by focusing its research and innovation on digital security and adaptive software systems ensuring that AI technologies are developed with robust safeguards. Measure 41 addresses Safety and Security in the context of AI by prioritizing the development of intelligent security systems for data exchange and communications and by investing in high levels of cybersecurity as part of its digital innovation agenda.
init1_name2: Seminar of the Network of Experts in the Field of Data and AI Technologies in the Field of ESIF and EFSI
init1_justif2: In the period August 31 - September 5 the first of a series of events dedicated to the development of the Network of Experts in the Field of Data and AI Technologies in the Field of ESIF/EFSI was held in Albena Resort. The topics discussed during the seminar were among others cybersecurity applying artificial intelligence and data processing in the daily practice of government experts. Reps from the Institute of Public Administration (IPA) took active participation in the seminar. The IPA was established under the Civil Servant Act in 2000. It is a structure under the Council of Ministers of the Republic of Bulgaria and is the leading institution for training civil servants.
init1_name3: INSAIT and the Ministry of Defense will cooperate to implement and use AI for the countrys security
init1_justif3: The Institute for Computer Science Artificial Intelligence and Technology (INSAIT) at Sofia University and the Ministry of Defense will cooperate in the field of innovation computer science AI scientific research cyber security and development. This is done under a Memorandum of Cooperation. INSAIT is the first of its kind in Eastern Europe to offer world-class research facilities and conditions. INSAIT’s sole focus is on scientific excellence: conducting world-class research in AI attracting outstanding international scientists and training the next-generation of graduate students and technology leaders. For this indicator it is especially important that the cooperation will be in the field of AI cyber security and development.

init1_name1: Demystifying the process of creating digital assistants the experience of the first state institution with AI in Bulgaria
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Institute for Public Administration's (IPA) digital assistant (CHIPA chatbot) is based on GPT-4o-mini technology (p.2). All its responses come from a pre-selected verified knowledge base rather than uncontrolled internet sources (p.2). To tackle the hallucinations the chatbot is constantly monitored and adjusted and extremely strict control is exercised over the correct answers; (p.3) and verified and tested by external experts and IPA colleagues (p.4). The model is designed as a 'living' organism by continuously learning based on interactions with users. Quality is ensured by a controlled training approach where data is integrated only when verified (p.4). IPA is a structure under the Council of Ministers.
init1_name2: In-person Training on the topic AI as a Strategic Tool in the Judicial System
init1_justif2: The National Institute of Justice (NIJ) was established by the Judicial System Act in 2003. The NIJ carries out its activities in accordance with the Judicial System Act and the principles of judicial training adopted by the EU Judicial Training Network. The NIJ held a training ' Artificial Intelligence as a Strategic Tool in the Judicial System ' in which 41 judges prosecutors and investigators participated. The lecturers provided a detailed overview of the technical aspects of AI in the courtroom. The introductory lecture was dedicated to the philosophy terminology and basic concepts of AI such as BlackBox machine learning and “deep learning”. final part presented the practical aspects of AI in criminal proceedings.
init1_name3: Opinion of the Bulgarian Personal Data Protection Commission on the use of a video surveillance system with facial recognition functionality in retail outlets (stores)
init1_justif3: The document enhances transparency by explicitly explaining how the facial-recognition system works including data flows human involvement and storage arrangements (p.2–4). It improves explainability by clearly identifying the categories of data processed why they qualify as biometric data and the legal bases the company claims to rely on (p.5–7). It further increases transparency by detailing risks proportionality concerns and high-risk AI implications helping the public understand both the system’s impact and the basis for regulatory conclusions (p.12–15).  The CPDP provides a step-by-step legal analysis showing why these justifications do not meet GDPR and AI Act requirements in a clear way even for non-experts (8-12).

init1_name1: Creating a Safe Digital Environment for Cambodian Children (Workshop)
init1_drive1: The workshop does not provide accessible outcome report and we do not possess any relevant documents regarding the event. Therefore we cannot upload documents to Google drive from our end.
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC)-led technical workshop addresses Children's Rights in the context of AI by prioritizing the mitigation of emerging AI-related threats. It focused on implementing the Child Online Guidelines and specifically discussed the challenges posed by generative artificial intelligence. The primary goal was to strengthen the MPTC’s regulatory role and enforce platform accountability to proactively ensure digital systems including those that use AI are designed and deployed in a manner that protects children's rights.

init1_name1: Cambodias AI Readiness Assessment Report
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Assessment a government-led initiative supported by UNESCO embeds cultural and linguistic diversity as a core part of its vision for ethical AI.  “Social & Cultural Dimension” (starting on p.24) assesses how AI adoption can reflect Cambodia’s social-cultural context not just technical readiness. It highlights specific inclusive tools such as the Khmer Braille project (p.13 35) which enables visually impaired students to access reading materials as an “AI for Good” initiative (p.35) showing attention to accessibility and disability. It also notes the development of Khmer-language datasets (p.10) and large language models to support Khmer and local dialects helping preserve linguistic heritage while enabling AI in Khmer.

init1_name1: Cambodiass AI Readiness Assessment Report
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: It addresses gender equality chiefly under its “Responsible AI Inclusion and Well-being” pillar (p.49) noting persistent gender gaps in digital and STEM access in Cambodia (p.26). It observes that the gender gap in internet usage is narrowing (p.24) and highlights efforts to promote digital skills and AI opportunities for women and girls. Accordingly one of the key recommendations is to “3.1. Advance Gender Equality in the AI Ecosystem” (p.51) suggesting targeted inclusion capacity building and representation of women in AI-related education employment and governance.

init1_justif2: This initiative led by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC) and ITU promotes gender equality by empowering girls and young women with digital and STEM skills essential for AI careers. Session 2 of the agenda engaged participants to experience digital learning tools and applications aiming to build awareness and practical skills by introducing students and teachers to accessible digital technologies (coding AI cybersecurity).

init1_name1: Helping Canadians identify misinformation online
init1_justif1: This government-led initiative directly addresses AI-facilitated misinformation through funding and implementation of technological countermeasures. The Government of Canada via the Digital Citizen Contribution Program supports the development of an AI-powered tool by Université de Montréal to detect and mitigate misinformation across multiple formats and languages. By integrating large language models into user-facing tools such as browser extensions the initiative enhances individuals’ ability to identify false content and reduces its spread across languages modalities (text audio video images) and sources.

init1_name1: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) AI Complaint Investigations
init1_justif1: The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) is an active state-based non-judicial mechanism handling AI-related grievances. During the study period the OPC launched AI-specific investigations including OpenAI/ChatGPT (April 2023 ongoing) TikTok (June 2024) and X Corp. (February 2025). In 2024–2025 the OPC received 1458 PIPEDA complaints with rising AI-related cases. The OPC investigates complaints issues findings and recommends remedies providing Canadians with a formal non-judicial channel to seek redress for AI-related privacy harms.

init1_name1: Grants and Contributions - The Machine-Readable Child Governance of Emotional AI used with Canadian Children
init1_justif1: The Grant by the Canadian government valued at $81464.10 is meant for a research project titled 'The Machine-Readable Child: Governance of Emotional AI used with Canadian Children' wich will focus on evaluating PIPEDA and proposed Canadian privacy and AI legislation for their fitness to govern the use of Emotional AI used with children highlighting gaps and offering suggestions where appropriate.
init1_name2: Statement on AI (AI) and Children
init1_justif2: This statement on AI and children is the result of a convening between Canada's Office of the Privacy Commissioner and a roundtable of G7 data protection and privacy authorities. It identifies 3 main risks of AI in the context of children's privacy and data protection: (AI-based decision-making; manipulation and deception; and training of AI models based on data-scraping) (p. 2). It also sets out several things that regulators in Canada and G7 countries may wish to regulate given these issues (p. 3).

init1_name1: The Government of Canada calls for stronger international cooperation on digital governance at UNESCO
init1_justif1: The activity led by the Government of Canada (through Canadian Heritage) as reflected in its 2025 engagement at UNESCO focuses on strengthening international cooperation on digital governance in response to the rise of digital platforms and artificial intelligence. It addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by emphasizing the protection and promotion of diverse cultural expressions in the digital environment and highlighting the importance of preserving cultural heritage and identity. It also promotes inclusive AI ecosystems.

init1_name1: AI integration roadmap for numerical weather and environmental predictions
init1_justif1: The roadmap of the Canadian Government outlines an overview of how AI can be integrated into ECCC's Research-Development-Operation (RDO) production chain for weather and environmental predictions for the future of Canada. Although the majority of it focuses on how AI can be used for the environment itself (thus outside of scope) there are also several references to reducing the impact and resource consumption of AI itself for example noting that 'current and future DA algorithms will struggle with significantly larger ensemble sizes. It is therefore essential to invest in code optimization. ' which directly affects the efficiency of the algorithm itself by paying attention to optimized codes (DA Section).

init1_name1: 4th review of the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The periodic bi-annual reviews of the Directive help ensure that the Directive is robust and meeting its goals. One of the primary goals of the Directive is to ensure fair and reasonable outcomes of automated decision-making systems. The 4th review provides for changes to the language of the Directive that ensure that a discrimination on the basis of various parts of identity is explicitly assessed (see page 11). Adding these bases of discrimination explicitly into the assessment of automated systems helps strengthen the fairness of a system's outputs.

init1_name1: Third Review of the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The third review of the Directive includes the following requirements/changes: 'Expand the scope to cover internal services'; 'Introduce measures supporting the tracing protection and lawful handling of data used and generated by a system'; 'Expand the bias testing requirement to cover models'; 'Mandate the completion of Gender Based Analysis Plus during the development or modification of a system'; 'Establish explanation criteria in support of the explanation requirement and integrate them into the Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA)'; 'Expand the AIA to include questions concerning an institution's reasons for pursuing automation and potential impacts on persons with disabilities'; among other requirements.

init1_name1: Implications of AI Technologies for the Canadian Labour Force Report of the Standing Committee on Human Resources Skills and Social Development and the Status of Persons with Disabilities
init1_justif1: This report is aimed at Employment and Social Development Canada and examines the impact of AI on jobs in Canada. It includes recommendations to protect workers from AI-driven discrimination in hiring promotion and termination. Specifically on pages 12-15 it addresses the risks of bias and discrimination highlighting the need for updated labour laws and ethical frameworks that ensure protections for all workers including those under algorithmic management without traditional contracts. Page 12 discusses bias risks and the exclusion of vulnerable groups while pages 14-16 recommend reviewing legislation mandating transparency by employers about AI use and ensuring diverse representation in AI oversight to address discrimination risks.

init1_name1: Government of Canada AI Register (Minimum Viable Product) - GC AI Register (MVP)
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Open Government Portal's AIA Register requires federal departments to publicly disclose completed Algorithmic Impact Assessments prior to deploying automated decision systems (Directive on Automated Decision-Making ss.6.1.1 6.5.1–6.5.2). Each published AIA discloses the system's purpose algorithm data sources decision type impact level and mitigation measures. Multiple AIAs were published within the study period providing citizens with accessible information on how the Government of Canada uses AI in administrative decisions affecting their rights and services. This portal was operational from April 2020 and had 22+ AIAs published within the study period.

init1_name1: List of interested AI (AI) suppliers
init1_justif1: This is a list of suppliers who can provide responsible and effective AI services solutions and products to the Government of Canada. It was compiled by Public Services and Procurement Canada as well as the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat. These pre-qualified suppliers fall into three bands which determine the maximum dollar amount that can be rewarded with Band 1 allowing work up to $1 million before taxes Band 2 allowing work up to $2 million before taxes and Band 3 allowing work up to $37.5 million before taxes. The list also shows which suppliers commit to support the Government of Canada's effort in leading the way on ethical AI. NB: The page was updated in Nov. 2025; the government considers the list a current resource.

init1_name1: Learning Week on AI
init1_justif1: The Government of Canada’s Learning Week on Artificial Intelligence directly supports public sector skill development by providing federal public servants with opportunities to deepen their AI understanding and skills. Held between June 9-13 2025 this week offers flexible participation through live expert sessions self-paced learning videos job aids and discussions focused on responsible AI use. It targets all public servants from beginners to advanced users and aligns with the 2025-2027 AI Strategy priority to build talent and support ethical secure AI adoption. The initiative promotes practical learning and continuous skill development tailored to operational needs.
init1_name2: Using Generative AI in the Government of Canada (DDN321-EV)
init1_justif2: The reoccurring course “Using Generative Artificial Intelligence in the Government of Canada (DDN321-EV)” delivered by the Canada School of Public Service (Government of Canada) constitutes a structured training initiative that supports public sector skill development. Delivered as a virtual classroom with instructor-led sessions it provides hands-on learning on the practical application of generative AI tools in government contexts. Participants are trained in core competencies such as prompt design evaluating AI outputs and applying principles of responsible and inclusive AI use.
init1_name3: Generative AI in your daily work
init1_justif3: The resource “Generative AI in your daily work” led by the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (Government of Canada) constitutes a knowledge-generation output that supports public sector skill development. It provides structured evidence-informed guidance on the practical use of generative AI within government workflows translating technical and policy considerations into clear actionable insights for civil servants. The web-publication outlines core functionalities limitations and risks of generative AI alongside appropriate use cases and safeguards.

init1_name1: Lets Promote Education of the Future Together Initiative
init1_justif1: This initiative is actively building AI literacy by training future teachers in AI. The Ministry of Science in partnership with UMCE CENIA and Kodea launched an AI course for Pedagogy students from four state universities to equip them with AI knowledge and pedagogical skills for classroom use beginning in 2025 helping them teach AI concepts responsibly to their future students. A follow-up report highlights that this “Let's Promote Education of the Future Together” initiative is being expanded to more universities and aims to certify hundreds of future teachers in AI competencies emphasising ethical critical and effective use of technology and the multiplier effect as trained teachers bring AI literacy into Chilean schools.

init1_name1: Action Plan National Policy on AI (UPDATE 2024)
init1_justif1: The action plan of Chile's National AI Policy establishes 177 cross-ministerial initiatives. A crucial component within the policy's third axis—Governance and Ethics—is the explicit mandate to study and implement measures against digital disinformation and violence. This includes measures such as ‘Impulse to multidisciplinary investigation about disinformation digital violence and mental health’ (initiative 172) but also makes explicit reference to the commission against disinformation: 'Reports from the National Disinformation Commission to issue recommendations to address the phenomenon in the digital environment' (initiative 173 p. 41).
init1_name2: Advisory Committee against Disinformation
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The Advisory Committee Against Disinformation was established by the Chilean Ministry of Science in June 2023 to advise the government on analysing and countering the global phenomenon of disinformation at the local level. Comprising academic and civil society experts the Committee produced this second report with 72 specific public policy recommendations. The initiative directly tackles AI-facilitated threats in Chapter 5 and advocates for a multi-lateral governance framework. The report treats AI not merely as a tool but as a systemic actor that exacerbates misinformation and violence requiring coordinated regulatory educational and research-based responses (for example warning labels or supplementary information when AI is used).
init1_name3: Hold on check share Government Campaign
init1_justif3: The 'Hold on Check Share' campaign led by the Ministry General Secretariat of Government (SEGEGOB) is a direct implementation of the Advisory Committee against disinformation's public policy recommendations to build societal resilience. It specifically operationalizes the Committee's call for nationwide media and information literacy by equipping citizens with critical verification skills. This foundational behavioral intervention aims to protect the public against sophisticated AI-generated disinformation and synthetic media. By promoting a culture of verifying before sharing online the initiative directly targets the viral mechanics that amplify harmful content thereby mitigating the social impact of AI-facilitated misinformation.

init1_name1: Action Plan National Policy on AI (UPDATE 2024)
init1_justif1: The Chilean AI Action Plan addresses Gender Equality through a dedicated strategy (Section 3.4 pages 31-33). It aims to increase women's participation in AI research development and the industry to at least OECD average levels with campaigns to showcase women leaders in tech and promote communities for women in AI implementing gender quotas fostering girls' early interest in STEM and increasing female enrollment in higher education STEM programs to 40% actions to increase women's employment in AI under decent conditions achieve 40% female participation in high-potential AI startups and close the gender employment gap.
init1_name2: Cupos Ms Mujeres Cientficas (+MC) initiative
init1_justif2: The Cupos Más Mujeres Científicas (+MC) initiative addresses gender equality in AI-related fields by increasing female participation in STEM careers (pp. 11-12) which form the foundation for AI skills and workforce development. It provides additional university quotas exclusively for women in over 500 STEM programs across 44 universities aiming to reduce gender gaps and structural barriers in higher education. By promoting women’s entry into science technology engineering and mathematics the program strengthens gender representation in critical areas of AI development like in academic programs like Computer Science Engineering or Civil Engineering in Data Science contributing to a more inclusive and balanced future AI workforce.

init1_justif1: An initiative funded by the Chilean National Agency for Research and Development is strongly aligned with Chile’s National AI Policy whose Action Plan explicitly references the GobLab UAI ethical algorithms tools. GobLab developed three instruments for responsible AI including the Algorithmic impact assessments. This tool guides development teams in identifying risks—data bias inequity privacy cybersecurity—and defining mitigation measures before deployment. Using an 11-questions survey it strengthens transparency and accountability. More info of the tools: https://web.archive.org/web/20250909063950/https://algoritmospublicos.cl/herramientas

init1_name1: Evaluation of the Implementation of Law 21431 Which Amends The Labor Code Regulating The Contract Of Workers in Digital Platform Service Companies
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: This initiative consisted of evaluating the implementation of Law 21.431. The report combined quantitative data from the National Employment Survey qualitative findings from a FLACSO-Chile study involving participant observation and stakeholder feedback from social dialogues and lobbying meetings. It found persistent high informality (~75%) a near-total dominance of independent contractor status despite legal provisions for both categories and significant failures in achieving mandated algorithmic transparency and non-discrimination. The evaluation concluded that while the law established pioneering rights it had a limited impact on formalising labour relations or ensuring fair algorithmic management within its initial year.
init1_name2: Tripartite Roundtable on Digital Platforms
init1_justif2: This initiative is a government-led forum for tripartite social dialogue between the government platform companies and worker unions. The initiative aims to advance and tackle the difficulties in the implementation of Law 21.431. Specific areas raised for discussion which are central to AI-driven labour protections include algorithmic bias transparency contract types and remuneration of gig work among other issues.

init1_name1: Resolution on Recommendations from the Transparency Council on Algorithmic Transparency
init1_justif1: The initiative addresses Transparency and Explainability by requiring public bodies to disclose clear accessible information on how automated and semi-automated decision systems (ADS) operate their objectives methods or model used data sources and impacts. It mandates proactive publication of this information through “Algorithmic Transparency” items (Section 5 and 6 pp. 5–11). These measures ensure citizens understand when and how AI systems influence decisions thus promoting explainability and accountability
init1_name2: Algorithmic transparency on the Transparency portal
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: This is the Transparency Portal maintained by the Council for Transparency (CPLT) which operationalises the CPLT’s Recommendations on Algorithmic Transparency by providing a dedicated space for public institutions to disclose information on algorithmic and automated decision-making systems. However data downloaded from the portal show very limited adoption: so far only five public institutions have created algorithmic transparency pages and only one—the Social Security Institute (IPS)—has explicitly declared the use of automated decision-making systems (ADMs). IPS disclosed information on three algorithmic systems including a predictive model based on neural networks for pending pension claims.
