init1_name1: Measures on the Management of Personal Information Protection Compliance Audits (with guidelines)
init1_justif1: The system is mandated by the central regulator the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) (p.2 promulgating entity) and enforces administrative compliance. The audit is a proactive/ex-post measure where the government orders companies handling large data volumes (1million ppl for non-sensitive info; or 100k for sensitive) to audit their compliance (article 5 pp.2-3). This applies to platforms using AI that have large user base or train AI using personal data. The CAC can mandate external audits in high-risk scenarios or following significant data breaches. The audit reports outline deficiencies in areas like automated decision-making and consent mechanisms forcing the company to correct the flaws.
init1_name2: Chinas First Case on AI-Generated Voice Infringement (Beijing Internet Court 2024)
init1_justif2: A 2024 decision by the Beijing Internet Court addressed harms from AI-generated voice technology. The court found that defendants used a voice actor’s recordings to train and operate a text-to-speech AI system without authorization infringing her voice rights under the Civil Code of the People's Republic of China (Art. 1023). The court ordered the responsible companies to cease the infringement issue a public apology and pay RMB 250000 in damages. This judicial ruling demonstrates how individuals can seek legal remedy and compensation when AI systems misuse personal data or likeness.

init1_name1: Clear & Bright Summer 2025 Special Rectification of the Online Environment for Minors
init1_justif1: The special action is an effort by the Central Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) to enforce existing regulations like the Provisions on the Protection of Minors on the Internet. The action explicitly targets AI-related risks in its oversight scope focusing on three areas: 1. Content safety and functional regulation of children's smart devices; 2. Inappropriate application of AI functions in the field of minors; 3. Issues related to inducing addiction using AI features. The campaign aims to protect minors' rights by clearing harmful content such as network bullying explicit material self-harm inducement and content using minors' images for malicious profiteering (e.g. 'soft pornography').

init1_name1: China Telecom launched the Xingchen AI model for Chinese Dialects
init1_justif1: China Telecom's (central state-owned enterprise a type of SOE directly supervised by the central government) Xingchen model an audio-based language model designed to understand and generate content in more than 30 Chinese dialects. The initiative directly addresses the Cultural and Linguistic Diversity indicator by developing an AI system that promotes and preserves various linguistic backgrounds. This initiative is a form of government-led initiative and also an example of capacity building and knowledge generation by a state-owned enterprise aiming to improve accessibility and cultural representation within AI technology.
init1_name2: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Multilingual AI Technology
init1_justif2: The lab was founded by the regional government and its specific initiatives were largely guided and funded by the state. Initiatives related to cultural and linguistic diversity in AI include: Developing AI tools for the Mongolian language such as speech recognition and synthesis machine translation and text recognition; Creating the Beijiang Ancient Books Resource Database which is described as a comprehensive platform for the preservation and analysis of ancient Mongolian texts; Leading a National Key R&D Project to develop a bidirectional Mongolian-Chinese speech translation system. As the website only says 'june 2024' without mentioning the exact start day I put the 30th for the start day.

init1_name1: Green Data Center Evaluation National Index System
init1_justif1: The initiative is the formal implementation (June 1 2025) of the national standard Green Data Center Evaluation which provides a comprehensive technical standard for green and low-carbon data center certification. It addresses the sustainability of AI by creating mandatory auditable benchmarks for the energy-intensive infrastructure that supports AI development. The standard includes strict weighted metrics for efficient utilization of energy resources (e.g. PUE (table 1 no.1 p.1) efficiency per unit of computing power (table 1 no.16 p.2)) water utilization (table 1 no.6 p.1) and crucially Green Operation and Maintenance Level which includes requirements for the recycling and reuse of retired server equipment (pp.3-4)
init1_name2: Eastern Data Western Computing Plan
init1_justif2: The 'East Data West Computing' project addresses the environmental impact of AI by strategically relocating data centers to leverage clean energy and natural cooling. The initiative shifts energy-intensive computing tasks from the developed eastern regions which rely heavily on fossil fuels to the western regions which have abundant renewable resources like solar wind and hydropower.

init1_name1: Qinglang campaign Rectification of Prominent Algorithmic Problems on Internet Platforms
init1_justif1: The initiative enforces fairness and non-discrimination by strictly prohibiting 'Big Data Price Discrimination' where algorithms use personal data like consumption levels or device types to set higher prices for different users on the same product. It specifically protects vulnerable groups by requiring that algorithms for delivery and ride-hailing services balance platform profit with the 'legitimate rights and interests' of gig workers preventing AI from setting unrealistic safety-compromising deadlines. To ensure competitive equity the policy bars platforms from using algorithms to engage in monopolistic practices such as 'choosing one from two' (exclusive dealing) or manipulating traffic to unfairly suppress specific merchants.

imp1_body: n/a
init1_name1: The Global Digital Empowerment for Women Development Exchange and Cooperation Center
init1_justif1: The establishment of the Global Exchange and Cooperation Center for Digital Empowerment of Women directly addresses gender equality in the context of AI by working to close the gender digital divide. The center is a strategic initiative by the All-China Women's Federation to 'eliminate gender digital gaps' and is described as an innovative practice for promoting the international governance of digital technology.The initiative aims to boost women's digital capabilities cultivate digital talent and support research on women and digital technology. These actions are a tangible response to the ethical risks identified in AI safety governance frameworks which include the 'aggravating of social bias and widening intelligence divide'.
init1_name2: Statement on AI (AI) for Promoting Accessibility at UNHRC
init1_justif2: China spoke on behalf of a group of countries at the 56th session of the United Nations (UN) Human Rights Council addressing the role of AI in promoting women's rights and ensuring their participation in AI governance. The joint statement initiated by China calls for upholding the principle of gender equality to ensure the 'comprehensive equal and meaningful participation of women in AI governance'. It also advocates for non-discrimination in AI systems and for enhancing ethical standards and norms for AI to advance gender equality. However I cannot find the exact speech on that day.

init1_name1: Generative AI Service Filing
init1_justif1: The Generative AI Service Filing enforces determination by ensuring that any AI with 'public opinion or social mobilization capabilities' undergoes a rigorous state-led review before deployment. According to the Interim Measures (the main framework) the implementation unit (Cyberspace Administration of China) conducted assessments of relevant models and issued filing numbers. The services need to display filing numbers which also allows the government to trace the specific human entity responsible for any AI malfunction or illegal content. More info about the assessment: https://web.archive.org/web/20260127152116/https://ocpl.substack.com/p/expert-insight-chinas-ai-services
init1_name2: Sword-Shining Pujiang 2025
init1_justif2: This initiative is part of the 'Sword-Shining Pujiang' campaign by the Shanghai provincial Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC). It discovered that several platforms bypassed mandatory security assessments leading the AI to generate illegal content like 'human flesh searching' (open-boxing) money laundering and deepfakes. Despite being ordered to shut down three companies illegally restarted their services leading to formal legal prosecution. The Shanghai Cyberspace Administration (CAC) summoned the companies for a regulatory interview in accordance with the law issued a serious critique and demanded comprehensive rectification.

init1_name1: CAC special action of clear and bright governance of typical algorithm issues on online platforms
init1_justif1: This is a time-bound joint campaign by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) and other departments to address persistent problems in platform algorithms. It 1) highlights the need to prevent platforms from reckless profit-seeking at the expense of workers 2) mandates that platforms clearly disclose algorithm rules for time estimation fee calculation and route planning. 3) prohibits algorithms from being used to carry out monopolistic and unfair competitive practices 4) protects the legitimate rights and interests of workers 5) requires improvement of the platform's order allocation remuneration composition and payment working hours rewards and punishments and other related algorithms.

init1_justif1: The official Algorithm Filing system provides a public-facing database where citizens can view the provider service purpose and basic 'algorithm operating mechanism' for registered systems. While the system primarily targets internet service providers some state-owned enterprises and government-affiliated entities appear in the registry offering limited public disclosure of algorithmic systems.

init1_name1: Politburo 20th study session on AI
init1_justif1: The 20th Politburo Study Session on AI (April 2025) is a Knowledge Generation initiative as it serves as the authoritative mechanism for synthesizing expert information and disseminating strategic knowledge across the state apparatus. The session's purpose is to allow experts to inform senior leaders enabling General Secretary Xi Jinping to officially command the bureaucracy's focus. The resulting political directive mandates strengthening AI education across all phases and enhancing public awareness. This strategic knowledge output directly implements the core goal of the 14th Five-Year Plan's talent mandate (P.58) signaling ministries to prioritize and fund the upskilling of leading cadres in AI and data competencies.

init2_name1: Beijing No 1 Intermediate Peoples Court Yunti Lecture Series Specialized Training on the Challenges of Infringement Liability in the Age of AI
init2_justif1: The Beijing No. 1 Intermediate People's Court held this training on August 27 2025 for judges and court personnel. It focused on the complex legal challenges of AI infringement liability based on specific articles of the Civil Code. This directly contributes to Public Sector Skills Development by equipping judicial officers with the specialized legal knowledge and theoretical support needed to accurately apply the law to new technologies and resolve AI infringement disputes within the existing legal framework.

init1_name1: Skilled Workers Training Program and Rating System Reform
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security (MOHRSS) has recognized 72 new professions during the '14th Five-Year Plan' period (2021-2025 https://web.archive.org/web/20250826190521/https://en.ndrc.gov.cn/policies/202203/P020) including generative artificial intelligence system operator and published 328 national profession standards to improve the working environment and motivate people to explore new skills. The official state media claimed to have 44 million people gained authorized skills level qualifications since 2021 and 6000-plus have been titled senior skilled workers and chief technical engineers thanks to the optimized State-level rating system. AI-related qualifications and certificates are included in these numbers.

init1_name1: China AI Development and Safety Network (CnAISDA)
init1_justif1: The 'China AI Development and Safety Network' is the National AI Safety Network in China that is composed of research forces from various sectors including industry academia and research institutions within China. It has been set up to act like China's AI safety institution (AISI) and join international AISI events. It conducts research on frontier technologies in artificial intelligence enhancing the safety reliability controllability and fairness of AI technology.
init1_name2: Internet Information Service Algorithm Registration System
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: All AI models are required to register in this national system before deployment. As a set of registration processes the government also mandates providers of high-impact algorithms (including most AI models) to submit a Security Self-Assessment. This forces companies to address potential security flaws and ensures the traceability of AI models.
init1_name3: Frontier AI Risk Management Framework and Risk Analysis Technical Report
init1_justif3: Shanghai AI Laboratory a national AI lab partners with CSO Concordia to produce the Frontier AI Risk Management Framework. The framework proposes a robust set of protocols designed to empower general-purpose AI developers with comprehensive guidelines for proactively identifying assessing mitigating and governing a set of severe AI risks that pose threats to public safety and national security. Alongside the framework Shanghai AI Laboratory released a technical report that evaluated several frontier models across key areas: cyber offense biological threats large-scale persuasion and harmful manipulation and loss of control risks (executive summary p.2).

init1_name1: Qinglang campaign Rectification of Prominent Algorithmic Problems on Internet Platforms
init1_justif1: This initiative mandates a shift from black box algorithms to verifiable accountability. It asks platforms to publicly explain the principles and intentions behind ranking search and recommendation systems in 'simple clear and understandable' terms; for gig work and e-commerce systems must disclose the specific mechanics of price calculations route planning and estimated times banning vague excuses for pricing or delays; hot-search rankings must be fully transparent with platforms maintaining logs to prove their algorithmic logic is verifiable by regulators.
init1_name2: Algorithm Registry System
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) created a mandatory registration system for recommendation algorithms that came into effect on March 1 2022 alongside the regulation. The registry requires companies to file detailed information about their algorithms including self-assessment reports and data on how the algorithms are trained and deployed. The CAC has released lists of algorithms that have been registered. This registry serves as a foundation for future legislation and provides a means for the government to gain insight into how algorithms function. The public can key in the name/serial number of algorithms in the search box to find information. The blue box (??????) is for AI providers to fill algorithms into the registry.

init2_name1: Measures for Identifying AI-Generated and Synthetic Content
init2_justif1: China has implemented a comprehensive AI content labeling regime that came into effect on September 1 2025. This initiative is a concrete example of implementing transparency. The regulations require all AI-generated content—including text images audio and video—to have both explicit labels (e.g. a text tag like 'AI??' meaning 'AI generated') (article 3. p.1) and invisible metadata tags that are machine-readable (article 5 p.2). The regulations place the onus on internet platforms like WeChat Weibo and Douyin to detect and label AI-generated content. Platforms must maintain compliance logs and can face penalties for non-compliance including content removal account suspension and fines.

init1_name1: Ethical AI Framework Quick Reference Guide
init1_justif1: The quick reference guide is intended to provide readers with quick reference and initial understanding of the Ethical Artificial Intelligence  Framework and procedures that should be embedded. The reference guide outlines the 12 ethical AI principles which includes Fairness. Under the fairness principle on p.5 recommendations and results from AI applications should treat individuals within similar groups in a fair manner without favouritism or discrimination and without causing or resulting in harm. This entails maintaining respect for the individuals behind the data and refraining from using datasets that contain discriminatory biases.

init1_justif1: The quick reference guide is a shortened version of the ethical artificial intelligence framework. This version seeks to provide a quick guide for the public's reference. The guide includes robustness reliability and security in the 12 ethical AI principles and offers guidance on how this principle can be achieved during the AI lifecycle(p.5). Mitigation steps should be incorporated to minimise damages in the case of failure(p.11).

init1_name1: Standards on Algorithmic Transparency used by the State - Office of the Attorney General of Colombia and Office of the Ombudsman of Colombia
init1_justif1: Joint Directive 007 of 2025 establishes that algorithmic systems used by the State that intervene in or support processes related to the provision of services to children and adolescents must comply with additional requirements of transparency explainability and impact assessment (Article 13). These measures include analyzing the algorithmic impact prior to deploying the system taking risk mitigation measures to avoid infringing on fundamental rights establishing procedures for objections to automated decisions and clarifying the justification for affirmative action when designing systems that differentiate or prioritize vulnerable groups such as children and adolescents (Article 17).

init1_name1: Ruling T-323 of 2024 - Constitutional Court of Colombia (2024)
init1_justif1: Ruling T-323 of 2024 establishes guidelines for the use of AI in the judicial branch emphasizing that it should not replace human rationality and the principle of human control. The Court reaffirms that judicial decisions must be made and justified by human judges (para.219) who must verify supervise and assume responsibility for the use of AI (para.372). In addition it orders the Superior Council of the Judiciary to issue guidelines on the use of AI ensuring that human judgment prevails in all judicial proceedings (para.406). This is in line with the principles of oversight and accountability established in the Ministry of Science's Roadmap.

init1_justif1: Ruling T-534 of 2023 addresses labour protections in AI by requiring platforms that use algorithmic or automated decisions (para.38) to respect due process guarantees for workers. init1_drive1: This is a project to introduce artificial intelligence (AI) into the education system in Côte d'Ivoire. It is still ongoing (no official documents are available at this stage).
init1_justif1: The Ministry of National Education and Literacy in partnership with UNESCO and GPE-KIX Africa 21 Hub organised a three-day workshop in Yamoussoukro (CIV) from 15 to 17 September 2025. This event brought together decision-makers experts and education practitioners to develop an AI skills framework tailored to teachers and learners with a focus on integrating AI and digital technology into education systems. The work included the co-construction of AI skills and the translation of digital resources into national languages for inclusive accessibility. It resulted in concrete recommendations for adapting educational curricula. This is an active open-ended initiative operational as of 30 September 2025 contributing to AI literacy.

init1_name1: Designation of authorities under Article 77 of the EU AU Act
init1_justif1: The Government has designated several authorities responsible for the protection of fundamental rights in accordance with Article 77 of the EU AI Act including the Ombudsperson for Gender Equality. The authority 'shall have the power to request and access any documentation created or maintained under this Regulation in accessible language and format when access to that documentation is necessary for effectively fulfilling their mandates within the limits of their jurisdiction' (Article 77(1)). The designation of these authorities represents the establishment of an institutional framework for the protection of human rights as envisioned by the AI Act.

init1_name1: SARA Automated Response and Attention System
init1_justif1: SARA addresses gender equality in AI by using an AI chatbot to lower barriers for women and girls facing gender-based violence. It provides guidance on recognizing abuse shares contacts for shelters legal and psychological support and connects users to the *212 emergency line. It acts as a first discreet point of contact rather than a replacement for authorities. This government-led deployment operationalizes AI for protection and access to rights expanding timely information and referral pathways for survivors
init1_name2: Women in science technology and innovation key to economic empowerment and equality in Latin America and the Caribbean Panel
init1_justif2: Led by the Ministry of Women this initiative advances gender equality in AI by promoting women’s participation retention and leadership in science technology and innovation and by urging policies that prevent AI from reproducing inequalities. It calls for women’s inclusion in AI decision-making removal of barriers like stereotypes and discrimination and wider access to the benefits of emerging technologies. High-level engagement links these goals to concrete public agendas and investments that expand women’s pathways into AI education jobs and leadership.

init1_name1: Special Bill on work in digital platforms for passenger transport
init1_justif1: A special Bill on work in digital ride-hailing platforms is currently under review by a Senate special commission. This proposed legislation aims to establish a specific legal framework for platform drivers guaranteeing their labor and social security rights while promoting fair competition with traditional transport services. By bringing gig workers under enforceable protections the law directly addresses AI-mediated algorithmic management. The bill still under committee review reportedly includes provisions for a minimum wage mandatory enrollment in social insurance and pension contributions for drivers. These represent key labor protections for workers managed by platform algorithms.

The session discusses generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) from another perspective to spot deepfakes explores how forgers are using generative AI to create fake videos and discusses how to leverage AI to spot these fake videos. It also highlights how generative AI and its applications work and covers different techniques to detect content created by generative AI tools.

init1_name1: PROVISIONS RELATING TO THE IMPLEMENTATION OF AI AND TECHNOLOGIES
init1_justif1: The creation of the AI National Agency (ANIA) is established in Art 7 of the Law. This Agency has issued a provision requiring systems registered with the Agency—whether voluntarily or because they operate in mandatory sectors such as health biometrics or security—to operate and incorporate bias testing and equity assessments.  According to Article 4 (Definitions) of the Law bias is defined as any systematic error in the outcomes of an AI system that disproportionately favors or disadvantages certain groups or individuals including when gender is among the relevant characteristics.  Furthermore Art 15 of the Provisions establishes that systems registered with ANIA must conduct algorithmic impact assessments that include bias impact.

init1_name1: Automated decisions and profiling
init1_justif1: States the right to challenge automated decisions. AKI explains you have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing and sets 5 safeguards—crucially the right to human review and to contest an automated decision. Gives step-by-step redress instructions and deadlines. The page tells you what to include in your request and that the controller must respond within one month (extendable up to two). If they refuse they must justify and inform you of the right to complain to AKI or go to court.

To promote a trustworthy and human-centered data economy and digital government the strategy focuses on extensively raising people's awareness and data literacy (p.55) realizing the potential of data-driven technologies and understanding and addressing the threats requires systematic and continuous development of digital competencies among young people and adults as well as raising awareness and relevant competencies among companies and public institutions themselves.
init1_name2: Childrens internet use and opportunities to prevent online sexual abuse of children
init1_justif2: The report's theoretical background section mentions that a new and growing concern is the creation and distribution of sexualized content depicting children using AI. It also notes that the European Commission's Lanzarote Committee for children's rights is calling for a focus on the role of technology in child abuse. The qualitative study with children found that some children are using AI such as Google AI or ChatGPT to find information on online safety seeing it as both entertaining and educational. The initiative explicitly points out a key risk: children may not be able to assess the truthfulness of the information provided by AI. page 6

init1_name1: Study of risks of AI and machine learning technology and their mitigation
init1_justif1: Cybernetica AS commissioned by RIA compiled an analysis of the risks of AI technology and the possibilities for mitigating them. https://web.archive.org/web/20251017094003/https://www.ria.ee/uudised/ria-ja-cybernetica-aitavad-eesti-ettevotetel-tehisintellekti-turvaliselt-rakendada. the RIA 2024 AI risk study treats environmental impact as a core risk. It notes AI’s energy-intensive training/inference increases the “environmental footprint” and recommends defining metrics and pursuing energy-efficient AI R&D.  It also requires builders to assess each AI system’s impact on the natural/living environment (e.g. energy and resource use) in the quick risk-assessment and to modify/limit the system or add if harms are found. p79

init1_name1: Ethiopias Largest-Ever Tech Expo (ETEX 2025)
init1_justif1: ETEX 2025 co-organised by the Ethiopian Artificial Intelligence Institute (EAII) and the Information Network Security Administration (INSA) supports AI safety and security by bringing policymakers and industry together around AI and cybersecurity. The expo highlighted advances in both fields and emphasised data and application security helping build partnerships and awareness that strengthen secure AI adoption.

init1_name1: Pix Certification and Digital Skills Reference Framework
init1_justif1: The initiative addresses AI literacy through the mandatory 'Pix 6th grade awareness certificate' which attests that students have received training on 'artificial intelligence' 'content generated by [AI]' and associated 'risks' and 'abuses'. Furthermore the document mandates a specific 'Pix course dedicated to AI' for students in grades 4e and 2de. The underlying CRCN framework also covers foundational AI skills such as 'processing data' (Domain 1) and 'programming' (Domain 3).

imp1_body_name: Audiovisual and Digital Communication Regulatory Authority (ARCOM) National Commission for Information Technology and Civil Liberties (CNIL) national network for coordinating the regulation of digital services
init1_name1: Pix Platform - Responsible digital skills on Pix
init1_justif1: The Pix 'Responsible digital skills' framework directly addresses this indicator through its 'Social and societal issues' domain. It requires users to 'adopt a critical mind regarding... the biases produced and the dissemination of false information'. The curriculum specifically covers 'countering disinformation'  evaluating image authenticity and recognising 'cyberbullying and knowing how to react' to limit exposure to cyberviolence. Additionally it trains users to understand 'biases produced' by digital content and the ethical stance regarding generative AI training directly mitigating AI-facilitated risks.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: The National Commission for Information Technology and Freedoms
init1_name1: Decision No MED-2024-150 of November 15 2024 (Formal Notice to Ministry of Interior)
init1_justif1: This initiative constitutes a 'corrective order' where the CNIL exercised its remedial power to stop a rights violation. By issuing Decision No. MED-2024-150 the CNIL formally ordered the Ministry of the Interior to cease the illegal use of facial recognition in the BriefCam software. This serves as a direct state-based non-judicial remedy effectively halting the infringing conduct (illegal biometric surveillance) and enforcing the public's right to protection against unlawful algorithmic processing as mandated by the GDPR and the Data Protection Act.

imp1_body_name: Authority for the Regulation of Audiovisual and Digital Communication (Autorit de rgulation de la communication audiovisuelle et numrique Arcom) National Commission for Information Technology and Civil Liberties (Commission nationale de linformatique et des liberts CNIL) The administrative authority responsible for competition and consumer affairs (Lautorit administrative charge de la concurrence et de la consommation DGCCRF)
init1_name1: Arcom Technical Framework on Age Verification
init1_justif1: The Arcom 'Référentiel' (October 2024) establishes technical standards for age verification systems to protect minors from online pornography explicitly referencing the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and the 'best interests of the child' (p. 7). It specifically regulates AI-driven solutions ('machine learning' p. 12) mandating they minimize discriminatory biases to ensure equal effectiveness across all demographics (pp. 12-13). The standards also require robustness against AI-enabled circumvention risks like 'deepfakes' (p. 11) and enforcing 'double anonymity' to protect user privacy (pp. 14-18).

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
init1_name1: Alliance for Language Technologies
init1_justif1: This initiative addresses diversity by explicitly aiming to 'protect and promote European cultural and linguistic heritage' within the Artificial Intelligence ecosystem. Coordinated by the Ministry of Culture it functions as a European consortium designed to develop 'sovereign' models trained on diverse linguistic data. This effort directly counters the dominance of English-centric systems by enabling the 'emergence and sharing of giant... language models capable of enhancing our cultural and linguistic heritage' (Page 1) ensuring Artificial Intelligence technologies respect and reflect the diversity of European languages.

imp1_body_name: Ministry of Ecological Transition Biodiversity and International Negotiations on Climate and Nature
init1_name1: Public consultation to enhance the annual Achieving digital sustainability survey (Expansion for AI/Cloud)
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: This ARCEP public consultation directly implements the framework's transparency requirements by proposing an expansion of its annual environmental data collection to include cloud service providers. The goal is explicitly to assess the environmental impact of AI. Proposed indicators aim to measure greenhouse gas emissions power consumption and water consumption specifically linked to cloud services including the impact of generative AI on resource use and hardware lifecycles. This establishes a monitoring system for the environmental footprint of AI infrastructure and operations. (Source: Pages 1-2)

init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: This official government webpage details the designated authorities. The text and the flowchart (Page 7) specify the bodies responsible for enforcing the AI Act's fairness rules. Under the section 'Prohibited practices' (Page 2) the document states the CNIL and DGCCRF will control 'social assessment classification or rating'. The CNIL is also assigned to monitor 'biometric categorization' and 'predictive policing' . Under 'Systems covered by Annex III' (Page 4) the CNIL is designated to control 'employment and workforce management' and the ACPR will control 'creditworthiness' systems.

imp1_body_name: DGCCRF Direction gnrale de la concurrence de la consommation et de la rpression des fraudes (Directorate-General for Competition Consumer Affairs and Fraud Control) & DGE Direction gnrale des Entreprises (Directorate-General for Enterprise)
init1_name1: Designation of national competent authorities (AI Act)
init1_justif1: This initiative operationalises the EU AI Act by designating the national bodies responsible for its enforcement. These authorities are tasked with enforcing the Act's legally binding non-discrimination provisions. For example they will oversee high-risk AI systems used in employment (Annex III) to ensure compliance with bias mitigation rules. They will also enforce prohibition on discriminatory biometric categorisation based on sexual orientation directly addressing gender equality by providing a mechanism to protect against bias.

init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: This initiative operationalises the EU AI Act in France by designating the specific national authorities (CNIL DGCCRF DGE etc.) responsible for its enforcement. The EU AI Act framework which these bodies now oversee directly mandates human oversight measures. Specifically the Act requires that high-risk AI systems be designed and developed in a way that allows them to be effectively overseen by natural persons. This initiative thus creates the national mechanism responsible for enforcing the framework's human oversight requirements.

init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: This initiative designates the authorities (DGCCRF and CNIL) responsible for overseeing the Fundamental Rights Impact Assessments (FRIA) mandated by Article 27 of the EU AI Act. By appointing these bodies as market surveillance authorities France establishes the institutional mechanism to receive and review these assessments. Specifically deployers of high-risk AI must notify these authorities of their impact assessment results. This designation ensures that the mandatory evaluation of risks to fundamental rights is not just a paperwork exercise but is subject to official regulatory oversight and enforcement.

imp1_body: No there is no implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation.
init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: This initiative designates the authorities responsible for enforcing the EU AI Act's labour protections. It ensures oversight of high-risk AI systems used for recruitment promotion termination and worker management (Annex III(4)) monitoring compliance with risk management (Art. 9) bias control (Art. 10) and human oversight (Art. 14) obligations. Additionally it enforces the ban on emotion recognition in the workplace (Art. 5(1)(f)) and ensures deployers fulfill their duty to inform workers subject to high-risk AI systems (Art. 26(11)).

imp1_body_name: Commission for Access to Administrative Documents
init1_name1: Guide on Public Algorithms (Guide des algorithmes publics)
init1_justif1: This official guide serves as the operational standard for implementing the transparency obligations of Article L. 312-1-3. It provides a step-by-step methodology for public administrations to identifying their algorithms inventory them and publish the required information. Specifically the section 'L'obligation de transparence' details the legal scope while the section 'Comment expliquer ?' provides technical and editorial guidelines for publishing source code and writing intelligible explanations to ensure effective public disclosure.
init1_name2: Report Algorithms AI systems and public services (Rapport Algorithmes systmes dIA et services publics)
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: This report constitutes a monitoring and evaluation mechanism assessing the implementation of transparency obligations (CRPA). Part III (pp. 28-39) specifically addresses 'Transparency' noting that the obligation to publish rules defining main algorithmic systems often remains a 'dead letter' (p. 37 ). It identifies failures in public disclosure (p. 37 ) and formulates recommendations to enforce these rules such as establishing a national census of compliance and introducing sanctions for administrations that fail to publish their algorithmic rules (p. 37 ).

imp1_body: n/a
init1_name1: Innovative Public Procurement
init1_justif1: This initiative addresses the procurement of Artificial Intelligence by establishing a simplified legal mechanism that allows public buyers to award contracts up to 100000 euros without advertising or competitive bidding for 'innovative works supplies or services.' The definition of innovation used explicitly covers new or significantly improved implementations which directly applies to Artificial Intelligence solutions. By removing complex administrative barriers the initiative aims to facilitate the integration of digital technologies from startups and Small and Medium-sized Enterprises into the public sector encouraging the state to act as a 'first customer' for emerging tech.
init1_name2: State Buyer - Key Figures 2024
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The report actively monitors 'The development of innovation procurement' (p. 5) which serves as the primary mechanism for acquiring AI solutions. It explicitly tracks expenditure on 'start-ups' (€498M across 1305 entities since 2023) and 'innovative SMEs' (9% of 2024 spend) quantifying the state's engagement with the ecosystem developing AI . Furthermore it isolates 'Information technology' as a major spending category (€4.26B) (p. 3) and identifies top digital suppliers often involved in AI services such as Capgemini Technology and Thales Digital Services (p. 4).
init1_name3: Innovation Rendezvous AI and Public Procurement
init1_justif3: This initiative is a targeted matchmaking event organised by the State Procurement Department (DAE) to facilitate the Public Procurement of AI. Titled 'Innovation Rendezvous: Artificial Intelligence and Public Procurement' it connects government buyers with the tech ecosystem to meet 'priority procurement needs' specifically in 'artificial intelligence' [p. 2]. By focusing exclusively on AI (alongside cybersecurity and real estate) the session functions as a specialised platform for public purchasers to identify and source innovative AI solutions directly from the market [p. 2].

init1_name1: Public Digital Campus (Campus du Numrique Public)
init1_justif1: The initiative is a centralized government platform specifically designed to upskill the entire French civil service ('agents publics'). Managed by the Interministerial Directorate for Digital Affairs (DINUM) it addresses skills development by aggregating and creating digital training programs. Crucially it features a dedicated 'Data and Artificial Intelligence' category offering specific modules like 'Discover Generative AI' (co-produced with Inria Academy). This directly implements the national mandate to 'massify' AI literacy and equip state employees with the technical competencies required for public sector transformation.

init2_justif1: The JUST fAIr project directly addresses public sector skills development by providing a structured 24-month training program led by the National School for the Judiciary (ENM). It aims to bridge the 'digital gap' among justice professionals (magistrates clerks and lawyers) through 'acculturation' and technical training.

init1_name1: Compte Personnel de Formation (Personal Training Account)
init1_justif1: The Compte Personnel de Formation (CPF) is a government-managed financing mechanism that credits a dedicated budget (in Euros) to every active worker's account for autonomous training purchase. The platform functions as the official national marketplace enabling workers to directly search for book and pay for eligible training courses aimed at skills adaptation including digital and technical skills required for the evolving labor market.

init2_name1: Comptences et Mtiers dAvenir (Skills and Jobs of the Future)
init2_type1: Dedicated budget lines or public tenders
init2_justif1: This France 2030 initiative funds training projects to meet the skills needs of 'jobs of the future' specifically targeting the 'digital' (numérique) and 'STEM' sectors. It aims to train 400000 people per year and 1 million new graduates by 2030. The call funds 'training schemes' (dispositifs de formation) for both 'initial and continuous' training (reskilling). Crucially it mandates that projects be carried out by a 'consortium' composed of employers (enterprises) and training organizations (universities schools) ensuring partnerships to develop curricula adapted to sovereign needs.

init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: The designation of these national authorities is the primary mechanism for enforcing the EU AI Act in France. The AI Act itself contains core provisions on safety and security specifically requiring high-risk AI systems to be designed with appropriate levels of accuracy robustness and cybersecurity and to be resilient against errors or malicious attacks (Article 15). These designated bodies are responsible for monitoring compliance with these technical standards and taking corrective action against unsafe or unreliable AI systems thus directly implementing the framework's safety and security objectives.

init1_name1: Designation of National Competent Authorities
init1_justif1: France designated independent authorities to enforce the AI Act under Art. 70 justifying the 'Independent' qualification. The CNIL (Data Protection Authority) oversees high-risk systems (biometrics employment) and prohibited practices leveraging its autonomous sanctioning powers. The Arcom (Audiovisual Regulator) supervises transparency for GenAI and deepfakes (Art. 50). Unlike government ministries these bodies operate with statutory independence to protect fundamental rights like privacy and freedom of communication.

init1_name1: Train the Trainers workshop at the University of The Gambia
init1_justif1: Organised by the Commonwealth Secretariat and the Commonwealth Artificial Intelligence Consortium (CAIC) (of which The Gambia is the lead for the Working Group on Research and Innovation for the CAIC) university educators were trained in Python Programming and Artificial Intelligence (AI) at the University of Gambia. Lead government agency: Ministry of Trade Regional Integration and Employment. The Minister noted that “This initiative not only empowers our educators but also ensures that our youth who are at the forefront of AI development are well-prepared for future workforce opportunities.'
init1_name2: Conference on the growing role of AI (AI) in the classroom
init1_justif2: The conference organized by Africa Mission Global (AMG) in partnership with the Ministry of Basic and Secondary Education aimed to demystify AI for educators and equip them with basic tools to integrate it into their teaching methods. The conference was aimed at clearing up misconceptions around AI explain its uses and also talk about the risks of over-reliance on machines and the ethics of AI in youth education.

init1_name1: Nationwide campaign against misinformation and disinformation
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Information Media and Broadcasting Services launched a nationwide campaign against misinformation and disinformation on January 24 2025. The campaign aims to raise public awareness about the dangers of false information promote responsible consumption of media publications and equip citizens with tools to identify and counter misinformation and disinformation. The relevant Minister noted at the launch that 'it is crucial that policymakers legislators and people in all walks of life are equipped with the necessary tools and knowledge to address artificially-generated disinformation and misinformation”. The awareness tour started on the 31st of January to different areas of the Gambia and seems to be ongoing.

imp1_body_name: Federal Ministries for Economic Affairs Ministry (BMWK) Research (BMBF) and Labour (BMAS)
init1_name1: AI in vocational education
init1_justif1: This initiative builds AI literacy in vocational education by offering structured AI specialist-certified training under the Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) project “AI B3” responding to reported skills shortages in AI technologies (Intro). For apprentices the “Additional qualification in AI” targets foundational understanding of AI’s relevance for their professional futures across sectors (section “Trainee”). For workers “AI Specialist (IHK)” develops applied competence (data ML evaluation of use cases) including ethical questions and basic programming.

init1_name1: German Digital Services Coordinator (DSC/BNetzA) commissioned study Gesellschaftliche Auswirkungen systemischer Risiken (DSA systemic-risk research)
init1_justif1: The DSC-commissioned study treats AI as an amplifier of disinformation and violence-related harms within DSA systemic-risk work: it notes that disinformation can be produced and spread using AI especially generative AI (incl. deepfakes) increasing scale quality and personalisation (Sec 4.4 pp. 28–32 Summary p.3). It links disinformation ecosystems to digital violence threats and extremist mobilisation contributing to radicalisation and violence (pp. 39–42; p. 70).

imp1_body_name: BfDI (Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information) 16 Lnder data protection authorities
init1_name1: DSK (The German Data Protection Conference) Working Group on AI (AK KI)
init1_justif1: The The German Data Protection Conference (DSK) formally established its dedicated Working Group on Artificial Intelligence (Arbeitskreis KI) in late 2024. This working group coordinates the legal and technical expertise of all federal and state DPAs. This initiative is a structural action to enhance the capacity of Germany’s federal and state data protection authorities to process AI-related complaints under Article 77 GDPR.
init1_name2: DSK Guidance on Technical and Organisational Measures for AI
init1_justif2: The DSK guidance embeds intervenability so that affected persons can seek redress. It defines intervenability as the ability of controllers to intervene across the lifecycle to implement data-subject rights and authorities’ orders (p. 7). It requires that outputs can be questioned and traced by users/human controllers with corresponding functions provided (p. 16). At deployment authorities must document human intervention options and data-subject rights (p. 19). In operation it prescribes human-control measures (pending state mandatory human confirmation periodic release) to enable timely intervention (pp. 22–23).

init1_name1: BNetzA Service Desk
init1_justif1: BNetzA’s function as national market-surveillance authority enables prevention detection and sanctioning of unlawful AI that affects children. Its Single Point of Contact/Service Desk and surveillance powers provide a route for complaints and corrective action where minors could be harmed. The draft KIMUG bill BNetzA must involve the Federal Agency for Child and Youth Media Protection (BzKJ) whenever an AI system impacts minors.

init1_name1: The AI Language Proficiency Monitor
init1_justif1: The German government developed the AI Language Proficiency Monitor jointly between the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ) development agency GIZ and the German Research Center for Artificial Intelligence (DFKI) (p.1). The AI Language Proficiency Monitor tracks the performance of LLMs in up to 200 languages with a strong focus on low-resource languages (p.1 Abstract). Its overall aim is to help support private- and public-sector decision-making with a 'particular emphasis on underserved communities' (p.1). More information (https://slator.com/german-gov-backed-ai-benchmark-tracks-large-language-models-200-languages/)

init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: Managed by the Federal Energy Efficiency Center (BfEE) (part of Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control (BAFA)) this digital register collects mandatory annual data on energy consumption power usage effectiveness (PUE) and renewable energy use from approximately 1000 data centers. The first reporting cycles were completed in 2024 and 2025.
init1_name2: Official implementation support for data-centre duties (FAQ) BAFA + BMWK
init1_justif2: The Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action (BMWK)  and Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control (BAFA) creates resources such as FAQs to address environmental impact for AI by operationalising EnEfG duties for data centres which host AI compute. It explains mandatory public reporting of energy metrics via the national register under (Q “How and where… published?” p. 3) compliance with renewable electricity requirements under (Q on certificates/PPAs pp. 3–4) and the obligation to implement an energy or environmental management system (ISO 50001/EMAS) under (Q 'Is a data centre operator...' p. 4). Also federal funding programmes for energy-efficiency (p.5 from 'Are there any federal funding programs...')

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
init1_name1: National AI Act market-surveillance authority (BNetzA)
init1_justif1: BNetzA serves as Germany’s central market-surveillance authority for the EU AI Act. In this role it supervises compliance with provisions that curb discriminatory outcomes including the ban on exploitative practices and the high-risk obligations on risk management data governance transparency and human oversight that apply to areas such as employment and education. BNetzA can receive complaints demand corrective measures and impose sanctions for non-compliance providing a concrete enforcement route for fairness and non-discrimination in AI.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: EU AI Office and the BundesNetzAgentur on the national level
init1_name1: AI Observatory
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The AI Observatory (BMAS 2020) conducts research and develops indicators for the Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government of which includes gender in AI. These studies integrate gender perspectives into monitoring and policy evidence. However the Observatory’s role remains analytical and advisory; it does not impose obligations or corrective mechanisms to address gender discrimination in AI.
init1_name2: AI Campus AI power for women 45+
init1_justif2: “AI Power for Women 45+” builds AI literacy skills and confidence among mid-career and later-career women a group underrepresented in AI and data roles. By offering accessible practice-oriented modules (e.g. understanding AI systems risks and responsible use) it lowers entry barriers to AI-related upskilling and employability broadens the talent pipeline beyond traditional STEM channels and enables informed participation in AI adoption and oversight. The course directly aims to combat age and gender discrimination.
init1_name3: Decisions of the 34th Conference of Ministers Senatorsand Ministers for Equality and Womens Affairs of the Lnder (GMFK Resolution)
init1_justif3: This GFMK resolution calls on the federal government to embed gender equality into AI Act implementation. The 2024 GFMK resolution on gender-responsive digitalisation recognises that AI systems can reproduce and amplify structural gender biases through skewed data opaque algorithms and automated decision making in areas such as employment welfare and policing. The resolution calls on the federal government to embed gender equality and non-discrimination into all procedures for implementing the EU AI Act including risk assessment testing standards and an AI register for public-sector systems so that gendered harms are identified prevented and remedied.

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
imp1_body_name: AI Office European AI Board Bundesnetzagentur (BNetzA)
init1_name1: Mandated AI Regulatory Sandboxes
init1_justif1: The Ministerial Draft Bill (Sept 2025) on p. 13 Section 4 mandates a national AI Regulatory Sandbox (KI-Reallabor). This can be considered a formal government action that commits to a programmatic structure and is a direct implementation of Article 57 of the EU AI Act. This program forces providers of high-risk AI systems to develop and test under regulatory supervision. This enforces Art. 14 of the EU AI Act for Human Oversight by ensuring systems can be stopped and its operations are fully traceable (Art. 12 logging) before being released.

init1_name1: BMWK (Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Change) AI Regulatory Sandboxes Portal
init1_justif1: The BMWK's (Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Change) Regulatory Sandboxes Innovation Portal provides a controlled environment for companies to test and refine their AI systems. The section 'International Pioneers and the European Framework' states sandboxes are now 'anchored in various legal acts such as the EU AI Act.' The AI Regulatory Sandbox as established by the AI Act under Art. 57 is designed to be the practical implementation mechanism that supports the regulatory compliance obligations of the Impact Assessments in Art. 57 of the EU AI Act. The sandbox helps deployers practice and document their full EU AI Act obligations including the effective execution and reporting of their FRIA prior to market deployment.

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
init1_name1: AI Cockpit Project Human oversight and control of AI at work
init1_justif1: The AI Cockpit is financed by the BMAS (Federal Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs) Think Tank “Digital Working Society”. Its premise is that workers and employers must be able to understand supervise and steer AI used on the job to maintain transparency and control in line with a human-in-command model. It is designed to give employees clear insight into an AI system’s outputs so they can make informed judgements on when to intervene in automated processes. Ex: Study 1 addresses the monitoring of AI in personnel selection by providing tools for HR departments and works councils to scrutinise algorithmic filtering. The software is available in 2 editions to support AI deployments that comply with the requirements of the EU AI Act.
init1_name2: BAuA (Federal Institute for Occupational Safety & Health) AI Workshop 2024 Research Regulation Working World
init1_justif2: A day-long workshop hosted by the Federal Institute for Occupational Safety & Health TU Dortmund University and the Lamarr Institute focused on trustworthy AI regulation and the workplace. The programme (p.5) addressed developments in AI research and the world of work like trustworthy AI systems and their integration into a human-centered work environment. Sessions such as ethical and legal challenges to align with the EU AI Act and balancing efficiency and ethics of AI in the workplace were held for the practical integration of AI into human-centered environments to tackle the legal safeguards required to protect employees' rights under the EU AI Act. (Program: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1WOWmGluHoqWCad-C9uULmz65kQAF5XMO/view).

init1_justif1: Germany’s AI Opportunity Marketplace (MaKI) implements the Guidelines’ transparency principle by serving as a public AI transparency register for the federal administration offering an overview of AI systems used by authorities and their purposes. The federal IT Council formally endorsed MaKI as the central transparency register for the federal administration and the IT-Planning Council commissioned a pilot rollout to Länder and municipalities extending disclosure beyond the federal tier.

init1_name1: AI Opportunity Market (MaKI)
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The AI Opportunity Market (MaKI) launched at www.kimarktplatz.bund.de serves as the federal administration's central marketplace and transparency database. It addresses procurement by providing a comprehensive overview of existing and planned AI systems (BMI Press Jan 2025). This initiative meets EU AI Act transparency mandates and streamlines government spending by facilitating the broader use of existing technical expertise across authorities. By acting as a pilot for state and local government inclusion in 2025 MaKI ensures that procurement is not fragmented promoting technological sovereignty and efficient resource utilisation while maintaining high ethical and transparency standards across the civil service.

The project operationalises reskilling by creating certified occupation-oriented AI curricula delivered through IHK structures enabling workers to transition into AI-augmented roles.

init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Federal Network Agency’s AI Service Desk acts as Germany’s single national intake and triage channel for AI-Act enquiries and complaints from providers deployers and the public. The AI Service Desk operationalises safety and security by guiding organisations on risk-based duties and routing cases into supervision. The portal explains the AI Act’s aim to safeguard health safety and fundamental rights outlines risk levels and high-risk systems and links to transparency obligations notification single point of contact and market surveillance functions. This chain operationalises risk controls testing and incident response.

init1_name1: DIN/DKE National mirror committee of CEN/CLC/JTC 21
init1_justif1: DIN and DKE established a mirror committee of CEN/CLC/JTC 21 for German experts to contribute to the operationalisation of the AI Act's technical specifications incuding requirements in Art. 12 on explainability and Art. 13 on transparency. The development of the standard 'Record keeping:built-in logging capabilities in AI systems' defines the technical specifications and auditable log formats for tracing system operations and verifying outputs for high-risk AI systems (Art. 12). The standard 'Transparency and information to the users of AI systems' mandates technical requirements for clear Instructions for Use and detailed information disclosure about the system's capabilities limitations and expected accuracy to deployers (Art. 13).
init1_name2: Mission KI
init1_justif2: A key pillar of Mission KI is to foster the development of transparent and explainable AI ensuring compatibility with the EU AI Act’s requirements for high-risk AI systems. The initiative creates assets and knowledge capacity such as the voluntary Mission KI Quality Standard and webinars on transparency in AI.

init1_name1: Union Government issues advisory to social media intermediaries to identify misinformation and Deepfakes
init1_justif1: The advisory issued by the Union Government implements gender equality by targeting harms that disproportionately affect women notably deepfakes and explicitly anchors these obligations in the IT rules. It requires intermediaries to identify and remove such content within 36 hours reiterates legal consequences for non-compliance under the IT rules (including Rule 7) and enables affected individuals to seek remedies.
init1_name2: Grievance Appellate Committee Workshop
init1_justif2: In January 2023 the government appointed Grievance Appellate Committee GACs and since then the GACs have received over 2300 appeals of which 2081 are resolved. The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) organized a workshop on the Grievance Appellate Committee (GAC) on 7th January 2025 to discuss strengthening the grievance redressal framework under the Information Technology Act 2000. The government urged social media intermediaries to act quickly against fake content to build public trust in grievance officers. It was also emphasized that GACs must work efficiently to resolve issues promptly ensuring users can navigate the internet safely and with confidence.

init1_name1: Online Safety Code
init1_justif1: The Code applies to video-sharing platforms and services. It requires these platforms to restrict certain categories of videos and associated content which include criminal content cyberbullying promotion of eating disorders promotion of self-harm and suicide hatred or violence for various reasons such as child sexual abuse material terrorism racism and xenophobia (pp. 2-3). It protects children from pornography and extreme or gratuitous violence (p. 3) to ensure consistency in the online safety approach between the Digital Services Act (p. 2).
init1_name2: Consultation on the Draft Online Safety Code and Statutory Guidance Material
init1_justif2: pg 6- The Ombudsman for Children's Office (OCO) initiative in the consultation on the Draft Online Safety Code is a critical intervention that addresses Children's Rights in the context of AI by promoting the principle of Safety by Design over mere optional parental controls.The OCO encourages the Commission to clarify in and through the draft Code and Guidance that including default settings among the list of parental controls that VSPS providers may adopt on their service does not obviate the need for providers to adopt a high level of safety by design and privacy by design in the development and modification of features on their service

init1_name1: The Responsible AI Canvas
init1_justif1: In accordance with Sec 6.1 P. 44 of the Guidelines for the Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Public Service the Responsible AI Canvas serves as the foundational mechanism for a project's initial impact assessment. Designed as a structured tool the Canvas enables public service workers to evaluate and align proposed solutions with the seven Principles for Responsible AI during the critical planning stage.By utilizing this tool before deployment organizations can proactively identify potential risks to fairness privacy and safety ensuring that the AI’s societal impact is thoroughly evaluated https://web.archive.org/web/20251227132442/https://assets.gov.ie/static/documents/Responsible_AI_Canvas.pdf

init1_name1: AI Dil ireann Debate
init1_justif1: A parliamentary discussion between Deputy Sinéad Gibney and  Minister for Enterprise Tourism and Employment about labour protections. Question 720. Deputy Sinéad Gibney asked the Minister for Enterprise Tourism and Employment further to Parliamentary Question No. 966 of 29 July 2025 the data and research his Department has to support the answer regarding the emergence of new jobs; whether these jobs will be equal or greater than the number of jobs lost to AI; whether the minister is aware of research which indicates AI has led to decreased graduate roles and increased labour displacement; and if he will make a statement on the matter.
init1_name2: AI Friend or Foe?An Analysis of How AI Could Impact Irelands Labour Market
init1_justif2: To establish a proactive framework for labor protection the report provides an evidentiary basis for quantifying Ireland’s significant labor market exposure to AI (63%) which exceeds the international average for advanced economies (60%) identifying a heightened potential for structural displacement. By pinpointing 'at-risk' occupations especifically the 38% of females in high-exposure low-complementarity roles and the 38% of bachelor's degree holders in vulnerable positionsthe analysis functions as a critical diagnostic tool for anticipating job losses and safeguarding worker security (Pg 17).

init1_justif2: pg 42. section 6.1 The Responsible AI Canvas Overview: The Responsible AI Canvas is a simple structured tool designed to help public service workers developimplement and oversee responsible AI solutions that meet the seven Principles for Responsible AI. Technical Robustness and Safety is one of the seven principles the canvas addresses. It Prompts for a risk assessment to identify potential failures errors or security vulnerabilities (e.g. adversarial attacks). It mandates planning for rigorous testing and validation to ensure the system is reliable secure and performs consistently thereby preventing unintended harm.

It combats the spread of false information using AI advocates safeguarding citizens against fraud and harassment enabled by AI and exhibits proactive prevention measures.

init1_name1: Consip Integration of Environmental Standards (CAM) in ICT and Data Center Procurement
init1_justif1: Consip (in-house company of the Ministry of Economy and Finance manages the public procurement system) from 2024 starts to systematically integrate Criteri Ambientali Minimi (CAM) into ICT procurement including data centers and cloud services. Section 11.2 of the Relazione sulla Gestione highlights CAM application to ICT tenders requiring energy-efficient server consolidation virtualization and cooling. Contracts include the “Opzione Verde” for renewable electricity in data centers and mandate supplier reporting on energy performance. These standards ensure public AI workloads run on greener more efficient infrastructures reducing their environmental impact
init1_name2: Green-aware AI Spook - FAIR Foundation
init1_justif2: The Green-aware AI Spoke focuses on embedding environmental sustainability into AI by design. Its work packages target green-aware learning with reduced computational resources energy-efficient deep learning on IoT devices graph sparsification for disaster management to save energy and green-ML for healthcare. It also develops pilots in smart energy management renewable energy systems sustainable cities and Industry 5.0. These activities directly address the environmental impact of AI by reducing carbon footprint energy use and resource intensity

init1_name1: Decalogue for the provision of national health services through AI systems - Privacy Authority
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The Garante Decalogue establishes mandatory standards for AI in national health services focusing on justice non-discrimination openness and human-in-the-loop safeguards operationalizing GDPR (Arts. 22 35) in healthcare AI situations. It requires: (1) the principle of non-discrimination algorithmica with periodic bias checks and data rectification; (2) the principle of non-exclusivity to maintain human oversight; and (3) knowledgeability so patients understand the logic utilized (pp. 10-14 particularly Section 4).
init1_name2: Provision no 675/2024 against Foodinho srl (Glovo Group) algorithmic management of riders (Italian Data Protection Authority)
init1_justif2: Glovo's rider management algorithm was deemed by the Garante to be both opaque and possibly discriminatory. The decree (Provv. 675/2024) prohibits fully automated punitive decisions and mandates proportionality contestability and reasoning transparency. In order to prevent discrimination against workers guarantee meaningful human review and adhere to non-discrimination protections under the GDPR and national labor legislation it requires Foodinho to modify monitoring and scoring (pp. 2–6 12–15 of decision).

init1_name1: AI Impact assessment - AGID
init1_justif1: The AI Impact Assessment Guidelines (AgID Feb 2025) provide a structured tool for public administrations to assess AI systems before adoption. They operationalize impact assessments by requiring administrations to document scope purpose and risk level (C1) analyze effects on fundamental rights fairness and bias (C2) ensure robustness and explainability (C3) verify data governance and privacy (C4) test risk management and fallback procedures (C5) and guarantee accountability and transparency (C6). An appendix links directly to the AI Act’s risk levels ensuring compliance.

The authority stated there can be no “incontestable” algorithm deciding working conditions. It mandated safeguards to prevent discriminatory automated decisions obligations to inform workers about algorithmic logic and rights to challenge AI-driven outcomes.Ref: Garante Doc. 9782895

init2_name1: National School of Administration The Art of the Prompt course (Public Administration AI Training)
init2_justif1: The National School of Administration (SNA) delivers government-funded training for public employees on responsible and effective use of AI including generative systems and prompt design. The course builds technical and ethical competencies for integrating AI into administrative processes covering automated decision-making legal implications and practical applications. Multiple 2025 sessions including on 30/09/2025 demonstrate ongoing implementation.

init1_name1: Fondo Nuove Competenze (New Skills Fund)
init1_justif1: Fondo Nuove Competenze is a national government program that funds company workforce training by reimbursing wage costs during participation in approved courses including AI and digital-skills reskilling paths. It operationalizes the Strategy’s commitment to “incentives for reskilling and upskilling in all production contexts” (p.30) and supports structured training aligned with technological transition priorities (F.5 pp. 33–34) enabling workers to adapt to AI-driven labour market changes.

init1_name1: National Cybersecurity Agency adheres to International Guidelines on AI Safety
init1_justif1: By integrating secure-by-design procedures and resilience posture into the national cybersecurity plan ACN's compliance with the International Guidelines on AI Security is a government-led initiative that operationalizes Italy's responsibilities. It exhibits acceptance and application outside of the policy wording conforming to the Safety & Security clauses.
init1_name2: FAIR Spoke 3 Resilient AI
init1_justif2: Spoke 3 develops methodologies for robust and resilient AI in real-world and adversarial environments. It advances algorithms resistant to malicious data and attacks supports verification and validation of AI pipelines (including safety-critical sectors like automotive) and promotes strategies for unforeseen failures. It also addresses ethical/legal issues to ensure compliance with safety norms (pp. 3–6 Spoke 3 – Resilient AI).

init1_name1: AgID Public Consultation on AI Guidelines for Public Administration (Draft 2025)
init1_justif1: The proposed Guidelines place clear transparency requirements on PA AI systems including the need for decision logic to be explicable and for users to be informed when engaging with AI. They require notice requirements disclosure procedures and communication protocols and make reference to AI Act Art. 50 transparency duties (pp. 39–41 §§7.1–7.2). These clauses seek to make AI in public services transparent responsible and reliable.
init1_name2: FAIR Spoke 1 Human-Centered AI
init1_justif2: Spoke 1 develops explainable and trustworthy AI through human-in-the-loop methods interpretable decision-support systems and empirical pilots (WP1.1 WP1.6 WP1.7). It focuses on explainable AI for medical and industrial case studies co-design of trustworthy-by-design systems and risk assessment of explainers ensuring users can understand evaluate and trust AI outputs (pp. 3–12 17–21).

init1_name1: Govt to implement AI workforce readiness strategy
init1_justif1: In September 2025 the Government of Jamaica in partnership with the Amber Group established a National AI Lab at the University of Technology Jamaica (https://www.jamaicaobserver.com/2025/10/17/amber-group-bets-ai-robotics-next-decade/). The Lab's explicit objective is to upskill Jamaicans in AI offering advanced AI training including machine learning data science and deep learning. This constitutes a formal tertiary-level AI literacy initiative equipping students with technical competencies to understand evaluate and create AI systems. The initiative is supported by a signed MOU between UTech and the Amber Group and is operational as of September 2025.

init1_name1: National Child Online Protection Committee (NCOP)
init1_justif1: Cabinet approved the NCOP in November 2021 (1). The mandate of the committee is to develop a National Child Online Protection Strategy. While the 27-member committee has been formed and is looking at the threats and other aspects of AI as well as other dangers associated with internet use no strategies have been developed to date. In fact the committee's Chair indicated in May 2024 that they were seeking to have the strategies developed within 9 to 12 months (2). (1)https://jis.gov.jm/strategy-approved-to-protect-children-online/ (2) https://web.archive.org/web/20251102161435/https://iriefm.net/cabinet-appointed-committee-developing-strategies-to-protect-children-from-artificial-intelligence-and-other-related-threats-online/

init1_name1: National Intelligence Task Force Policy Recommendations
init1_type1: Mandated bodies (commissions task forces or inter-agency working groups) to draft an AI-related framework.
init1_justif1: The report was the culmination of extensive research stakeholder engagement and rigorous analysis conducted by the National AI Task Force whose members represented academia industry government and civil society. Their recommendations (pp. 22 24 and 30) talk about the need for Jamaica to develop national ethical guidelines that outline core principle of responsible AI that focus on fairness transparency and accountability.

init1_name1: Office of the Information Commissioner (OIC)
init1_drive1: NA - No document but the website of the oversight body is linked.
init1_justif1: The OIC is responsible for monitoring compliance issuing guidelines and advising the Minister on data protection matter. The DPA grants individuals the right not to be subject to decisions made solely by automated means that significantly affect them. It also gives them the right to request human review or challenge such decisions. They can do this to the data controller who has to respond within thirty days. The OIC has oversight and may order the data controller to reconsider the decision or to take a new decision that is not based solely on automated decision.

init1_name1: Reskilling Upskilling and Re-skilling (RUN) Project
init1_justif1: The Transformation Implementation Unit (TIU) of the Government of Jamaica operates the RUN (Reskilling Upskilling New-skilling) Project which provides fully funded access to over 7000 courses and certification programmes for public sector employees via Coursera and Academy+. Artificial Intelligence is explicitly listed as one of five priority skill development areas alongside Data Science Power BI Microsoft Excel and Customer Service. The programme is open to all public sector employees with selection prioritising those whose professional roles align with the subject matter.

init1_name1: National AI Task Force Policy Recommendations
init1_type1: Mandated bodies (commissions task forces or inter-agency working groups) to draft an AI-related framework.
init1_justif1: The report was the culmination of extensive research stakeholder engagement and rigorous analysis conducted by the National AI Task Force whose members represented academia industry government and civil society. Their recommendation (p. 7) talks to workforce adaptation and addressing the need for re-skilling and up-skilling to align with AI-driven economies. The government will be implementing an artificial intelligence (AI) workforce-readiness strategy (1) following these recommendations. (1) https://web.archive.org/web/20251107155741/https://jamaica-gleaner.com/article/news/20250322/govt-implement-ai-workforce-readiness-strategy
init1_name2: Reskilling Upskilling and Re-skilling (RUN) Project
init1_justif2: The Reskilling Upskilling and New-Skilling (RUN) Project started in July 2024 and is a direct response to the need to equip Government of Jamaica employees with relevant skills to function in the digital age. The RUN Project is managed by the Transformation Implementation Unit. The project offers public sector employees access to over 7000 self-paced courses and certification programmes from world-class universities and companies including Google Harvard Yale and Salesforce via online portals from Coursera and Academy+. Several AI courses are included.

init1_name1: National AI Task Force Policy Recommendations
init1_type1: Mandated bodies (commissions task forces or inter-agency working groups) to draft an AI-related framework.
init1_justif1: The National AI Task Force Policy Recommendations published February 2025 (https://opm.gov.jm/national-ai-task-force-report-published/) were developed to guide Jamaica's national AI policy framework. Safety and security are explicitly prioritised (p. 3). Pages 30-31 propose developing AI standards and best practices to ensure interoperability and consistent implementation across sectors. Pages 7 and 14 address cybersecurity objectives including strengthening AI systems' resilience against cyber threats and protecting critical digital infrastructure.

init1_name1: Lets learn ICT literacy in five fields - How not to be deceived by fake and misinformation
init1_justif1: The Japanese Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications (MIC) launched a public education initiative to enhance Information and Communication Technology (ICT) literacy of the public. The program provides instructors with teaching materials and guidelines for courses across five thematic areas including “How not to be deceived by fake and misinformation (2nd edition).” This module specifically addresses generative AI-related misinformation such as fake images created by AI (Slide 48) and illustrates the danger by showing real-world examples that took place in Japan for instance AI-generated disaster footage that spread widely on social media and lead to panic or a fake CEO created by AI as a scam (Slides 31–32).
