Specifically for privacy violations the Appendix points out the need for followup response including subsequent remedy from damage [pp.135 152]. It also mentions under 'Specific Methods' regarding explainabillity to 'Establish and communicate the remedial mechanism including the process by which stakeholders can lodge complaints.' [p. 146].

init1_name1: Interim Report
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Report prepared by the AI Strategic Council under the “Integrated Innovation Strategy Promotion Council” emphasizes continuous and collaborative impact assessments throughout the AI lifecycle covering development provision and use. It calls on government and industry to analyses AI model characteristics potential risks and stakeholder impacts [p.13] urges information sharing regarding deployment [p.13] and highlights the need to ensure transparency at all stages of the AI lifecycle [p.17]. The Report also notes the importance of implementing safety evaluation systems as part of responsible deployment [p.18] and advises caution regarding outputs that may affect citizens’ rights emphasizing careful review beforehand [p.21].

init1_name1: The Employment Policy Study Group Report
init1_justif1: The report explains that the AI may lead to employment substitution and a reduction in jobs making it necessary to monitor labor demand and facilitate job placement to support workers who risk displacement [p.37]. It notes that tasks and roles will change and emphasizes labor–management communication to manage these transitions and prevent negative impacts on employment [p.38]. To address challenges that cannot be resolved individually the report calls for regional or industry-specific cooperation to support companies and workers who cannot adapt [p.39]. It further warns that AI systems can reproduce bias and discrimination stressing the need for considerate implementation of AI to protect workers from unfair treatment [pp. 40 57].
init1_name2: Guidelines to Business APPENDIX Ver 11
init1_justif2: The Appendix by the Ministry of Economy Trade and Industry (METI) serves as reference to the main Guidelines. The example on p.8  proposes the typical use-case of an AI-driven applicant-screening system that supports hiring decisions by analyzing past application data and acceptance outcomes. Also the example on pp.15-16 relates to the labour and work sector because it involves AI used in human-resources processes (specifically predicting whether applicants might withdraw) and shows how inappropriate handling of personal data in recruitment led to the system being abolished. The case directly concerns job applicants’ rights transparency and personal data protection in employment contexts which are core labour-related protections.

The research highlighted the importance of continuously disclosing high-priority data based on the method used in the survey.

init1_name1: Report on the Development of Digitally Skilled Workforce for the Era of Society 50 For Skills-Based Workforce Development
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Economy (METI) has held discussions on developing a digitally skilled workforce with experts through the Study Group on the Development of Digitally Skilled Workforce for the Era of Society 5.0 and its Working Group on Skills and Learning. The research findings were then consolidated in the report and released to the public. The report highlights the growing need to utilize digital transformation and AI and developing and securing human resources with the skills necessary to promote digital transformation [p.5]. The report also outlines many reasons and strategies for AI skill upscaling such as providing in-house AI training to equip employees with the skills to use generative AI leading to increased corporate value [p.16].

init1_name1: Evaluation Report of the 3rd joint test exercise Advancement of agent evaluation methods
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Japanese Institute of AI Safety (J-AISI) joined an AI safety exercise together with other AI safety-focused institutes from ten countries. The document presents their evaluation report on this exercise and its findings. Among the key questions they explored were how safe models are as agents with respect to common risk categories [p. 3]. They concluded that among the most important tasks is capturing an agent’s reasoning to identify when it displays unsafe thinking or behavior even if the eventual task outcome is not harmful [p. 4] and how to ensure agent safety across languages [p. 4].
init1_name2: Japan AI Safety Workshop
init1_justif2: The workshop's goal was to enable a discussion on the importance of ensuring that AI systems are designed with rigorous safety standards helps mitigate risks fosters transparency and promote responsible innovation [p.3]. It also noted that when organizations prioritize AI safety they build a foundation of trust that enables collaboration among stakeholders policymakers and the public and how a strong commitment to AI safety supports the sustainable growth of AI [p.3]. Akiko Murakami the Executive Director of the Japan AI Safety Institute (J-AISI) was also a Keynote speaker where she highlighted how innovation can only accelerate when safety is ensured [p.4] and to advance innovation and adoption of AI by integrating safety [p.5].
init1_name3: Research Report - Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems
init1_justif3: In order to provide an overview of security attacks specific to AI systems the Japanese AI ??Safety Institute (J-AISI) has compiled a list of potential attacks against AI and AI systems and their impacts that have been published as an academic paper. The document notes that AI-specific security measures are essential [p. 2] and the importance of measures against conventional cybersecurity attacks especially when system incorporates AI specifically those involve intervening in an AI model’straining or inference processes as well as exploiting or tampering with its inputsand outputs [p. 3]. Pages 10-20 then outline all of the potential attacks so users can be prepared.

init1_name1: Workshop to promote the realization of an AI-symbiotic society (3rd Meeting)
init1_justif1: The Information-Technology Promotion Agency Japan hosts a workshop to realize a society where humans and AI coexist assessing social issues that AI might cause in Japan. During the third meeting AI in the sensitive healthcare sector was discussed and in line with the framework the importance of implementing the AI system with transparency so that we can see what the software sees and what humans see was emphasized [p.12]. The expert also suggested that it is important to treat AI as merely a simple tool especially in highly specialized fields [p.12-13].

init1_name1: AI Regulatory Framework for the banking sector (2025)
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The CBJ AI Framework ensures access to redress and remedy through contestation and oversight. Section 2.4 (Accountability page 13) mandates open channels for customers to submit inquiries or requests for review of AI-based decisions with an appointed AI System Owner responsible for governance and compliance. In parallel Sections 6.1–6.2 (Incident Response and Business Continuity page 20–21) require AI-specific incident protocols centralized logs and prompt reporting to FinCERT under CBJ supervision enabling investigation correction and regulatory follow-up on AI-related harms.

init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The Regulatory Framework for the Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Jordanian Banking Sector (CBJ July 2025) embeds Human Oversight and Determination throughout its provisions. init1_name1: AI-Qyzmet National AI Skills Training Program for Civil Servants
init1_justif1: AI-Qyzmet is a government-led national training program launched by the Agency for Civil Service Affairs and the Ministry of Digital Development to build AI competencies among public officials. The program trains civil servants to use AI tools in administrative processes planning information processing decision-making and task automation. It includes practical modules on working with modern language models and implementing domestic AI solutions. Training is delivered through the Academy of Public Administration and its regional branches using flexible formats such as short “Snack Learning” video lessons. In 2025 the program aims to train around 30000 civil servants contributing directly to public-sector skills development.

init1_justif2: Build With AI for Public Sector 2025 is a government-supported national program launched by the Ministry of Digital Development together with Google and Apolitical to strengthen AI competencies across the civil service. It provides multi-level training covering senior leadership middle management and general staffon the responsible and effective use of AI tools in public administration. The program includes workshops applied courses and training modules based on Apolitical’s Government AI Campus a global platform for public-sector AI governance skills. By focusing on practical AI use cases decision-making and best practices for integrating AI in government workflows the initiative directly supports public-sector skills development

init1_name1: The Kenya High Courts 2025 Worldcoin judgment [ Republic v Tools for Humanity Corporation (US) & 8 others Katiba Institute & 4 others (Ex parte Applicants) Data Privacy & Governance Society of Kenya ]
init1_justif1: The Kenya High Court’s 2025 Worldcoin judgment directly advances access to redress and remedy for AI-related grievances by applying the Data Protection Act (2019) to biometric and automated decision-making systems. The Court recognized individuals’ rights to seek judicial relief where AI-driven processing causes harm—such as exploitation or unlawful data collection.It ordered suspension of Worldcoin’s AI-powered biometric operations erasure of unlawfully obtained data and mandated the ODPC to enforce compliance showing coordination between judicial and administrative mechanisms. This creates a practical pathway for redress—victims can file complaints with the ODPC or seek court intervention when AI  systems violate their privacy.

init1_justif1: The KEBS Code of Practice for AI is a Standard developed by the Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS) that aims to provide guidance on responsible development testing deployment and use of AI systems in Kenya. The Code of Practice is intended to help organizations develop provide and use AI systems responsibly. The Code explicitly incorporates AI impact-assessment concepts and requirements as part of responsible AI risk governance. It embeds these concepts within risk management ethical/societal concerns and documentation practices. The Code calls for AI impact assessments to evaluate the potential consequences of AI system failures and their effects on stakeholders and integrates impact assessment into its risk-management.

init1_justif1: The State Agency for Personal Data Protection under the Cabinet of Ministers of the Kyrgyz Republic and the Ministry of Internal Affairs jointly created the Interdepartmental Working Group on the Protection of Personal Data. It enhances coordination between government bodies to address violations of personal data rights in digital environment including those resulting from AI-driven processing and automated decision-making. Its establishment strengthens mechanisms for citizens to seek redress and ensures AI-related harms are handled transparently and effectively.
init1_name2: Official Request to Commercial Bank Kyrgyzstan OJSC Following Large-Scale Debiting Incident
init1_justif2: The State Agency for Personal Data Protection issued an official request to “Commercial Bank Kyrgyzstan” OJSC on 23 January 2024 after a large-scale unauthorized debiting of citizens’ funds allegedly linked to automated banking processes and may affect the interests of citizens and violate the principles of security and confidentiality of personal data. The Agency’s intervention aimed to ensure accountability transparency and compliance with data protection and security principles. This action strengthened access to redress by compelling the bank to clarify the causes address potential AI-related data misuse and take corrective measures to protect citizens’ rights.

init1_name1: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovation Technologies developed and released for public discussion the Draft Cabinet Resolution On the Approval of Requirements for the Methodology of Hazard Assessment of AI Systems and Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovation Technologies developed and released for public discussion the Draft Cabinet Resolution “On the Approval of Requirements for the Methodology of Hazard Assessment of Artificial Intelligence Systems and Requirements for High-Risk Artificial Intelligence Systems”. It establishes a unified framework for AI hazard (impact) assessments. It requires AI system owners to evaluate and classify risks before deployment introducing a step-by-step self-assessment algorithm aligned with the Digital Code (Art. 193). The initiative promotes transparency mitigates risks to citizens’ rights and aligns with OECD and EU AI regulatory principles.

init1_justif1: The research report was funded by the Ombudsman of the Republic of Latvia. It analyses non-discrimination law and AI regulation. The study collects and analyses examples from international practice related to discrimination caused by AI systems in four high-risk areas i.e. biometric identification education employment and access to basic services based on such 'protected characteristics' as gender disability race ethnicity social status age sexual orientation. The examples analysed reveals how the use of AI systems and algorithms can lead to discrimination.

The study collects and analyses examples from international practice related to discrimination caused by AI systems in four high-risk areas i.e. biometric identification education employment and access to basic services based on such 'protected characteristics' as gender disability race ethnicity social status age sexual orientation (p.5-10). The examples analysed from other countries reveal how the use of AI systems and algorithms can lead to gender bias discrimination and gender inequality (p.24-34). Recommendations related to these matters are provided for the implementation of the EU AI Act.

init1_name1: Guidelines Data Protection Impact Assessment
init1_justif1: According to the AI Act the fundamental rights impact assessment  FRIA and the Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under the GDPR have a complementary relationship. Article 27(4) of the AI Act states that if a DPIA already covers some elements required for the FRIA the FRIA can build upon the DPIA to avoid duplication. The State Data Inspectorate provided these guidelines to serve as standards for these impact assessments.
init1_name2: Research report AI systems and discrimination aspects
init1_justif2: The report analyses the regulatory framework for the prohibition of discrimination data protection and artificial intelligence as well as draws attention to the problems and challenges of the existing legal framework in the context of the use of algorithms and AI systems. It explores the most visible examples from international practice related to discrimination caused by AI systems in the four areas mentioned above. The report emphasises the need to carry out fundamental rights impact assessment of AI systems to prevent discrimination risks. Impact assessment is also listed as one of the essential recommendations in the conclusion (see p.57).

init1_name1: AI and the Labor MarketRelationship to Industrial Revolutions Possible Futures and Policy Choices
init1_justif1: Report prepared by the Research Division of the Information and Communication Department of the Chancellery of the Seimas of the Republic of Lithuania (parliament) that examines the impact of artificial intelligence (AI) applications on the global economy. The work creates 4 short scenarios of the consequences of AI: optimistic (AI as an expansion of human capabilities) pessimistic (structural unemployment and inequality) futuristic/utopian/dystopian (almost complete replacement of human labor) realistic (skills mismatch problem). It is intended for policymakers and all those interested in future developments. The conclusions emphasize that the ultimate consequences of AI likely depend more on policy decisions than othe technology (p.3).

init1_name1: MACRA Data Protection Authority (Independent Oversight Body)
init1_justif1: MACRA Data Protection Authority enforces human oversight through monitoring compliance with Section 25 prohibition on solely automated decisions (Act p.15). Investigating complaints about automated decision-making violations via Section 44 powers (pp.26-27). Issuing compliance orders requiring safeguards for AI systems under Section 45 (pp.27-28). Registering controllers using automated processing per Registration Regulations 2025. Imposing penalties up to K50M/2% turnover for violations (Section 43 p.25). Complaint system at dpa.mw enables individuals to challenge automated decisions lacking human review. Authority ensures AI systems processing personal data maintain meaningful human control through regulatory oversight.
init1_name2: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) System for Automated Processing
init1_justif2: DPIA System requires pre-deployment assessment under Section 30(2)(a) for automated processing (Act p.17). Section 30(3) mandates risk evaluation and safeguards (p.17). Section 30(4) requires Authority consultation for high-risk systems (p.18). Ensures human evaluation before AI deployment ongoing monitoring and regulatory oversight. Controllers must demonstrate human oversight measures preventing purely automated decision-making.
init1_name3: MACRA Data Protection Stakeholder Engagement and Training Program
init1_justif3: Government institution workshops educating MDAs on Section 25 automated decision-making prohibitions (Act p.15). Stakeholder training on DPIA requirements under Section 30 for automated processing (p.17). Public awareness campaigns on data subjects' rights to challenge automated decisions under Section 44 (pp.26-27). Registration guidance teaches controllers about safeguards required by Section 25(3) when automation is permitted (p.16). Training covers individual rights compliance obligations and human control mechanisms. Educates the public sector on preventing solely automated decisions implementing human review processes and ensuring meaningful human oversight in AI/automated systems processing personal data.

init1_justif1: The MACRA Regulatory Sandbox enables innovators to test AI solutions under regulatory oversight requiring compliance with risk management security and consumer safety standards. The Sandbox sets safety benchmarks for deployments helping identify and mitigate risks before full market entry.
init1_name2: Reserve Bank of Malawi AI-powered Cybersecurity System
init1_justif2: The Reserve Bank of Malawi’s AI-powered cybersecurity system is designed to counter a new generation of AI-driven cyberattacks targeting the country’s financial sector where attackers already use AI to automate reconnaissance social engineering and intrusion attempts against banks and payment systems. By deploying an AI-based tool (Darktrace) that learns normal network behaviour and autonomously detects and responds to anomalous AI-enhanced attack patterns in real time the initiative directly addresses AI-powered cyber risks rather than introducing new ones thereby helping to ensure that AI systems and tools operate safely and reliably and do not exacerbate existing harms.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The Mongolian AI assessment report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP. It highlights that while Mongolia has taken steps to improve digital literacy a broader approach is needed to equip citizens with AI-related critical thinking ethical considerations and data literacy. Specifically on page 37 Pillar 1. 2 Skills report recommends to expanding initiatives such as the E-Mongolia Academy and integrating AI literacy into public education and professional development programs those can ensure that both individuals and government officials are better prepared for AI adoption. The report contributes to knowledge generation on AI literacy by identifying policy gaps and proposing future actions.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: On page 66. Pillar 3. 4 Transparency addressed the issue as follows 'Robust AI governance frameworks should also incorporate effective mechanisms that empower individuals adversely affected by AI decisions to challenge and seek redress for such outcomes'. which basically means that AI frameworks should allow people to challenge and ask remedy for AI errors and harms. The assessment document is initiated and requested by the Ministry of Digital Development and Communications and assessment is conducted and funded by UNDP.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: Page 34 Pillar 1 Infrastructure First recommendation says as follows: Promote the Development of Energy-Efficient Data Centers: As Mongolia advances towards the widespread adoption of AI the demand for reliable scalable and sustainable data infrastructure isexpected to increase substantially. To address this growing need it is critical for the government to prioritize the development of energy-efficient data centers. Mongolia’s cold climate and abundant renewable energy resources present a strategic opportunity to establish cost effective environmentally sustainable data infrastructure. These natural advantages position the country as a competitive location for green data centers that can underpin long term digital transformation.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP assessed current state of Mongolian AI ecosystem and its readiness for further development in many areas of responsible AI. In relation to the Fairness and Non-discrimination dimension report explicitly recommended establishing an AI Ethics Committee that can provide oversight on risk assessment privacy protection and bias mitigation in context of AI governance in Enhance AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks section Pillar 2 1 Technology on page 48. Also report recommended to reduce existing biases in relation to population including gender age ethnicity disability and other important factors in Pillar 3 2 Inclusivity section on page 62.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The research 'Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment of Mongolia' was initiated by Ministry of Digital development Innovation and Communications and funded and conducted by UNDP. Research revealed that there is a notable gender gap in digital competencies—favoring women. Women scored 10.9% higher than men across key digital skills which presents opportunities for Mongolia fostering a more gender inclusive AI workforce and ensuring women’s participation in AI development and deployment. Report recommended reducing economic social gender equality in the context of AI and promoting gender equality through establishing AI committees with representatives from gender equality advocates in Pillar 3.2 Inclusivity section on page 62.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: On Page 58 Pillar 3 Accountability addressed human oversight as follows: Furthermore it is required that digital systems which collect process and use data without human input should incorporate a technological solution for decisions made by algorithms to be able to be reviewed reprocessed and reviewed and confirmed by a person.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: In relation to AI Impact assessment mentioned 12 times in the document. The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP discusses the need to establish AI risk categorization frameworks and algorithmic impact assessment tools at the government level. It serves as a knowledge-generation initiative that identifies policy gaps and provides recommendations for integrating impact assessment practices into Mongolia’s AI governance. For example on page 28 Pillar 3 Ethical AI 3. Safety. document mentioned 'establish an official AI ... algorithmic impact assessment tools at the government level'.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP notes that while Mongolia has a strong commitment to e-governance and data protection it lacks mechanisms for algorithmic transparency. In order to improve transparency accountability and public trust in AI-driven decision-making process accountability section (p.59 Pillar 3 1) recommends enhancing transparency by establishing a public-sector AI Algorithm Registry to document and disclose key information about government-used AI systems. Report further notes that opportunities for Mongolia to enhance documentation and disclosure of AI use in the public sector remain (p. 29) and although there are strong data protection laws AI-specific guidelines need improvement (p. 41).

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP the assessment report provides a foundational understanding of the current state of Mongolia’s AI ecosystem and constitutes an important initial step toward identifying its strengths existing gaps and opportunities for inclusive and sustainable development. The report recommended that Mongolia should establish a Government AI Procurement Framework with standardized guidelines to enhance transparency and efficiency in Pillar 2 1 Technology on Page 48. Also report highlighted lack of adequate training programs on AI procurement among public servants and introduction of clear and standardized guidelines for AI procurement oversight and performance evaluation on page 70.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP the report revealed that there is significant skill gaps among civil servants both technical and non-technical as well as a lack of adequate training programs on AI procurement ethics and governance. In addition report explicitly addressed need of establishing and expanding formal training programs to build necessary knowledge and skills for AI adoption among civil servants. Moreover report provided recommendations for strengthening AI Governance Skills Across the Public Sector building Technical AI Capacity Within Government Institutions and expanding Digital Literacy and IT Skills Among Civil Servants in Pillar 2 3 skills section on page 53 in the report.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The AI landscape assessment of Mongolia report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP discusses that there is currently no comprehensive policy addressing technical AI skill development in Mongolia. However report recommended to establish national AI skills framework for AI workforce training partnerships with the private sector and incentives for upskilling. On Page 53 Pillar 2 3 Skills section addresses importance of strengthening AI Governance Skills Across the Public Sector building Technical AI Capacity Within Government Institutions strengthening AI Recruitment and Talent Retention Strategies expanding Digital Literacy and IT Skills Among Civil Servants and leveraging Online and International Training.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP discusses that while Mongolia has existing regulations provide a foundation for assessing digital systems and ensuring data protection there are opportunities to establish an official AI risk categorization framework and algorithmic impact assessment tools at the government level. The document recommended to enhance AI safety and security in several sections specifically on page 65 Pillar 3. 3 Safety as follows: 'AI safety governance and risk assessment should go beyond a narrow focus on cybersecurity to encompass a broader range of potential harms that may affect individuals communities and society as a whole' and 'should focus on data privacy security' etc.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment of Mongolia
init1_justif1: The report developed jointly by the Government of Mongolia and UNDP assessed current state of Mongolian AI ecosystem and its readiness for further development in many areas of responsible AI. The assessment was requested and initiated by the Government of Mongolia to UNDP and UNDP was funded and conducted the assessment in 2025. The report explicitly stated that there is no comprehensive AI transparency framework in current Mongolian government policies and regulations and recommended Government of Mongolia to undertake a systematic review and enhancement of AI transparency-related regulations in near future in Pillar 3 4 Transparency section on page 67.

init1_name1: AI Landscape Assessment (AILA)
init1_justif1: In the Inclusivity chapter under Recommendations it is written that policymakers should engage Civil Society and Minority Groups in AI Policy Development: Involve representatives from civil society gender advocacy groups and minority communities in discussions about AI policy and project design. Their insights can ensure that AI policies address unique needs of underrepresented populations. Montenegro government has a role responsible for convening these stakeholders and integrating their input into national AI strategies and regulations. By leading participatory consultations and embedding diversity considerations into policy processes government ensures that AI governance reflects the cultural and linguistic diversity in Montenegro.

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: AILA Montenegro (2024) addresses fairness/non-discrimination by highlighting bias risks and recommending measures to prevent discriminatory AI. It notes AI readiness must address risks like bias (Background note page 6). Under AI ethics it recommends a national ethics/accountability framework with standards focusing on fairness (Pillar 3 - Accountability pages 36–37) and Inclusivity framework to reduce inequalities promote representative data and bias mitigation and require gender and diversity audits of AI systems (Pillar 3 - Inclusivity pages 38–39). Strategic recommendations reiterate ethical AI standards emphasizing fairness and inclusive AI practices (page 45).

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024) conducted by the Ministry of Public Administration and UNDP represents the first systematic observation and baseline assessment of AI implementation in Montenegro. Gender equality is addressed in the Inclusivity section. Page 38 highlights the need to reduce inequalities across socioeconomic and gender lines. Recommendations include mandating gender and diversity audits for AI systems incorporating gender impact metrics in AI evaluations and engaging gender advocacy groups in AI policy development (pages 38 and 39).

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024) conducted by the Ministry of Public Administration and UNDP represents the first systematic baseline assessment of AI implementation in Montenegro. The report highlights the importance of accountability transparency and citizen oversight in AI governance. It recommends the establishment of a national AI coordination or oversight bodyand mechanisms enabling individuals to challenge AI-driven decisions in order to ensure meaningful human oversight and responsibility in AI use (Ethical AI and Governance sections pages 36–41).

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) addresses impact assessments indirectly by evaluating Montenegro’s readiness to manage AI risks. While it does not mandate formal ex-ante or post-impact assessments the report identifies the absence of systematic AI risk and impact assessment mechanisms as a governance gap. It recommends developing structured AI safety and accountability frameworks including regular risk assessments and oversight mechanisms for future AI deployments (Pillar 3: Ethical AI - Accountability (pages 36-37) and Safety (pages 40–41).

init1_name2: AI AGE - AI Supported Identification of Novel Non-invasive Biomarkers of Aging
init1_drive2: The evidence does not consist of a document.
init1_justif2: The AI-AGE project proposes the use of machine learning algorithms and evaluation of AI tools to train and create prediction models to identify novel non-invasive biomarkers of aging and increased risk for development of age-related conditions. AI-AGE project is implemented through partnership between the Faculty for Information Systems and Technologies (UDG) and Faculty of Medicine (UoM). The project is funded by the Ministry of Education Science and Innovation. Through research activities training events and collaboration with Montenegrin institutions the project builds technical capacity and knowledge that can later support evidence-based policymaking and research in the public sector particulary in healthcare and education.
init1_name3: The National conference AI in the function of innovation and services
init1_justif3: The National Conference “AI in the Function of Innovation and Services” was held on 22 November 2024 at the Science and Technology Park Montenegro in Podgorica organised by the Association of Managers of Montenegro (AMM). The event gathered representatives of the public sector the technology community and the business sector focusing on the potential of artificial intelligence to improve innovation public administration and services. The panel discussion featured Sava Laketic (Director of the Tax Administration) and MinasTrubljanin (General Director in the Ministry of Finance) who discussed digitalisation tax compliance and the possibility of using AI for real-time monitoring of budget spending.

init1_name1: NCC Montenegro - The National Competence Centre for High-Performance Computing
init1_drive1: Evidence does not consist of a document.
init1_justif1: The National Competence Centre (NCC) Montenegro established in 2021 under the EuroCC project and coordinated by UDG in cooperation with Ministry of Education Science and Innovation provides continuous training workshops and mentoring focused on artificial intelligence data analytics and high-performance computing (HPC). Through cooperation with academia industry and public institutions the NCC enhances digital competencies reskills professionals for AI-related fields and supports workforce adaptation to AI-driven technologies in Montenegro. The NCC serves as a link between national stakeholders and European HPC resources helping businesses researchers and policymakers utilize the power of advanced computing technologies.
init1_name2: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif2: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (UNDP 2024) analyses the country’s readiness for AI adoption. The AILA initiative addresses reskilling and upskilling by identifying significant AI-related skills gaps and recommending targeted capacity building. It highlights low technical and non-technical AI skills within government (Pillar 1 – Skills pp. 24–25) and calls for comprehensive AI training programmes. As an enabler it proposes national AI and data skills initiatives digital competency training and rural AI skills centres encourage upskilling through online and remote Learning (Pillar 2 – Skills pp. 28–29) ensuring workforce readiness for AI-driven change.

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro addresses safety and security in the context of AI under Pillar 3: Ethical AI specifically the Safety chapter. The report identifies that Montenegro is at an early stage in managing AI-related risks and highlights the need to prevent malicious or unintended use of AI systems and reduce security and ethical risks (Pillar 3 – Safety pages 40–41). It recommends establishing a national AI safety framework adopting risk assessment and mitigation measures strengthening cybersecurity and aligning AI safety standards with international best practices to ensure secure and responsible AI deployment (Recommendations pages 40–41).

init1_name1: The AI Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024)
init1_justif1: The Artificial Intelligence Landscape Assessment (AILA) Montenegro (2024) addresses transparency and explainability in a dedicated chapter Transparency (pages 42 and 43). The report notes that transparency and explainability of AI systems are currently limited and not systematically ensured particularly in the public sector. It recommends to develop an AI Transparency Framework strengthening transparency by clearly informing users and citizens when AI systems are used improving the explainability of AI-supported decisions and defining responsibilities and procedures that allow individuals to understand monitor and challenge decisions influenced by AI.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: The General Directorate for Digital Transition
init1_name1: An Engineering School Dedicated to Digital Transition and AI
init1_justif1: The Minister of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform signed an agreement to establish the Higher School of Engineering in Digital Transition Digital Technology and Artificial Intelligence as well as the development of specialised training programs in these fields. This initiative reflects the desire to bring training programs closer to real-world needs aligning academic pathways with the needs of local areas and the priorities of national economic and technological development as outlined by the Ministry's website.

imp1_body: n/a
init1_name1: Signing of an agreement for the regulation of ethical and technological uses
init1_justif1: The National Commission for the Protection of Personal Data (CNDP) the Commission for the Right of Access to Information (CDAI) and the Rabat branch of the Economic Military School (EGE) signed an agreement in the field of business intelligence and the regulation of ethical and technological uses. The president of the CNDP  Mr Seghrouchni stated that this agreement aims to encourage and explain the risks of unethical use of technologies particularly with regard to 'deepfakes' and 'fake news.' This aligns with the framework's last two recommendations supporting the cross-sectoral collaboration to enhance research in this scope and raise awareness.

imp1_body: No there is no implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation.
init1_name1: The National Control Commission for the Protection of Personal Data (CNDP) consults experts and institutions to regulate AI
init1_justif1: The National Commission for Personal Data Protection (CNDP) has initiated a consultation regarding AI and personal data protection. The goal is to ensure that citizens have access to remedies in cases of automatic decision-making. The CNDP has begun deliberations on AI data processing and has opened this consultation to experts and institutions for their input.

init1_name1: Human Rights in Morocco and the Challenges of Emerging Issues
init1_justif1: The National Council of Human Rights emphasised the importance of creating a national legal framework to regulate the use of AI in line with a human rights during a workshop held on December 18 2024. They also highlighted the necessity of anticipating the risks associated with certain digital applications and their potential negative impact particularly on children and vulnerable individuals. The council underscored the need to achieve a balance between technological advancement and the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms.

init1_name1: Convention with Current AI
init1_justif1: On the sidelines of the first National Conference on Artificial Intelligence held in Rabat on July 1 and 2 2025 the Minister of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform signed a cooperation agreement with Mr. Martin Tisné 'Current AI' on the technological development of artificial intelligence in the public interest. This agreement aims to promote ethical secure and inclusive artificial intelligence by supporting pilot projects in high-impact areas such as health linguistic diversity and governance.
init1_name2: The 28th edition of the Fez Festival of World Spiritual Music
init1_justif2: The Minister of Digital Transition and Administration Reform participated in the Fez Forum in the dialogue session entitled: 'What can artificial intelligence offer to culture and heritage?' where she highlighted the vast potential offered by AI to monitor in real time the state of conservation of monuments archaeological sites and historical structures thanks to advanced and precise tools. She also emphasised that AI helps overcome linguistic and sensory barriers thanks to neural translation speech synthesis techniques and sign language recognition which ensures a wider dissemination of culture among various segments of society. She added that AI constitutes a strategic lever for promoting Amazigh heritage.
init1_name3: National Platform for Responsible AI
init1_justif3: The Ministry of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform and the National Commission for the Control of Personal Data Protection have concluded a partnership for the development of a national platform for responsible AI and a platform framework based on an LLM. The agreement concerns the establishment of a responsible AI framework that integrates the design of a national platform framework based on an LLM developed with national specificities taking into consideration the Moroccan language and culture the national legal framework and sovereign digital identity. This aligns with one of the strategic goals established at the National Conference on AI which aims to leverage AI to preserve the national cultural and linguistic heritage.

init1_name1: National Conference on AI Summary Report
init1_type1: Commissioning concept notes white papers or outlines that serve as inputs for drafting.
init1_justif1: The summary report of the National Conference on AI recapitulates the first roadmap for a National AI Strategy outlining and validating the major strategic orientations in this regard (p.7). One of the proposals aims to pinpoint strategic locations for the establishment of data centres linked to renewable energy production specifically solar and wind with a focus on southern Morocco to enhance performance sustainability and energy independence (p.18). Additionally one of the pillars for action supports the responsible investment in essential and sovereign digital infrastructures (p. 31).
init1_name2: Green Cloud Morocco
init1_justif2: Several agreements were signed during a ceremony held on the sidelines of the First National Conference on AI. One of the agreements was initialled by the Ministry of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform and the Founder and Secretary General of the Digital Cooperation Organization (DCO). It includes the Green Cloud Morocco initiative which will position Morocco as a regional hub for sustainable digital infrastructures. This project includes the construction of three AI factories including a green data centre enabling CO2 emissions and energy costs to be reduced by up to 70%. (Paragraphs 17-21)

init1_name1: National Conference on AI Summary Report
init1_type1: Commissioning concept notes white papers or outlines that serve as inputs for drafting.
init1_justif1: The National Conference on Artificial Intelligence outlined and validated the major strategic orientations forming the foundation of Morocco's future National AI roadmap (p.7). The summary report of the National Conference on AI recapitulates the first discussions and recommendations for a National AI Strategy. One of the strategic orientations is to produce ethical guidelines to ensure that AI prioritises fairness transparency and accountability and addresses potential risks such as discrimination and job loss p.44.
init1_name2: Convention with Current AI
init1_justif2: On the sidelines of the first National Conference on Artificial Intelligence held in Rabat on July 1 and 2 2025 the Minister of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform signed a cooperation agreement with Mr. Martin Tisné 'Current AI' on the technological development of artificial intelligence in the public interest. This agreement aims to promote ethical secure and inclusive artificial intelligence by supporting pilot projects in high-impact areas such as health linguistic diversity and governance. This will help ensure an inclusive and fair development of AI in the Kingdom.

init1_name1: Women in Tech Salon Connecting Women
init1_justif1: One of the projects established by the Ministry of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform to strengthen gender equality in AI is the 'Women in Tech Salon' training program. This program was launched by the Ministry in collaboration with a private entity with the objective of strengthening the skills of female civil servants in new information and communication technologies such as AI IoT Cloud and the metaverse. This aligns with the strategy's objective of bridging the digital divide and promoting digital inclusion for women through upskilling measures.

init1_name1: The 3rd Parliamentary Economic Forum
init1_justif1: On the sidelines of the 3rd Parliamentary Economic Forum in Marrakech the Minister Delegate for Digital Transition and Administrative Reform stressed the urgency of responsible deployment of AI based on principles of ethics transparency and trust. The minister called for the establishment of a specific legal framework. This should clarify responsibilities impose technical audits guarantee the transparency of algorithms and protect privacy. She emphasised that a draft framework law is already in preparation along with the creation of a national directorate dedicated to AI. Thus technical audits will enable human oversight when evaluating and monitoring AI technologies. Transparency requirements will also facilitate oversight.

init1_name1: Training of judicial staff in digitalization and AI
init1_justif1: A partnership agreement was signed between the Ministries of Justice and Higher Education Scientific Research and Innovation focusing on training Justice Ministry staff in digitalization and AI. The Minister of Higher Education emphasized the importance of strengthening the professional and communication skills of Justice Ministry staff to address the challenges posed by AI and digitalization and to confront the growing constraints. He noted in a similar statement that this agreement will pave the way for further partnerships with universities to ensure the ongoing training of all Justice Ministry personnel and provide new training programs to meet the major challenges ahead.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
init1_name1: The National AI Conference
init1_justif1: The first National Conference on Artificial Intelligence engaged multiple stakeholders in drafting the AI roadmap in Morocco. Ministers emphasized that training public and private sector stakeholders in AI is essential to fully harnessing its potential and making it a driver of sustainable growth. They highlighted AI's role in modernizing education enhancing economic competitiveness transforming the labor market and optimizing energy management. The presentations also stressed the need for a suitable legal framework the development of local skills and leveraging Morocco's strengths-digital connectivity green energy and human capital-to establish it as a regional technology hub.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: The General Directorate for Digital Transition
init1_name1: National Platform for Responsible AI
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform and the National Commission for the Control of Personal Data Protection have concluded a partnership for the development of a national platform for responsible AI and a platform framework based on an LLM. The objective is to provide citizens businesses and administrations with generative and conversational AI tools that are secure efficient and respectful of fundamental rights. This aligns with the strategic goal of establishing robust infrastructure for AI operation enabling both AI security and efficiency.

init1_name1: Convention with Current AI
init1_justif1: On the sidelines of the first National Conference on AI the Minister of Digital Transition and Administrative Reform signed a cooperation agreement with Current AI to develop AI in the public interest. This agreement aims to promote ethical secure and inclusive artificial intelligence by supporting pilot projects in high-impact areas such as health linguistic diversity and governance. It aims to strengthen international cooperation around transparent responsible and beneficial AI particularly emphasising Moroccan engagement in linguistic diversity health and accountability (p. 24). The first National Conference on AI built the strategic axes of the national AI strategy(p.7) in alignment with the recommendations of the framework.

init1_name1: INTIC Leads Discussion on AI in Mozambican Education During Pedagogical Training Session
init1_justif1: This initiative foused on training educators on the role of AI in teaching and learning. During the Matola Pedagogical Training Session INTIC officials introduced teachers to AI concepts ethical considerations risks and strategies for responsible integration in classrooms. The programme strengthened digital pedagogy and aimed to build teacher capacities to understand and guide AI use in schools.

init1_name1: III Seminar on CybercrimeCombating Organized Crime in the Era of AI
init1_justif1: The III Seminar on Cybercrime held by the Office of the Attorney General (PGR) with participation from INTIC and UNODC aimed to strengthen institutional capacity to prevent and combat AI-enabled cybercrime. The event addressed challenges such as AI-driven fraud organized cybercrime and threats to children and critical infrastructure. It promoted ethical AI use in the justice system enhanced interagency collaboration and supported the development of digital skills legal frameworks and technological solutions to mitigate AI-facilitated misinformation and violence.
init1_name2: Safer Internet Month Understand How Scammers Use AI Such as Voice Cloning and Deepfakes to Commit Fraud
init1_justif2: During Safer Internet Month the National Institute of Information and Communication Technologies (INTIC) published an awareness article explaining how AI is used in online fraud including voice cloning and deepfakes. The initiative notes that “techniques such as voice cloning and deepfakes have become common among criminals” enabling 'misinformation manipulation and financial harm'. It provides guidance on verifying content authenticity identifying phishing and investment scams and avoiding manipulated media. The initiative addresses AI-facilitated misinformation through public education and digital literacy.

init1_name1: 3rd Edition of the Lusophone Internet Governance Forum Panel 2 Linguistic and Cultural Diversity on the Internet Challenges and Opportunities for the Portuguese Language in the Digital Environment
init1_justif1: The 3rd Lusophone Internet Governance Forum (between 22-23 of September) organized by Mozambique’s National Institute of ICT (INTIC) on 22 September 2025 in Maputo featured a panel on “Linguistic and Cultural Diversity on the Internet.” The session addressed the role of Portuguese the integration of local varieties and the lack of AI and digital tools for African Portuguese indigenous and local languages.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

init1_name1: AI Challenges and Opportunities for Mozambique
init1_justif1: Artificial Intelligence: Challenges and Opportunities for Mozambique addresses AI reskilling and upskilling through a structured workforce development approach in Sections 7.3–7.8 (p.108–114). Section 7.4.1 (p.110) emphasises continuous professional development and postgraduate AI training including the UEM Master’s in AI. Sections 7.5 and 7.6 (p.112–113) propose a national human resources reference framework and map technical non-technical and transversal AI skills. Section 7.7 (p.113–114) promotes formal non-formal and continuous training pathways including bootcamps and certifications while Section 7.8 (p.114) highlights public–private partnerships internships and training programmes to support labour market insertion.

init1_name1: AI Challenges and Opportunities for Mozambique
init1_justif1: The INTIC Book Artificial Intelligence: Challenges and Opportunities for Mozambique addresses AI safety and security. Section 3.4 (Challenges of Artificial Intelligence in Mozambique) point 10 (Safety and Data Security) notes that AI in corporate and everyday applications poses significant safety and data security risks that must be managed to ensure system reliability. Cybercriminals are actively seeking to exploit AI. Section 4 emphasizes that safe AI adoption requires infrastructure human capacity and regulations. Section 11 stresses continuous updates and that safety must be considered throughout the AI project lifecycle from planning to deployment and maintenance.

init1_name1: International Conference on Applied Research in Education
init1_justif1: At the International Conference on Applied Research in Education 2025 Myanmar’s Ministry of Education highlighted the integration of artificial intelligence and language resources in education and research alongside broader themes of science and technology for sustainable development. The event emphasized the need to build future generations’ capacity through AI literacy digital education and research-driven innovation to advance national and regional progress.
init1_name2: AI training course under the Mekong-Korea Cooperation Project at the Industrial Technology Training Institute in Yangon
init1_justif2: Myanmar is listed as a participating country in the Mekong–Republic of Korea Cooperation Fund (MKCF) projects for the period 2013–2025 (see p.13 of From the Mekong–Republic of Korea Cooperation Fund (MKCF) Projects document). Within this framework on 11 November 2025 the Ministry of Science and Technology launched an AI training course under the Mekong–Republic of Korea Cooperation Project at the Industrial Technology Training Institute in Yangon.

init1_name1: AI (AI) Technology Training Course
init1_justif1: Across the five initiatives (2024–2025) government-run AI trainingsfocused on basic AI concepts history types of AI machine learninggenerative AI practical applications ethics and the use of AI tools inpublic administration. They aimed to build AI literacy countermisinformation and deepfakes and improve digital skills among civilservants in regions including Mandalay Ayeyarwady Lashio Yangonand Nay Pyi Taw.

init1_justif1: The capacity development program was organized by the National Assembly (Upper House) for its members to enhance knowledge on digital technologies and get acquainted with 'AI cybersecurity and digital literacy'. 32 participants including 17 members(parliamentarians) of the Assembly and other officials took part in this training.

imp1_body_name: 1 The Ministry of Education Culture and Science 2 SLO (National Center of Expertise for Curriculum Development)
init1_name1: AI Impact Game
init1_justif1: The AI Impact Game supports AI Literacy by enabling educators to experience discuss and reflect on the impact of generative AI in schools building awareness of opportunities and risks and stimulating critical thinking about policies and responsible use in education. Participants explore future scenarios from different roles which helps them understand how AI could affect teaching learning and school organisation. It does this through workshops which is supported by downloadable documents to let schools run this themselves.
init1_name2: Digital Literacy - Illustrations of the core objectives
init1_justif2: The SLO (National Center of Expertise for Curriculum) has created this illustration document to provide concrete educational examples for translating the framework into classroom practice . It addresses this through :1. Foundational Knowledge: Explaining that AI learns from human-provided data and that algorithms are human-made (Section 22D p.1)2. Technical Understanding: Describing sensors Large Language Models (LLMs) and training methods like supervised learning (Section 21D p. 4).3. Practical Skills: Training AI systems to recognise images or using LLMs to structure texts (Section 21D p.4).4. Critical Evaluation: Discussing bias in datasets the ethics of AI in healthcare and the need for AI regulations (Sections 21D & 23C p.4)

imp1_body_name: Autoriteit Consument & Markt and Dutch Data Protection Authority
init1_name1: BENEDMO (Belgium-Netherlands Digital Media Observatory)
init1_justif1: BENEDMO identifies and analyses harmful disinformation across the the flemish and dutch regions and develops cross-border multidisciplinary tools research and fact-checking to counter it. It builds an expert network advances early detection supports policy analysis and strengthens media literacy through targeted educational programs and public initiatives.It has received supplementary grants from the Dutch government to strengthen the fact-checking network in the Netherlands( as mentioned in the framework Page 7). Publications on the provided link show that the project is still ongoing.
init1_name2: isdatechtzonl
init1_justif2: IsDatEchtZo addresses AI-facilitated misinformation by explaining generative-AI deepfakes and chatbots offering practical detection tips checklists and educational materials to spot AI images/videos and misleading chatbot outputs. The project started in 2021 but is still ongoing: https://www.isdatechtzo.nl/wat-is-nepnieuws/ai-en-desinformatie/je-gelooft-je-eigen-ogen-toch-wel/. It is fully funded by BZK (Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations). see: https://www.isdatechtzo.nl/over-ons/
init1_name3: Dealing with Misinformation for Local Authorities
init1_justif3: The evidence is a guidance developed by the Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations addressing AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by providing local officials with legal and practical frameworks to identify and respond to synthetic threats. It explicitly defines deepfakes (audio/video) and generative AI as emerging drivers of misinformation that can confuse public debate. It offers a stepwise response plan ('Stappenplan') including platform reporting mechanisms to remove harmful AI content and legal steps for officials facing intimidation (Chapter 3 & 4).

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
init1_name1: Directorate Coordination Algorithms (DCA)
init1_justif1: The DCA strengthens access to redress by coordinating oversight of algorithmic systems across Dutch regulators ensuring complaints are effectively routed to competent authorities. It supports existing complaint desks (“loketten”) and clarifies citizens’ rights through guidance and outreach making it easier for individuals to report harms and obtain corrective measures. The DCA also facilitates enforcement actions such as warnings corrective orders or sanctions ensuring remedies are not only available but actionable. Since it is part of the Dutch Data Protection Authority(AP) grievances can be filed through AP.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations (BZK) plays a coordinating role monitoring progress and adjusting policy changes
init1_name1: Strategy for Childrens Rights Online
init1_justif1: The 'Strategie kinderrechten online' or the 'Children’s Rights Online Strategy' informs the House of Representatives about the government's commitment to a safe and healthy digital living environment for children and young people. On page 6 Section 3 (“Education as a foundation for digital resilience”) it calls for “clear frameworks for the responsible use of artificial intelligence (AI) in education with due regard for autonomy privacy and equal opportunities.” The same section outlines a curriculum revision to include “digital systems media security privacy data and AI. Page 3 (Section 1) highlights risks from “harmful algorithms and addictive design techniques” which implicitly includes AI algorithms.
init1_name2: Child Rights Impact Assessment
init1_justif2: It addresses children’s rights in the context of AI by offering a structured framework to identify assess and mitigate digital service risks to children’s well-being and rights. The CRIA Manual discusses AI in Section 2.2 (p. 9) as a technology that may require a (FRAIA) alongside the CRIA when digital services use algorithmic or AI systems. It situates AI within risk-based design considerations. Section 3.5 (pp. 20–21) details risks from AI-driven systems algorithms smart toys behavioural design biometrics like profiling and manipulation. The Ministry of BZK has applied CRIA to Instagram Snapchat and TikTok https://www.rijksoverheid.nl/documenten/kamerstukken/2025/09/04/kamerbrief-bij-kinderrechten-impact-assessments
init1_name3: Research report on the implementation of KIA
init1_justif3: The report addresses children’s rights in the context of AI by evaluating how digital services (like TikTok Instagram and Snapchat) affect children’s well-being and fundamental rights. It serves as a self-assessment tool to help platforms identify discuss and mitigate AI-related risks such as algorithmic profiling recommender systems and dark patterns that can influence children’s autonomy privacy and safety. For the benefit of children the report explains AI in simpler terms 'Social media apps use smart computers that work with different types of technologies. Think of AI or algorithms. These smart computers help the app show you the content in your feed.' Reference: Chapter 1.2–1.3 (pp. 7–9) and Chapter 3 (pp. 17–20)

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
imp1_body_name: Algorithms Coordination Directorate (DCA) and Government AI Validation Team (Rijks Al-validatieteam)
init1_name1: GPT-NL
init1_justif1: The Netherlands will develop its own language model called GPT-NL. The model is being developed by research organization TNO the Netherlands Forensic Institute (NFI) and the ICT cooperative SURF under the guidance of The Ministry of Economic Affairs and Climate Policy (EZK). GPT-NL promotes cultural and linguistic diversity by developing a Dutch-language large model grounded in local data culture and societal values. It addresses English dominance in AI by training on high-quality Dutch datasets from consenting providers and engaging public institutions to ensure representativeness.
init1_name2: ALT-EDIC
init1_justif2: The Alliance for Language Technologies-EDIC preserves cultural & linguistic diversity by federating language and multimodal resources for all EU national and regional languages (including low-resource languages) funding model development that supports multilingual/multimodal capabilities building bias-aware evaluation/certification and running a cultural outreach programme to involve data-producers and public administrations. The Dutch government has co-financed this programme and participates as a full member.

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
imp1_body_name: Algorithms Coordination Directorate (DCA) and Government AI Validation Team (Rijks Al-validatieteam)
init1_name1: ELSA Lab for Technical Industry
init1_justif1: The AiNed ELSA Labs programme explicitly lists energy & sustainability as one of its core application domains. The ELSA Lab for Technical Industry emphasises “contributions to global sustainability goals” through responsible AI deployment in industry.  This shapes the focus on reducing environmental footprint and aligning AI use with sustainable development.
init1_name2: Generative AI and Sustainability
init1_justif2: This report directly addresses the environmental impact of Generative AI (GenAl) by investigating its 'sustainability costs'—specifically energy consumption CO2 emissions and water consumption (p.5-7). It then provides mitigation recommendations for government organizations and the Ministry (BZK) . Key actions include creating a sustainable digitalization strategy monitoring energy use and choosing GenAl models sustainably .(Pg-6)

init1_name1: Algorithm Testing Framework
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Netherlands Court of Audit (NCA) addresses fairness and non-discrimination by acting as an independent watchdog. Its initiative audits government algorithms specifically for risks of bias and discrimination holding the government accountable. Through the Algorithm Testing Framework first launched in 2021 it published a report 'Understanding algorithms' followed by a 2022 audit which investigated 9 government algorithms and found 6 failed to meet basic requirements exposing 'risks of bias'. In 2024 the updated framework assessed three algorithms for objectivity security and IT management revealing deficiencies in the Royal Netherlands Marechaussee and GDPR non-compliance in Rijkswaterstaat although no bias was detected.
init1_name2: Non-discrimination in algorithms and data
init1_justif2: The RADIO (IT-AcademieOverheid) AI programme addresses fairness/non-discrimination by providing targeted learning and capacity-building. It specifically has an 'E-learning – non-discriminatie in algoritmes” module microlearnings on the ethical side of AI webinars and workshops that teach how algorithmic bias arises and how to prevent it plus it also has the Nationale AI-course  covering privacy reliability and fairness concerns.
init1_name3: DUOs approach to fraud found to be discriminatory and illegal
init1_justif3: The Dutch Education Executive Agency DUO used an algorithm to check whether students were abusing the grant for students living away from home. This was found to be discriminatory and therefore unlawful by the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) after an investigation. The algorithm used a risk profile based on 'Type of education'  'distance'  and 'age'. These factors were established based on 'experience and common sense' with no objective justification.This practice led to indirect discrimination against students with a non-European migration background. DUO was asked to suspend the use of the algorithm and the government announced refunds/compensation for affected students.

init1_name1: Green and Digital Jobs Action Plan
init1_justif1: In the section on 'Pillar 1: Increasing intake in beta-technical education' the letter states that a key focus of the actions is on 'underrepresented groups of students' in technical subjects and career choices. The letter explicitly mentions that this includes a focus on 'more gender equality and diversity'. It is also mentioned in the government website under the Ministry of Education Culture and Science here. https://www.government.nl/topics/gender-equality/womens-labour-force-participation

init1_name1: Algorithm Register
init1_justif1: The Dutch Algorithm Register addresses human oversight by mandating transparency. It requires government bodies to publicly document their algorithms using a standard template. This template explicitly asks agencies to declare their human oversight procedures in the human_intervention field (in Dutch: 'Menselijke tussenkomst')
init1_name2: FRAIA (Fundamental Rights and Algorithms Impact Assessment)
init1_justif2: FRAIA is a tool to identify the impact of specific algorithm on fundamental rights and was piloted at 15 dutch govt org. It addresses Human Oversight and Determination in Part 3: How? specifically in Sec 3.2: The role of humans in the decision (p 51-52). It mandates defining the precise role of humans in decision-making based on algorithmic output and ensuring they are empowered to decide responsibly. It explicitly frames this control using models such as 'human in the loop' 'human on the loop' and 'human in command'. It links oversight to organisational accountability by requiring the identification of ultimate responsibility (Part 1 p18-19) and ensuring sufficient qualified staff are in place to manage and review the system (Part 3).
init1_name3: AI4Oversight Lab
init1_justif3: The AI4Oversight Lab directly addresses human oversight by designing AI as a support tool for human inspectors not a replacement. Its entire purpose is to research and build 'human-centric AI' that requires a 'meaningful interaction between computer and inspector. One of the lab's research agenda includes 'Hybrid AI' where the human (inspector) remains in the loop using their judgment and experience (determination) to make the final decision. The AI provides data-driven advice to help the inspector and the inspector's feedback improves the algorithm. This ensures the human retains control.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
imp1_body_name: European AI Office Dutch AP RDI Netherlands Institute for Human Rights
init1_name1: FRAIA (Fundamental Rights and Algorithms Impact Assessment )
init1_justif1: The FRAIA (Fundamental Rights and Algorithms Impact Assessment) structurally addresses the concept of Impact Assessments by being a multi-stage comprehensive assessment tool itself. The core of this function is embedded in Part 4: Fundamental rights (Roadmap) (Pages 66-70) which outlines a 7-step structured process to systematically evaluate and mitigate the risks posed by algorithms to fundamental rights. Furthermore FRAIA is designed as an overarching tool that integrates and complements other assessments such as the Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA).

imp1_body_name: European AI Office Dutch AP RDI Netherlands Institute for Human Rights
init1_name1: Algorithmic management insight and workplace implications
init1_justif1: This publication was produced by the Nederlandse Arbeidsinspectie (The Netherlands Labour Authority) which is a national government body that operates under the Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment and is responsible for monitoring and enforcing labour laws. The Labour Authority conducted a survey on the influence of Algorithmic Management (AM) at Dutch companies and commissioned literature reviews (by TNO). The stated aim was to gain insight into how AM affects managers and employees. The document identifies AI risks (e.g. declining autonomy discrimination) and asserts that existing labour laws like the Working Conditions Act and Works Councils Act apply mandating the inclusion of these risks in company risk assessments (Page 4 5)

imp1_body_name: The Advisory Board on Openness and Information Management (ACOI)
init1_name1: AI register
init1_justif1: The Dutch Algorithm Register addresses public disclosure by listing all government algorithms including their purpose general function and responsible agency. It operationalises transparency beyond the Woo by providing a centralised publicly accessible inventory. While not a law it implements government policy on algorithmic transparency.
init1_name2: Fundamental Rights and Algorithms Impact Assessment (FRAIA)
init1_justif2: FRAIA (fundamental rights and algorithm impact assessment) otherwise called IAMA(Impact Assessment Mensenrechten en Algoritmes) in Dutch is a discussion and decision making tool for government organisations. The tool facilitates an interdisciplinary dialogue by those responsible for the development and/or use of an algorithmic system(Page 2). It addresses disclosure by acting as the standardised 'transparency report' for high-impact algorithms. It forces agencies to document the system's inner workings before deployment creating a comprehensive public record. The FRAIA is compulsory for the Central Government when developing high-risk AI systems.(https://www.tweedekamer.nl/kamerstukken/moties/detail?id=2022Z06024&did=2022D12329)

init1_name1: AI Procurement Clause
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: This initiative addresses public procurement of AI by providing model contractual clauses that public authorities can include in procurement contracts for AI systems. The clauses operationalise EU AI Act requirements at the purchasing stage by allocating supplier obligations on risk management (Art. 2 p 3–4) data governance (Art. 3 p 4–5) technical documentation and transparency (Arts. 4–6 p 6–7) human oversight (Art. 7 p. 7) and accuracy robustness and cybersecurity (Art. 8 p. 8). They also enable audits and compliance checks by contracting authorities (Arts. 18–19 p 11–12). The preliminary remarks clarify their specific use for public sector AI procurement especially high-risk systems (p 1–2).
init1_name2: Buyer Group Market Vision and purchasing strategy
init1_justif2: A Buyer Group brings public clients together who develop a shared market vision for a specific product category and embed into procurement practice. It strengthens AI procurement via four themes. Sec 3.1 ensures Quality through lifecycle management (p.20) and legal 'Model Provisions' (p.25). Sect 3.2 enhances Cybersecurity by mandating BIO/CSIR standards (p. 29) and requesting Software Bills of Materials (p.30). Sect 3.3 promotes Data Sharing via an 'Open unless' policy (p.36) and ARBIT/GIBIT terms (p.37)  to secure ownership. Sec 3.4 addresses Ethics by requiring AI Impact Assessments prior to tendering (p.42) and registration in the Algorithm Register (p.41). The initiative is led by the Dutch AI Coalition with PIANOo supported by BZK.

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
imp1_body_name: 1 The Netherlands Digitalisation Strategy (NDS) council 2 Administrative Consultation on Digitalisation (BDO)
init1_name1: AI Factory
init1_justif1: While not specific to public servants the AI Factory contributes to public-sector skills development through the establishment of a dedicated Expertise Centre scheduled to become operational by mid-2026. As articulated under Priority 3 of the Netherlands’ Digitalisation Strategy (NDS) (pp. 10 13) the initiative is explicitly aligned with the strategic objective of training attracting and retaining digital talent within government. The Expertise Centre is designed to provide the government with low-threshold access to advanced AI infrastructure and specialised skills training. By offering a secure environment for responsible experimentation the AI Factory enables public officials to develop hands-on experience with AI systems.
init1_name2: AI Acceleration Team
init1_justif2: The AI Acceleration Team (Priority 3 of the NDS) addresses public sector skills by prioritising 'AI literacy' and the 'training and retaining of talent' (Sections: 'Framework for responsible use' and 'Strategic objectives'). Led by Larissa Zegveld the team shifts focus from policy documents to 'tangible progress' involving CIOs and directors from all government levels to ensure AI applications are practically feasible for staff (Section: 'The AI Acceleration Team'). It translates complex regulations like the AI Act into actionable procurement and deployment guidance. By fostering 'shared solutions' and active knowledge sharing the initiative builds collective expertise across the public sector to ensure AI is used responsibly.

init2_name1: Congres Rechtspraak en AI (National Rechtspraak and AI Congress)
init2_justif1: The “Jaaroverzicht 2024 AI activiteiten Rechtspraak” (2024 Overview of AI Activities in the Judiciary) is an official PDF summary of AI-related activity within the Dutch Judiciary (Rechtspraak) in 2024 which contains evidence that 'Congres Rechtspraak en AI (National 'Judiciary and AI' Congress) which had approx 400 participants aimed at building AI capability within the judiciary. Under information and training it also mentions workshops and internal learning programmes.

init1_name1: SLIM SUBSIDY
init1_justif1: The SLIM (Stimuleringsregeling Leren en Ontwikkelen in MKB-ondernemingen) subsidy supports reskilling/upskilling by funding internal learning and development initiatives that organisations design for their workforce. Though it doesn't mention AI explicitly but it funds planning and training structures that can include digital/AI-related learning. Secondary websites like https://amsterdamdataacademy.com/slim-budget/ confirm that SLIM budgets can be used for AI trainings by SMEs. This sectoral development plan by the government also covers ICT. https://www.rijksoverheid.nl/onderwerpen/hervormingen-arbeidsmarkt/meer-geschikt-personeel-en-blijven-ontwikkelen-tijdens-werk
init1_name2: Human Capital Agenda ICT
init1_justif2: The Human Capital Agenda ICT (HCA-ICT) is a national action plan led by the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Climate in the Netherlands. It addresses AI-related reskilling/upskilling by coordinating national efforts to train retrain and upskill ICT professionals including AI and data skills to close talent gaps in the digital economy. It supports career-switchers lifelong learning and employer-led training fostering workforce adaptability to technological change.
