imp1_body_name: European AI Office Dutch AP RDI Netherlands Institute for Human Rights
init1_name1: Digital Europe Programme (DEP)
init1_justif1: The initiative contributes to AI security through targeted investment in cybersecurity under the EU Digital Europe Programme (2025–2027). The programme allocates €1.7 billion to support deployment of cybersecurity technologies including innovative security technologies strengthening digital reliance and protection of critical digital infrastructure. Dedicated cybersecurity calls explicitly aim to strengthen resilience reduce strategic dependencies on non-EU technologies and improve preparedness across public authorities and industry.(https://www.rvo.nl/onderwerpen/cybersecurity-funding-portal/toelichting) Complemented by €16.2 million in national co-financing these measures support the secure operation of AI and digital systems.
init1_name2: Security Peace and Justice (VVR) working group of the Dutch AI Coalition
init1_justif2: The Security Peace and Justice (VVR) working group of the Dutch AI Coalition (NLAICoalitie) addresses AI safety and security by bringing together government industry and knowledge institutions (a 'triple helix'). This work is facilitated by Security Delta (HSD) on behalf of the Ministry of Justice and Security is concentrated in a few key areas which includes building a catalogue of AI use cases privacy-proof data sharing developing solutions for cybersecurity and ensuring decision support applications are safe and reliable.
init1_name3: ROBUST
init1_justif3: The program is an initiative of the Innovation Center for Artificial Intelligence (ICAI) and is funded by the Dutch Government. It is a large-scale public-private partnership. The program ROBUST concentrates on six core dimensions: accuracy explainability reliability repeatability resilience and safety which guide the development of algorithms designed to address objectives inspired by the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

imp1_body_name: Dutch Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens the Netherlands national Data Protection Authority RDI (Rijksinspectie Digitale Infrastructuur) Netherlands Institute for Human Rights
init1_name1: Algorithm Register
init1_justif1: The Dutch Algorithm Register addresses transparency by being a mandatory public database where government bodies must disclose what algorithms they use why (the purpose) what data is involved and what human oversight is in place. It tackles explainability by requiring a plain-language description of the algorithm's 'operation' and 'logic'. This provides 'societal explainability' making the 'application and outcome' of AI understandable to citizens who can then hold the government accountable for its use. While this is voluntary currently the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) has called for the register to be mandatory.
init1_name2: The DECIDE project - Democratising AI Empowering Citizens through Transparent Decision Making
init1_justif2: The DECIDE project (“Democratising AI: Empowering Citizens through Transparent Decision-Making”) is funded by the Dutch Research Council (NWO) an independent government-funded body under the Ministry of Education Culture and Science. The €6.8 million multidisciplinary project brings together nearly fifty partners including universities municipalities and civil-society organisations to co-develop AI systems with citizens. Instead of treating AI as a “black box” DECIDE prioritises transparency and explainability focusing on why decisions are made rather than just what the outcome is making AI more interpretable reliable and accountability.
init1_name3: Dutch DPA imposes a fine on Clearview because of illegal data collection for facial recognition
init1_justif3: The Dutch DPA fined Clearview AI €30.5M for serious GDPR violations. Clearview unlawfully processed personal and biometric data of individuals in the Netherlands without a legal basis (Articles 5 6 9 GDPR). It failed to adequately inform data subjects (Articles 12 14) and did not respond to access requests or facilitate individuals’ rights (Articles 12 15) highlighting major transparency and explainability gaps. These violations demonstrate how Clearview’s practices prevented people from understanding or controlling how their data was used.

init1_name1: GenAI in NCEAassessment FAQs
init1_justif1: The document addresses AI literacy by providing steps for schools and teachers on aspects such as acceptable use of Gen aI helping them navigate a) prevent and detect GenAI academic misconduct (see Q 3 of the doc) b) recommends students to follow school assessment policy and guidelines mentioning the GenAI tool used in assignments and explaining what the GenAI tool did/ was used for (see Q 11 of the doc) c) Show students examples of inaccuracy bias logical and stylistic problems to deter GenAI use in assessment (see Q 19 of the doc) d) Provide guidance on how to acknowledge use of GenAI (see qs 10 and 11).
init2_name1: Empowering Aotearoa An Inclusive Approach to AI Literacy in Tertiary Education
init2_justif1: The “Empowering Aotearoa: An Inclusive Approach to AI Literacy in Tertiary Education” project addresses AI literacy by co-creating resources and structured programmes to equip diverse tertiary learners and educators with critical AI knowledge ethical understanding and confidence for using AI tools in learning and assessment. It emphasizes cultural and linguistic inclusivity develops best-practice guidelines for AI integration and produces accessible materials in multiple languages and formats. The goal is to ensure equitable benefits practical skills and enhanced employability for all students with a special focus on Maori Pacific and neurodiverse learners.

init1_name1: Safeguarding children and young peoples privacy in New Zealand
init1_type1: Launching consultations or stakeholder workshops to gather input for a future draft.
init1_justif1: This report examines children and young people’s privacy in New Zealand with a focus on how well current laws regulations and guidance protect them now and into the future especially in the changing environment of children using social media & AI (Pg 3; second line). Between August- November 2023 the government sought feedback from stakeholders on these issues and published the findings in April 2024 summarising the views received. The consultations resulted in suggestions for government-led guidance on how to apply the Privacy Act and the Information Privacy Principles to help organisations and individuals understand privacy rules in specific contexts including when using technologies such as AI.

init1_drive1: NA as this is a web link and not a PDF.
init1_justif1: Under NZ Digital govt's key areas of work the Maori-Crown Digital Partnership Programme (established in August 2025) ensures cultural diversity in New Zealand’s public sector technology by embedding Maori rights values and aspirations in digital service design and delivery. It applies Te Tiriti o Waitangi principles supports Maori data governance and fosters equal Maori-Crown partnerships including in AI. This initiative aims to uplifts staff cultural capability strengthen Maori influence on emerging technologies and promote frameworks like Mana Orite - securing Maori cultural perspectives across government digital transformation.
init2_name1: Maori Data and AI guidance for business
init2_drive1: NA as it is a weblink and not a PDF document.
init2_justif1: The 'Maori Data and AI – guidance for business' guidance (although released in 2019; still holds relevance) from data.govt.nz addresses cultural diversity by outlining principles for respectful and responsible management of Maori data in AI. It urges businesses to distinguish between noa (non-sensitive) and tapu (sacred) data involve Maori in decision-making respect Maori data sovereignty and adopt culturally safe practices. The guidance encourages transparency careful use of Maori knowledge ethical data use and safeguards to minimise bias and benefit Maori communities—ensuring that Maori perspectives and values shape how AI systems incorporate and use data related to their culture.

In Section 8 namely Bias and other unfair outcomes the AIA user guide provides general guidance on bias and unfair outcomes with case studies & key considerations to mitigate the risk of bias/unfair outcomes. It also provides a brief on ways to address unwanted bias like decisions on how best to represent the training data in features interpretable by the algorithm data labelling trained and algorithms are validated before deployment. The guide is current and ongoing.

init1_name1: AI Procurement checklist
init1_justif1: The NZ AI procurement checklist is a guide published by MBIE to help organisations evaluate and engage AI suppliers. It outlines key factors to consider including supplier reputation legal compliance alignment with business and governance needs training data quality security performance monitoring and cost risks—when procuring AI systems. The checklist also recommends transparency from vendors about model data performance and support arrangements and encourages regular reviews and information sharing for responsible AI procurement and deployment.

init1_name1: AI Foundational Capability
init1_justif1: AI Foundational Capability program is a part of AI Foundations Programme. It explores what is needed to enable AI in practice including change management user-centred design and the trade-offs between customising or acquiring AI tools. This learning content has been created by the Government Chief Digital Office and is envisioned to help build capability in the Public Sector and is not intended as AI policy guidance or settings. It is meant to build the confidence and capability to assess and enable AI opportunities in a safe transparent and productive way uses consistent and well-informed approaches and reflects public service values and context and is not mandatory for public servants.

init1_name1: HAPDP Online Complaint Mechanism (Formulaire de plainte)
init1_justif1: HAPDP’s online complaint form operationalises access to redress or remedy for AI-related harms where AI involves automated processing of personal data. It lets the data subject file a complaint against an organisation and document key facts (incl. dates of access requests or when the harmful practice was discovered) (Q4 lines 40–41). It captures typical rights-violations relevant to AI decisions e.g. consent collection or use third-party sharing access response delays purpose disclosure and correction (Q5 lines 42–61). It also requires prior attempt to resolve with the organisation (Q6 lines 62–67) and asks what remedy the complainant wants HAPDP to take (Q8 line 86).

init1_justif1: The 3 Million Technical Talent (3MTT) Program addresses AI Literacy by directly delivering AI skills training to the populace.Capacity Building: It offers structured training in high-demand technical skills specifically including AI/Machine Learning for the first phase (Cohort 1 began in December 2023).Scale and Access: The program's large scale (3 million target) and hybrid model ensure that training which is a key NAIS initiative reaches a broad diverse demographic across all regions tackling the challenge of limited access to AI knowledge.This directly implements the NAIS objective to 'Equip individuals with the skills and knowledge necessary to participate actively in the AI-driven future' (Strategic Objectives).

init1_name1: NIGERIA DATA PROTECTION ACT (NDP ACT) 2023 -GENERAL APPLICATION AND IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTIVE (GAID) 2025
init1_justif1: GAID explicitly addresses automated decision-making (Art. 9) requiring data controllers to ensure the operation of human oversight mechanisms when automated systems produce decisions that affect data subjects directly operationalizing the rights to object (as per section 27 of the law). Enhanced protection obligations are established for vulnerable data subjects including the requirement for controllers to establish accessible internal procedures for handling complaints of damages arising from automated processing (Art. 6). Data subjects have the right to be informed about their rights to object to automated decisions providing practical guidance on how such disclosures should be structured (Art. 3).

imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
init1_name1: NIGERIA DATA PROTECTION ACT (NDP ACT) 2023 -GENERAL APPLICATION AND IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTIVE (GAID) 2025
init1_justif1: GAID provides technical and procedural guidance on the application of the Law's provisions including specific safeguards for the processing of children's personal data and obligations applicable to automated decision-making systems. It establishes specific safeguards for the processing of personal data of children and other vulnerable groups requiring data controllers to implement enhanced protection measures when deploying automated systems that process data of minors (Art. 6) including the mandatory assessment of risks to the rights and interests of minors before deployment. The requirement for parental consent for the processing of children's data (Section 31 Law) is extended to apply to digital services and online platforms (Art. 3).

init1_name1: N-ATLAS - Nigerias multilingual multimodal LLM
init1_justif1: N-ATLAS (Nigerian Atlas for Languages & AI at Scale) is a government-led programme (NITDA/NCAIR) building multilingual models and datasets for Nigerian languages (e.g. Hausa Yoruba Igbo). By expanding high-quality local-language data and benchmarks it reduces English-centric bias in NLP improves performance for under-represented groups and enables fairer access to AI services. Its open resources/tooling help developers test mitigate and monitor disparate performance—advancing NAIS goals on fairness non-discrimination and inclusion.
init1_name2: NCAIR AI Collective
init1_justif2: AI Collective advances Fairness & Non-discrimination by convening open meetups/workshops where practitioners learn to detect and mitigate bias (data curation model evaluation across demographics documentation/model cards). It lowers entry barriers for under-represented groups to participate in AI building promotes use of locally relevant datasets (reducing English-centric and urban bias) and shares practical guidance/tools that developers can adopt in real projects helping Nigeria’s ecosystem design more equitable AI systems.

init1_name1: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) AI Collective
init1_justif1: The NCAIR AI Collective is a government-run platform that grows Nigeria’s AI ecosystem through open calls community meetups workshops and collaboration spaces. By lowering entry barriers spotlighting participation from under-represented groups and promoting skills-building around responsible AI (fairness/bias) it supports women’s inclusion and career pathways in AI. This advances NAIS goals on inclusive human-centred AI and helps increase women’s representation in AI practice and entrepreneurship even though it’s not a women-only program.

It promotes integrating artificial intelligence (AI) machine learning (ML) and data analytics alongside security controls and points to government mechanisms like the Computer Emergency Readiness and Response Team (CERRT) and the Nigeria Data Protection Act (NDPA) 2023 as part of the security ecosystem supporting safe AI use.

In p.2–3 the curriculum’s Digital Literacy standards (IV-A.3 IV-A.7 IV-A.8; IV-B.3 IV-B.5) set learning objectives for safe organizing storing and sharing information and ethical critical use of digital contentfoundational privacy competencies for pupils; in p.14 the “Challenges related to AI” outcomes explicitly list “loss of privacy” as a risk and ask students to identify protections; in p.17 “Dangers when using AI” uses role-play scenarios (e.g. AI recommending friends) to have pupils spot risks and propose protective actions.

init1_name1: Digitalization of work and employment A threat or opportunity for employees?
init1_type1: Launching consultations or stakeholder workshops to gather input for a future draft.
init1_justif1: The initiative starts with: The application of artificial intelligence in the workplace can no longer be ignored! Then it lists the risks of the AI on workplace. The initiative is implemented by the Workers Unions and calls that they must negotiate transparency in algorithms data access/deletion rights and the 'right to disconnect' to safeguard worker interests. This initiative is part of EU Funded Project and involves Macedonian Free Trade Unions (FTU) as national partner. It has goal to modernize the labour law by adding the workers right to disconnect here also from the AI systems. On page 3-4 the initiative has produced an Action plan which is available here: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1DLjYqVoBejLRHrrkg6QXXeuvdF2JJfET/view?usp

init1_name1: AI - Framework for characterizing AI system methods and capabilities
init1_type1: Mandated bodies (commissions task forces or inter-agency working groups) to draft an AI-related framework.
init1_justif1: The Institute for Standardization of North Macedonia provides a standard framework of descriptors to support the consistent characterization of AI system methods and capabilities. The framework helps AI stakeholders describe AI systems and have a common understanding of them. This document applies to all types of organizations involved in any of the lifecycle stages of AI systems as well as to any AI stakeholder roles. It will then help the agencies and other public bodies to make specifications in procurement AI systems.

init1_justif1: The initiative led by the Ministry of Education and Science (MES) of North Macedonia allows secondary school teachers in North Macedonia to apply for advanced training on the application of artificial intelligence in teaching. If they have a basic level of knowledge of digital tools and are actively fluent in English they can apply for the 'AI in the Classroom' training which will help them incorporate artificial intelligence into all phases of teaching from lesson preparation to assessment.

init1_name1: Competence packages for digital competence in schools
init1_justif1: The Norwegian Directorate for Education and Training elaborated competence packages designed to support digital competence in Norwegian schools. These packages are aimed at teachers school leaders and school owners helping them develop the skills needed to support students’ learning and meet the requirements of the Kunnskapsløftet 2020 curriculum reform. For instance one of the packages includes basic insights into AI and its relevance to the curriculum and pedagogical practice addresses challenges and opportunities with AI including teaching practices assessment privacy information security and age-appropriate use of AI tools.It also includes discussion tasks and activities to encourage reflection among teachers and school staff.

init1_name1: AI and Democratic Elections International Experiences and National Recommendations Report by the Expert Group on AI and Elections
init1_justif1: The report is conducted by an expert group appointed by the government to assess the influence of AI deepfakes and AI-driven misinformation tools on elections and democracy. Chapter 1.5 p14 discusses genAI deepfakes dis/misinformation as a phenomena and what they entail. Chapter 3 addresses all the ways AI can be used in the context of elections. Chapter 5 assesses the current state of technology and other factors involved in elections in Norway latest changes that have already happened Lastly Chapter 6 offers conclusions and recommendations to implement to counteract the spread of deepfakes dis/misinformation and other harmful influences of AI.

init1_name1: Report on quality assurance Use of AI in health and care services
init1_justif1: The report issued by Norwegian Directorate of Health outlines the key considerations organizations must address to assess AI systems trustworthiness (p.4). Chapter 9.3 brings up incident management and how the healthcare staff and the patients can report adverse events and file complaints related to AI. 'The organization should ensure that its internal incident reporting systems are adapted to support reporting of AI-related incidents whether clinical or technical to capture all AI relevant events. Patients can also use this to report adverse events.[..]For AI systems the organization is obligated to report serious incidents that pose a risk to the supplier without delay in accordance with Article 26(5) of the AI Act.'

init1_name1: Report to the St 32 (2024 2025) Safe upbringing in a digital society
init1_justif1: The government presented the White Paper on Safe Growth in a Digital Society with government measures to provide children with a safer digital education. The government wants to be proactive and implement a clear policy that prioritizes the safety of children and young people in their daily digital lives which includes ensuring that digital services platforms and marketing are adequately regulated and safeguard children's rights (p. 69). The government will: promote children's rights online through dialogue with platform companies and the technology industry ensure transparency about algorithms on digital platforms and how provider oversight can be carried out and prioritize work on incorporating the Digital Services Act.

init1_name1: Report on large language models in Norwegian health and care services Risks and adaptations to Norwegian conditions
init1_justif1: Page 26 (Chapter 5): the report addresses the importance of adjusting AI tools and Large Language models used in healthcare to Norwegian realities maintaining the diversity of languages and dialects used. Page 29: 'If language models are to be used to process speech for example speech-to-text the language models should also master the dialects in use in Norway as well as other oral speech variations such as Scandinavian languages.' Page 28:'As with Sami language users efforts should be made to ensure that AI tools consider the cultural perspective in meetings between users from minority groups and society at large which will be in line with the Patient and User Rights Act.'

init1_name1: The footprint of Norwegian digital infrastructure 2024
init1_justif1: Report quantifies climate and environmental impacts of digital infrastructure operating in Norway in 2024.It's commissioned by the Norwegian Communications Authority(Nkom).Big part of the report evaluated the sustainability and impact of data centers. The report highlights that while the current footprint is moderate rapid growth in data center capacity and land use is expected making energy efficiency electricity sourcing and spatial planning critical for limiting future environmental impacts(p67 Conclusion). Chapter9.6 p.90: the analysis includes serverscooling systemsUPS and batteriesbackup generatorsbuildingsracks.This reflect awareness that embodied emissions(construction equipment manufacturing) matter as well as the whole.

init1_justif1: The Ombud is a government agency administratively subordinate to the Ministry of Culture. In 2024 they released a document on discrimination through algorithms and AI (linked)defining risks challenges and potential actions. While Norway has an overarching Equality and Anti-Discrimination Act it can only be vaguely applied to gender and AI. init1_name1: On the way to a super-intelligent synthetic and robot-supported everyday life? A nationwide survey on the populations encounter with AI
init1_justif1: The report carried out by Consumption Research Norway (SIFO) analyzes a nationally representative survey on the population's encounter with artificial intelligence. The data collection was carried out in March 2025 with respondents aged 15 to 80 years. Chapter 1.7 of the report discusses human agency vs. AI agency the importance of human control and oversight. Further chapters like 2.3 report on the results of the survey which demonstrates certain skepsis towards AI influence e.g. 'hyper-personalization' that potentially decreases human agency in decision- making

init1_name1: Report on quality assurance Use of AI in health and care services
init1_justif1: The report Phase 5 (Chapter 8) and Chapter 4 provides guidance and advice on risk/quality assessment when introducing new AI systems in health and care services. One can find some suggestions for assessment points (chapter 4.2) as well as pointers to some existing assessment frameworks that can be utilized e.g. Health Technology Assessment (HTA) in Chapter 5.3 and Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)/Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA) in Chapter 8.1. Overall the report serves as a valuable resource for understanding the legal ethical and practical aspects of AI impact assessment in the health sector.

imp1_body: Yes a pre-existing body unit or mechanism is responsible for implementing this framework.
init1_name1: PILOT COPILOT FOR Microsoft 365 Findings report from NTNUs project in the Norwegian Data Protection Authoritys regulatory sandbox
init1_justif1: The report documents NTNU’s pilot and evaluation of Microsoft 365 Copilot carried out as part of Datatilsynet’s regulatory sandbox for AI and privacy-friendly innovation. One of the report’s findings (Finding 7 page18) is that Copilot has the capability to monitor and measure employees’ performance and behaviour. This is relevant from a labour-rights perspective as workplace monitoring and AI-assisted performance assessment can affect employee autonomy fairness and transparency. The use of such tools may shift power relationships in the workplace and raises important questions about how performance data are interpreted who has access to it and how decisions based on this information are made.

The contents offer 'concrete examples and practical solutions that are already in use in municipalities' 'focuses on experience sharing' 'encourages copying and adapting solutions'. init1_justif1: This initiative is the direct execution of one of the government steps outlined in the AI Strategy (the framework defined for this indicator): 'The aim of the course is to teach as many as possible about what AI is what it can and cannot be used for and how to start using methods based on AI. The course combines theory with practical exercises and requires no prior knowledge.' The government has funded Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) (https://www.ntnu.edu/norwai/elements-of-ai) to make this AI course available for anyone in Norway who wishes to gain new knowledge and skills in AI

init1_name1: Report on quality assurance Use of AI in health and care services
init1_justif1: The report introduces a framework to support the quality assurance and safe use of artificial intelligence systems in Norway’s health and care services responding to the need for clearer structures to assess and manage AI technologies in the sector. Chapter 5.3 addresses assessing the security of AI system before acquiring and adopting it: 'As part of Phase 2 an overall assessment should be conducted to determine whether available AI systems on the market meet acceptable security standards. The Code of Conduct for information security and data protection (Normen) is a helpful tool for safeguarding information security and privacy in the health and care sector.'

init1_name1: Report on large language models in Norwegian health and care services Risks and adaptations to Norwegian conditions
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Health and Care Services has tasked the Norwegian Directorate of Health with developing a joint AI plan for the health and care services which has resulted in this report. The report discusses the importance of transparency and explainability in AI applications in health service defining the risks and offering solutions for the future (Chapter 4 web page 12 PDF version.) While the specific case study is healthcare the definitions sources and effects described in the report can be applied in various domains and are crucial for AI in public sector especially. The report states that it 'recommends measures that should be initiated now to lay the foundation for safe and responsible use of AI going forward.'

init1_name1: Workshop for State Communicators on Disinformation and AI
init1_justif1: This initiative provided training to state communicators on the risks of disinformation amplified by digital platforms and emerging AI tools. Led by communication scholar Silvio Waisbord the workshop focused on how AI can accelerate false content distort public debate and undermine trust in institutions. It raised awareness among government information officers about synthetic media misinformation dynamics and the need for responsible communication practices. Although not a technical program it directly addresses AI-related disinformation risks.

init1_name1: GuaranIA Project (SPLCIDIT Agreement funded by the IDB)
init1_justif1: The GuaranIA Project develops generative AI services in the Guarani language to promote digital inclusion among rural and vulnerable populations. The project includes dataset collection and structuring model training and the development of inclusive digital tools tailored to Guarani-speaking communities. It is currently being implemented with reported progress activities in the department of Caazapa a region with a significant Guarani-speaking population. The initiative is funded by the the IDB (IDB PR-T1373 https://www.iadb.org/es/proyecto/PR-T1373; see: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1nE7M7hOdcokLde90aHjPqQwiUDq0Vrv4/view?usp=share_link) and executed through collaboration between SPL and CIDIT.

init1_name1: Paraguay trained judges on the ethical use of AI in the justice system
init1_justif1: This initiative focuses on training judges on the ethical use of AI in the justice system emphasising equality in access to justice and the prevention of bias in automated decision-making systems. It aims to strengthen institutional understanding about UNESCO's tool kit on AI and the Rule of Law and public trust in fair and responsible AI not to develop new policy or regulatory instruments.

init1_name1: Paraguay trains judges on the ethical use of AI in the judicial system
init1_justif1: The initiative is the 'AI in the Rule of Law and the Judicial System' workshop organized by the Paraguayan Judiciary and UNESCO. The event addressed ethical foundations for AI in judicial settings based on UNESCO guidelines. Although the full agenda is not public the use of the 'AI & the Rule of Law Toolkit' confirms discussions on judicial responsibility and automation limits. Specifically point 4 (p. 43) of the toolkit details the 'Human-in-the-Loop' principle emphasizing that human oversight is mandatory for judicial decisions to ensure accountability and legal integrity.

init1_name1: Training Programme for Judges on the Ethical Use of AI
init1_justif1: The Judicial Branch of Paraguay in collaboration with UNESCO conducted a training programme for judges on the ethical and responsible use of artificial intelligence in the justice system. The initiative introduced core concepts of AI governance human rights safeguards algorithmic transparency and risks associated with automated decision-making. It strengthened judicial capacity to interpret AI-related legal issues and contributed directly to public sector skills development in AI.

init1_justif1: She Secures Paraguay 2025 is a government-led 24-hour virtual cybersecurity competition organized by MITIC through CERT-PY with OEA/CICTE support targeting women over the aged of 16 held September 19–20 2025. The initiative explicitly aims to reduce the gender gap in the technology sector by strengthening participants' technical skills including AI-related topics. It is national government-funded and operational within the study timeframe oriented to  enhancing AI skills for the workforce.

init1_justif1: The initiative aims to establish the principles and directives for the ethical use of artificial intelligence (hereinafter AI) at Indecopi. Sections 6 ('General Provisions') and 7 ('Specific Provisions') clearly establish the permitted and prohibited uses of AI for the execution of public services provided by the entity. To meet these new standards it is also noted that 'the inclusion of training in the Entity's Personnel Development Plan aimed at strengthening the capabilities of employees in AI-related topics' will be implemented. (Section 5.2).

init1_justif1: According to the article Education Secretary Sonny Angara has announced that 'the agency is set to revise the national curriculum to include training on artificial intelligence use' 'to better prepare Filipino learners and educators for the fast-evolving digital landscape.'“Soon babaguhin na rin natin 'yung ating curriculum para matutong gumamit ng A.I. ang mga bata at matutong gumamit ng A.I. 'yung mga guro natin. 'Yan ang pagbabagong ina-anticipate natin sa darating na mga taon” said Angara. (trans. Soon we will revise the curriculum so that students and teachers alike can learn how to use A.I. This is a change we anticipate in the coming years.)
init1_name2: Department of Education Order No 013 Series of 2025 Establishment of the Education Center for AI Research (E-CAIR)
init1_justif2: The Department of Education (DepEd) partnered with the Southeast Asian Ministers of Education Organization (SEAMEO) to establish the Education Center for AI Research (ECAIR). 5.d says ECAIR will “support the responsible inclusive and strategic integration of AI into the basic and secondary education system covering policy development curriculum enhancement research and capacity building.” 5.e. states that it will 'contribute to the alignment of Al curriculum integration and other related AI initiatives with the Philippine Qualifications Framework to strengthen the coherence of Al learning outcomes with national qualifications and labor market needs.' (p. 1)

init1_name1: Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT) and Meta Joint Initiative to Counter AI-Driven Scams and Online Disinformation
init1_justif1: The Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT) has coordinated with Meta to strengthen efforts to combat AI-generated scams and online disinformation. The initiative includes strategies to detect and filter AI-generated content particularly manipulated videos on social media platforms. The DICT’s Cybercrime Investigation and Coordinating Center (CICC) also works with law enforcement and private sector partners to monitor investigate and take down AI-generated scams and malicious content while assisting victims and raising public awareness about misinformation and online fraud.

init1_name1: Commission on Human Rights Position Paper on House Bills Promoting The Development and Regulation of and Establishing A Framework for AI
init1_justif1: The Commission on Human Rights (CHR) lauds the inclusion of the Bill of Rights of Filipinos in relation... AI as embodied in House Bill 7913. It cites the Right to Remedy which 'gives all persons the right to opt-out from the AI systems; all persons shall have access to human consideration and remedy when automated systems fail or commit error and the right or persons to appeal or contest the impact of AI will not be denied.' (p. 7) As the Bill of Rights is not present in other bills the CHR recommends 'a harmonized approach across all AI legislaion' to ensure consistent rights protections. (p. 7) The paper also recommends additional measures including 'ensuring accessible information and legal protections for all...' (p. 9)

init1_name1: NPC Advisory No 2024 03 or the Guidelines on Child-Oriented Transparency
init1_justif1: The advisory contains guidelines (p. 1 Whereas clauses) on the implementation of RA No. 10173 or the Data Privacy Act (DPA) which provides that Personal Information Controllers (PIC) must implement reasonable and appropriate organizational physical and technical security measures intended to protect personal data. In determining the appropriate level of security PICs must consider the risks D by the processing among other factors. The advisory covers (Sec. 1) all PICs and PIPs engaged in the processing of children’s personal data whether in a digital or physical environment. It covers products or services specifically intended for children or likely to be accessed by children. The DPA covers automated processing of personal data.

init1_name1: iTANONG (trans Ask)
init1_justif1: Under the Advanced Science and Technology Institute of the Department of Science and Technology iTANONG is a 3-year project proposal which starts in 2022 with an aim to develop a natural language interface to relational databases (NLIDB). This artificial intelligence (AI) application can be accessed through a web browser or a mobile app that will enable end-users to key in questions in Filipino English or TagLish (more languages can be accommodated for later research). The answer can be derived from the connected relational database management systems (RDBMS). This linguistic interface will precisely fill the gap between the users and the RDBMS being maintained by an organization.

init1_name1: Policy Note on AI
init1_justif1: The Policy Note prepared by the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) warns that 'AI may potentially accelerate resource consumption' and that 'it can lead to overexploitation.' It notes that 'data centers use significant amounts of water to manage their temperatures' and 'are also significant sources of greenhouse gas emissions.' (p. 48) The Policy Note recommends 'adopting standards for measuring energy consumption and the carbon footprint of AI processes and applications' to mitigate their environmental impact. It also recommends encouraging the use of energy-efficient technologies to reduce energy demands and to manage e-waste to minimize the environmental footprint of AI systems. (p. 56)

init1_name1: Policy Note on AI
init1_justif1: The Policy Note on Artificial Intelligence by the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) highlights governmental efforts to promote human oversight in AI governance. It notes that the Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT) and the Civil Service Commission drafted a Joint Memorandum Circular on the Principles and Guidelines for an Ethical and Trustworthy Use of AI in Government encouraging public institutions to adopt principles such as responsibility and accountability contestability and reasonable human control in AI use and human-centricity (p. 46). These principles aim to ensure that AI systems remain subject to human supervision and intervention.

init1_name1: Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas Project Sapiens Thematic Review of AI and Machine Learning in the Philippine Financial Services
init1_justif1: The Review by Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) or the Central Bank recommends that institutions must conduct a 'mandatory Privacy Impact Assessment for all AI systems that handle personal data' and a risk analysis 'to evaluate potential privacy risks to individuals arising from the system's operations.' Institutions must 'develop mitigation strategies to minimize identified risks...' The BSP suggests that 'risk-based assessment per project... through risk tiering be implemented to uniformly determine if proposed projects are within the risk appetite and tolerance of entity.' 'Identifying high-risk AI system is beneficial for prioritization and risk management.' (p. 13)

init1_name1: Workforce Competitiveness and AI Governance Consultation and Strategic Discussion
init1_justif1: The consultation focused on the responsible integration of AI in workplaces with a strong emphasis on ensuring job security fair employment practices and continuous workforce development. In his speech Department of Labor Sec. Laguesma emphasized the 'critical role of stakeholder collaboration in ensuring ethical use of AI' and that 'human accountability must remain central to decision-making.'AI must be introduced into the workplace in a way that supports and engages workers. Many employers acknowledge that AI can drive business efficiency and innovation but emphasize that its integration must be accompanied by strategic workforce planning particularly in reskilling and job transition support” the Labor Secretary said. (p. 3)

init1_name1: PLLuM (Polish Large Language Model)
init1_justif1: Goal: To build an open sovereign Large Language Model trained primarily on high-quality Polish data (literature legal texts scientific archives) rather than translating from English concepts. The project includes the Polish Linguistic and Cultural Competency Benchmark. This is a testing framework consisting of manual questions about Polish history geography literature and traditions to evaluate any AI model's 'Polishness' ensuring it understands local context (e.g. knowing who 'Wokulski' is or the significance of the 'Warsaw Uprising' without hallucinating).

init1_justif1: This initiative provides free training funded by public funds at the Branzowe Centrum Umiejetnosci (Sectoral Skills Centre) in Robotics in Wodzislaw Slaski. The centre offers courses such as “Robot Operator” for adults aged 25+ aimed at helping participants acquire practical skills in robotics and industrial automation. init1_name1: Draft Act on AI Systems
init1_justif1: Ministry of Digital Affairs of Poland prepared and published the Draft Act on Artificial Intelligence Systems which establishes mechanisms to ensure the safety and oversight of AI systems in Poland. The draft proposes creating the the Commission for the Development and Safety of Artificial Intelligence as the market supervisory authority responsible for monitoring AI systems conducting inspections and imposing sanctions for violations. It also introduces regulatory sandboxes (Chapter 8 – Support for Innovation) enabling companies to test AI solutions under regulatory supervision and verify safety requirements before wider deployment.

init1_name1: Scientific and technical presentation of the Portuguese AI model Amlia scheduled for September 30th
init1_justif1: The government’s initiative to launch the AMÁLIA model through a public workshop promoting Cultural and Linguistic Diversity by developing an AI system centered on the Portuguese language and its varieties. The model was trained on Portuguese-language data and integrates multimodal elements—text speech image and video—within a Portuguese cultural context. By emphasizing European Portuguese and its cultural heritage the initiative enhances the representativity of local language and culture in AI systems countering the dominance of English-based models.

init1_justif1: The CIG 2024 Action Plan explicitly includes formal education and awareness initiatives addressing gender bias in AI. Under the section “Program of the XXIII Constitutional Government – 3rd Strategic Challenge: Inequalities” the plan sets the goal of promoting a gender perspective in R&D content activities and careers. This involves coordinating with R&D institutions and emerging technological areas aligning with public funding frameworks and developing solutions that combat gender bias in AI systems (p. 24 of the PDF).

init1_justif1: The conference addressed AI governance challenges in the Portuguese and European context incorporating key terms tied to Human Oversight and Determination: regulation (pp.1–7 of the pdf) oversight (pp.412 of the pdf) accountability (pp.47–8 of the pdf) innovation and trust guided by ethical values inclusion and transparency (p.2 of the pdf) fundamental and human rights (p.4 of the pdf) regulatory framework centred on human rights (p.5 of the pdf) decision-making (pp.2711 of the pdf) and people empowerment/skills (pp.1–246 of the pdf). The conference emphasized that technologies must be regulated according to these human-rights-centred principles.

init1_name1: Guide to AI (GuiA)
init1_justif1: The Portuguese Guide for Artificial Intelligence highlights the EU AI ACT (p.17 of the PDF) and situates robustness and security as core components of a trustworthy AI ecosystem (pp. 41-46 of the PDF). Robust AI systems must be resilient to faults errors misuse and adversarial threats and maintain reliable functioning across their lifecycle (pp. 41-42 of the PDF). Security measures (e.g. risk mitigation safeguards against cyberattacks and resilient performance) ensure systems behave predictably and safely (pp. 44-46 of the PDF).

init1_name1: SMEs Go Digital workshop for the hotel sector
init1_justif1: The SMEs Go Digital workshop hosted by the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) in collaboration with Qatar Tourism (Visit Qatar) addresses AI transparency and explainability by providing SMEs with practical guidance on the adoption and use of AI solutions. Through expert-led sessions participants were introduced to real-world AI applications in the tourism sector including how AI systems function how outputs are generated and how AI can be used responsibly to improve customer experience and operations. This hands-on awareness approach supports informed decision-making and helps SMEs better understand assess and explain AI-driven tools used in their business processes.
init1_name2: International Conference on AI and Human Rights Opportunities Risks and Visions for a Better Future
init1_justif2: The International Conference was led locally by the National Human Rights Committee of Qatar in collaboration with the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology and the National Cyber Security Agency. The resulting Doha Declaration advances transparency and explainability by stating in its Preamble that transparency is fundamental to the trustworthiness and legitimacy of artificial intelligence systems (p.1). The section on Transparency and Explainability calls for public disclosure of artificial intelligence use understandable explanations of artificial intelligence-driven decisions and independent review and human rights impact considerations (pp.2–3)

init1_name1: Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI Human Rights Democracy and the Rule of Law
init1_justif1: Via Art. 8 and 9 the Framework Convention requires Parties to adopt or maintain measures to ensure that adequate transparency and oversight requirements are in place in respect of activities within the lifecycle of artificial intelligence systems. It further mandates Parties to ensure accountability and responsibility for adverse impacts resulting from AI systems’ lifecycle activities.These provisions establish a legal basis for governments to publicly disclose AI systems oversight mechanisms and documentation.

init1_name1: Romanias National Cybersecurity Directorate implements EU INSPIRE project
init1_justif1: Romania’s National Directorate for Cybersecurity has joined the EU-INSPIRE project coordinated by the University of Piraeus Research Centre in Greece which focuses on innovative multidisciplinary industry-oriented cybersecurity education aimed at upskilling and reskilling the EU workforce. Launched in January 2025 the project will deliver three specialised master’s programmes (MSc MBA and research-focused MSc) in cybersecurity and AI. The initiative seeks to address the cybersecurity skills gap by developing accredited training programmes and certifications centred on protecting AI-enabled systems and securing digital infrastructures.

init1_justif1: A one-day training workshop entitled 'Productivity Enhancement through AI: Empowering Government Operations' was held for government employees. The workshop was 'a collaboration between Taiwan Technical Mission (TTM) and the Caribbean Digital Transformation Project (CARDTP).' CARDTP is an initiative of the Organization of Eastern Caribbean States. The Government of St. Lucia has a leading role in the CARDTP as the local initiative. The training was implemented in Saint Lucia for public officials held at the National ICT Centre in Castries with technical support from the Taiwan Technical Mission.

init1_name1: Saudi Arabia launches AI school curriculum
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Education in collaboration with the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology and the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority announced this initiative to enhance educational quality through modern school infrastructure and partnerships with innovative private-sector schools. The launch of the new curriculum represents one of the largest national AI education deployments globally as Saudi Arabia integrates artificial-intelligence learning pathways from elementary through higher-education levels. While AI literacy is not always explicitly named the technology-driven design directly supports the development of AI skills and advanced digital competencies across the education system.

init1_name1: Report Fake News National Platform
init1_justif1: The National “Report Fake News” Platform enables citizens to report digital misinformation directly to authorities. Operated under the Digital Government Authority it employs artificial intelligence and machine-learning tools to swiftly detect classify and track misleading or harmful online content. This initiative demonstrates practical enforcement of the AI Ethics Principles (2023) and Generative AI Guidelines (2025) by using AI systems for content verification public protection and social stability translating ethical commitments on AI transparency safety and accountability into operational practice.

init1_name1: Humain
init1_justif1: The HUMAIN initiative addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity in AI by prioritizing Arabic-native large language models and culturally aligned AI systems tailored to Saudi and regional contexts. HUMAIN emphasizes Arabic language enablement and localization to reduce dependence on Western-centric AI models (HUMAIN website “About” and “What We Do”).HUMAIN was launched through a strategic partnership with the Ministry of Interior at the Absher Conference and operates within the national AI ecosystem. Moreover SDAIA is listed as a strategic ally confirming formal state backing alignment with Vision 2030 and sovereign-level coordination rather than a private or civil-society initiative.

init1_name1: The CST Sustainability Toolkit
init1_justif1: The CST Sustainability Toolkit (2024) was developed by the Communications Space & Technology Commission (CST) — the Saudi government’s national regulator for the ICT and emerging technology sectors.It demonstrates the government’s leadership in promoting environmentally sustainable AI and ICT practices providing national guidance on energy-efficient data centers renewable energy adoption and e-waste reduction.Sections 2.1 “Sustainable ICT Infrastructure” and 3.4 “Energy Efficiency and AI Operations” (pp. 10–15).This toolkit supports Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030 sustainability goals and the UN SDGs (7 12 13) by aligning AI growth with environmental responsibility.

init1_name1: Generative AI Guidelines
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The initiative addresses Fairness and Non-discrimination by explicitly requiring government entities to identify affected groups and actively prevent bias discrimination or profiling arising from GenAI data or algorithms. It mandates careful review of AI-generated content to detect stereotypes biased associations and under-representation and stresses user awareness of intersectional biases (e.g. gender race). It also requires understanding training-data sources and applying human oversight to eliminate discriminatory outcomes before use in government contexts (Section 3.1 Fairness p.4)

init1_name1: AI Ethics Principles
init1_justif1: The AI Ethics Principles (2023) issued by SDAIA promote Gender Equality in AI through their emphasis on fairness non-discrimination and inclusiveness. The framework requires that AI systems be designed and deployed to ensure equal treatment of all individuals preventing bias or discrimination based on gender or other attributes.Principles 2 (Fairness & Non-discrimination) and 5 (Accountability).
init1_name2: Elevate Program
init1_justif2: The Elevate program (launched by SDAIA with Google Cloud) directly addresses gender equality in AI by training 25000+ women in data science and machine learning aiming to close the gender gap in the AI workforce. It equips women with technical skills mentorship and career pathways to increase their representation in a male-dominated sector.

init1_name1: SDAIAs AI Ethics Certification Program
init1_justif1: SDAIA’s AI Ethics Certification Program operationalizes Principle 6 – Transparency & Explainability from the Principles of AI Ethics (pp. 23–24) by requiring certified service providers to document and disclose data sources algorithms decision logic and rationale behind AI outcomes. Certification audits verify that AI systems are traceable auditable and explainable with mechanisms for user feedback and redress. This ensures public trust through accountability and clarity in how AI decisions are generated and communicated.
init1_name2: The AI Adoption Framework
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The AI Adoption Framework enhances transparency and accountability by embedding continuous monitoring and evaluation mechanisms across the AI system lifecycle. It requires entities to document data flows model behavior and decision outcomes ensuring results are explainable and traceable. Sections on AI Governance and Monitoring (pp. 18–22) and Performance Management (pp. 26–28) outline periodic reviews reporting dashboards and compliance metrics to ensure AI systems remain transparent ethical and accountable over time.

init1_name1: National teacher training program in digital technology and AI in Senegal
init1_justif1: The Senegalese Ministry of National Education (MEN) officially launched a national teacher training program in digital technology and artificial intelligence (AI) . Coupled with the distribution of computers to science students this initiative marks according to authorities the operational launch of the Digital Education Strategy 2025–2029 a 130 billion CFA franc initiative unveiled in January. The training delivered entirely online should be accessible from a computer tablet or smartphone with an internet connection.  It aims to enable teachers to adapt their teaching practices to technological advancements; to use digital resources to enrich learning; to raise students' awareness of the challenges of digital technology and AI.

init1_name1: Using AI to improve language learning in Senegal
init1_justif1: Developed with the social enterprise Kajou and the research lab Pleias this simple and sustainable platform is based on a LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) language model with seven billion parameters. The Senegalese Ministry of Education has integrated a wide of variety of French text and royalty-free content into the app with the aim of developing activities that are specifically geared towards the linguistic and cultural context in Senegal.

init1_name1: Personal Data Protection Week 2025
init1_justif1: The Personal Data Protection Week 2025 included a PDP Summit from July 7 to July 10 which included a workshop session from 2-5pm titled 'It's Not Child's Play – Protecting Kids’ Data in a Grown-Up Digital World'. This workshop aimed at Data Protection Officers Compliance Teams Business Users and Professionals (especially from sectors that may handle children’s personal data such as retail education healthcare etc.) allows greater understanding of the regulatory expectations around children’s personal data under Singapore’s PDPA and exploration of practical strategies through scenario-based exercises to design safer and compliant digital experiences for young users.

init1_name1: National Multimodal LLM Programme
init1_justif1: The National Multimodal Large Language Model Programme (NMLP) addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by developing AI systems that understand Southeast Asia's diverse cultures and languages. The S$70 million program creates multimodal LLMs with regional context and builds upon SEA-LION (Southeast Asian Languages in One Network) which is more representative of Southeast Asia's cultural contexts and linguistic nuances supporting 13 languages including Thai Vietnamese Indonesian and managing context-switching between languages in multilingual societies (IMDA Infocomm Media Development Authority Press Release 4 Dec 2023 paragraphs 2-3).
init1_name2: Singapore AI Safety Red Teaming Challenge Evaluation Report (2025)
init1_justif2: The initiative advances cultural and linguistic diversity by designing a multicultural multilingual red-teaming exercise across 10 Asia-Pacific languages and 9 countries developing a cultural bias taxonomy and methodology tailored to local contexts and comparing exploits in English vs regional languages to reveal weaker safety guardrails outside English (69.4% vs 30.6% of successful exploits). Key discussions: Executive Summary; Cultural Bias Taxonomy (pp.14–20); Stage 4 Results Analysis – Quantitative Findings (Successful exploits across Bias Categories English vs regional) (pp.23 26–27).

init1_name1: Refreshed Green Mark for DCs 2024 (GMDC2024)
init1_justif1: The Refreshed Green Mark for DCs 2024 (GMDC:2024) addresses environmental impact in AI by setting strict energy efficiency (Section 1 pp. 5–8) carbon monitoring (Section 2 pp. 9–12) and renewable energy requirements for data centers which directly support AI workloads. It includes credits for energy-efficient IT equipment monitoring of greenhouse gas emissions and mandates for cooling system efficiency all critical for mitigating the environmental footprint of AI-driven data centers (Sections 1–2 pp. 5–12).

init1_name1: AI Verify Toolkit
init1_justif1: The AI Verify initiative was developed by the Infocomm Media Development Authority of Singapore (IMDA) a government statutory board. Its ongoing development and adoption are supported by the AI Verify Foundation a entity wholly owned by IMDA. The AI Verify Toolkit operationalizes fairness and non-discrimination by providing a standardized process for organizations to test their AI systems for demographic bias and fairness (see SGModelAIGovFramework2.pdf pp. 55–60 Section 7.7.2). It guides organizations to use technical and process checks to detect measure and address discriminatory outcomes ensuring that AI deployments produce fair and equitable results across diverse groups.

init1_justif1: Relaunch directly targets gender gaps in the tech/AI workforce by helping women who left work re-enter tech roles including AI-related jobs through training employability support and employer partnerships. The SG Women in Tech (SGWIT) platform – an IMDA initiative – positions Relaunch as part of its mission to attract and develop girls and women for the tech sector. IMDA’s press release on SGWIT’s 5th anniversary describes Relaunch as “empowering women to re-enter the tech workforce” with NTUC LearningHub as anchor partner (para. 4–7; link:https://web.archive.org/web/20260210093027/https://www.imda.gov.sg/resources/press-releases-factsheets-and-speeches/press-releases/2024/sg-women-in-tech-marks-5th-anniversary)

init1_name1: Testing Real World GenAI Systems
init1_justif1: The Global AI Assurance Sandbox implements ex-ante impact assessment mechanisms by providing a structured testing environment for organizations to evaluate potential risks and harms of generative AI applications before public deployment. Organizations partner with specialist technical testers to conduct comprehensive evaluation of hallucination undesirable content data disclosure adversarial prompt vulnerability and broader use-case risks such as safety financial impact trust regulatory compliance and human oversight. Each testing cycle produces public case studies and reports enabling ex-post documentation and learning from deployed systems.
init1_name2: Singapore AI Safety Red Teaming Challenge Evaluation Report
init1_justif2: This report operationalizes ex-ante impact assessment by developing a systematic methodology for evaluating potential harms before wider deployment. It establishes a 4-stage red teaming methodology (pages 10-23 Evaluation Report): 1. Risk Definition (pages 12-14) 2. Challenge Design (page 20) 3. Annotation (page 22) 4.Results Analysis (pages 23-27 40-42). The report directly supports the Model AI Governance Framework's Testing and Assurance dimension (Framework pages 19-20) by providing empirical evidence and benchmarking data to test LLMs for cultural bias.

imp1_body_name: Agency for Communication Networks and Services of the Republic of Slovenia (Agencija za komunikacijska omreja in storitve Republike Slovenije AKOS)
init1_name1: The Online Eye reporting
init1_justif1: Spletno oko is classified as Programmatic Implementation & Funding because it is an operational reporting service co-funded by the Ministry of Digital Transformation to remove illegal online content. As Slovenia’s national hotline it serves as a rapid response mechanism for AI-facilitated harms. The 2023–24 Annual Report (May 2025) provides evidence of its active implementation specifically addressing AI-driven threats in the “New and Growing Risks” section. It details the handling of cases involving AI-generated “deepnude” content used for extortion (p.13) and synthetic CSAM including “realistic images of non-existent children” (p.14) as well as broader pathways through which children become victims of sexual abuse videos (p.5).

init1_name1: Spletno Oko Annual Report
init1_justif1: The Spletno oko Annual Report (2023–24) provides direct empirical evidence of AI-facilitated harms including deepfake sexual content and AI-generated child sexual abuse material (pp.5 13 14). It documents how such technologies are used for exploitation and violence offering concrete insight into emerging risks and patterns of abuse. As Slovenia’s national reporting hotline co-funded by the Ministry of Digital Transformation and the EU (p.22) Spletno oko functions as an operational mechanism to identify and respond to these harms thereby directly addressing safety and security as well as AI-facilitated violence through real-world case handling and reporting.

init1_justif1: This monograph documents the outcomes of the government-funded RSDO project (2020–2023) created to prevent the 'digital extinction' of Slovenian. Chapter 2 written by researchers from the Fran Ramovš Institute for the Slovenian Language describes the essential infrastructure for AI and NLP development in the local language including speech transcription in the creation of the Artur speech database (P. 39-59). By releasing these open-access resources the initiative ensures that future AI systems can be trained on diverse high-quality Slovenian datasets preserving linguistic nuance and cultural representation

init1_name1: Public Tender Development of a unified horizontal platform for AI (JN006311/2025)
init1_type1: Dedicated budget lines or public tenders
init1_justif1: The public tender Publication No. JN006311/2025-EUe16/01 was issued on 11 August 2025 for the development of a unified horizontal platform for artificial intelligence. The project supports the National Programme’s objective of establishing shared AI infrastructure within the public sector. Financed by European Union funds the procurement follows an Open Procedure in line with the Public Procurement Act ZJN-3. The contract is awarded on the basis of the most economically advantageous tender reflecting an evaluation beyond price alone. Through this tender the government advances responsible AI procurement by investing in a centralized and standardized platform that enables consistent development and deployment of AI solutions.

init1_justif1: This initiative supports public sector AI skills development through concrete publicly funded training courses  delivered by the Slovenian Upravna akademija. These include “Generative Artificial Intelligence and Leadership” a 12 hour mentored programme for senior public officials focused on the safe ethical and effective use of generative AI tools such as ChatGPT and Copilot through practical case work; “Using ChatGPT and Generative AI in Practice” a 5 hour course for public sector employees covering everyday AI use prompt design and security considerations see https://drive.google.com/file/d/1YRP1rMI_2tdLwFML4mvABvpctZh-AJEG/view?usp=drive_link for curriculum.

init1_justif1: EDIH DIGI-SI operates as Slovenia’s central entry point for digital transformation co-funded by the Ministry for Digital Transformation and the EU. Through these activities EDIH DIGI-SI implements the objectives of the National AI Programme (NpAI) and equips the Slovenian workforce with competencies for an AI-driven economy.

init1_justif1: The Independent Electoral Commission (IEC) partnered with social media giants Google Meta and TikTok and non-profit organisation Media Monitoring Africa (MMA) to curb disinformation ahead of South Africa’s 2024 National and Provincial Elections. The parties signed a Framework of Cooperation to work together to combat disinformation and other digital harms including deepfakes. As part of the agreement the Real411 digital disinformation reporting platform created by MMA (see CSO initiatives) was implemented. While the agreement was for the 2024 election similar agreements were concluded during previous election cycles and will possibly be the same for future elections.
init1_name2: GUIDANCE NOTE ON THE PROCESSING OF PERSONAL INFORMATION OF VOTERS AND THE COUNTERING OF MISINFORMATION AND DISINFORMATION DURING ELECTIONS
init1_justif2: The South African Information Regulator (established under the Protection of Personal Information Act) issued a Guidance Note on the Processing of Personal Information of Voters and the Countering of Misinformation and Disinformation during Elections. The guidance note recognises the right to privacy and protection of personal information in cases where a person’s “voice and/or image is used without their consent to spread fake messages particularly in instances where generative artificial intelligence is used.”(pg. 18). It also provided guidance for how political parties and independent candidates can mitigate against misinformation and disinformation (pg. 18-19).

While the Ministry of Science and ICT remains the primary enforcement authority the Committee is tasked with reviewing national AI policies ensuring ethical and safety compliance and advising on standards and methodologies for conducting AI impact assessments.

init1_name1: Court Ruling Spanish Supreme Court STS 3826/2025
init1_justif1: The case in Supreme Court concerned a challenge brought by a CSO BOSCO against the use without disclosure of an automated decision-making system employed in the Spanish public sector. The claim relied on the GDPR arguing that the system involved automated profiling and that affected individuals were entitled to contest the decision and seek meaningful review. The ruling demonstrates how Articles 22 and 79 can be used to challenge decisions supported by algorithmic systems and to seek judicial review. It confirms that individuals and CSOs can rely on GDPR procedural rights to contest AI-supported decisions obtain reasoned explanations of how such systems operate and trigger independent oversight by administrative and judicial authorities.

init1_name1: Spains AI Sandbox for high-risk systems
init1_justif1: Spain’s AI Sandbox for high-risk systems is a government-led initiative designed to help companies identify assess and mitigate the potential negative impacts of deploying AI in concrete scenarios. Through supervised pilot tests the Sandbox allowed real-world use cases to be analysed generating practical insights that can be reused by other actors in the sector to better understand the risks. In this way the initiative supports more informed and targeted impact assessments by deployers. Although outside GIRAI’s data collection period the Sandbox concluded in December 2025 with the publication of a series of practical guidance documents to support impact assessment practices: https://aesia.digital.gob.es/es/guias

init1_name1: AI Sandbox
init1_justif1: Spain’s AI Sandbox for high-risk systems explicitly included employment use cases which makes it directly relevant to labour protections under the AI Act. Among the selected participants were Adevinta Jobs with its “How You Match” tool that ranks compatibility between applicants’ CVs and vacancy requirements and Shakers Global Solutions with its “Shakers AI Matchmaker” system that uses personalised recommendations to match freelance talent with business projects. By testing these HR and labour-matching systems in a controlled regulatory setting Spain is generating practical evidence on how such systems work what risks may arise in practice and what compliance measures will be needed.

init1_justif1: The launch of AI Clubs in schools was a collaborative initiative involving the Ministry of Technology the Ministry of Education AIClub SLASSCOM and IronOn. As part of the National AI Strategy 2024–2028 this initiative “aims to promote AI knowledge and skills preparing students for a future where AI competencies will be crucial for success.” The event was attended by multiple stakeholders from both the government and private sector as well as 300 school students. This initiative would be implemented across 100 schools with plans for expansion to all schools islandwide (https://web.archive.org/web/20250214221149/https://mode.gov.lk/notices/ai-clubs-in-sri-lanka-schools).
init1_name2: Memorandum of Understanding between Ministry of Education (MOE) and Microsoft Corporation to integrate AI (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) into Sri Lankas National School Curriculum
init1_justif2: This initiative aims to formally incorporate AI literacy into Sri Lanka’s education system. Led by the MOE in partnership with Microsoft the programme commenced as a pilot to integrate AI and ML into the national school curriculum. The curriculum is being developed collaboratively by global Microsoft Education teams and the National Institute of Education (NIE) ensuring alignment with national education standards. The pilot has been rolled out across 20 selected schools in 20 districts with phased expansion planned. By following a “train-the-trainer” model the initiative seeks to empower both educators and learners equipping them with foundational skills to navigate the era of AI.

init1_name1: Parliamentary Debates Adjournment Motion New Legislation to Protect Society from AI-related Harmful Activities
init1_justif1: The adjournment motion titled “New Legislation to Protect Society from Artificial Intelligence-related Harmful Activities” was proposed to Parliament by a member of the then-ruling party. He stressed the need for new legislation to protect citizens from AI-related harms on cultural religious and societal grounds (p. 47). He also noted the growing manipulation of human images videos photographs and audio using AI and the potential negative impact on national security (pp. 48-49). While he does not specifically mention AI-facilitated misinformation and violence the motion’s broad theme encompasses it by highlighting risks from unethical AI use.

Under “Challenges and Considerations” it also introduces high-level safeguards related to privacy bias and human oversight concerning children (pp. 31–32). While this demonstrates government awareness and capacity-building efforts it does not constitute an official policy or legal framework (https://web.archive.org/web/20250906000853/https://childwomenmin.gov.lk/feature/training/en).

init1_name1: FDPIC concludes investigation into voice recognition at PostFinance
init1_justif1: The Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC) has examined whether PostFinance AG is violating data protection regulations when using voice recognition as a means of authentication. It concluded the investigation on 16 May 2025 with a ruling instructing PostFinance to obtain the express consent of the person concerned when creating voiceprints for voice recognition and to delete voice prints for which no consent has been explicitly been given. In its investigation the FDPIC found that PostFinance's processing of biometric data for authentication purposes violates the principle of proportionality. In addition voiceprints are being created without customers explicitly providing their consent.

init1_name1: AI - Youth and Media
init1_justif1: The platform is the federal government's national platform for promoting media literacy including AI. It is intended for parents and professionals with the goal of empowering children and young people. The repository has the following content framed within the context of children and youth: Algorithms and their relationship with AI (p.2) ChatGPT and other forms of generative AI (p.3) Risks of AI (p.4) AI and youth protection (p.5) Legal (p.6) and Educational materials (p.7). There is guidance on the use of AI Chatbots in working with young people (p.6). The educational material includes access to other platforms.

init1_name1: Apertus a fully open transparent multilingual language model
init1_justif1: Switzerland's first open-source large-scale multilingual language model Apertus has had its entire development process including its architecture model weights training data and methods made open-access and fully documented. This marks a milestone in generative AI for transparency and diversity. Trained with 15 trillion tokens in over 1000 languages Apertus includes many languages ??that were previously underrepresented in language models such as Swiss German Romansh and many others. This initiative is led by the Swiss government which funds the project through the Swiss National Supercomputing Centre in partnership with EPFL and ETH Zurich universities. The model is being implemented by Swisscom.

The report should support the necessary actions for the safe use of AI with a view to security of supply (p.5).

init1_name1: OFCOM Monitoring of the AI Guidelines for the Confederation 2025
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Federal Office of Communications (OFCOM) completed the first official monitoring cycle of the AI Guidelines for the Confederation in 2022. The second report was published in 2025 and was also the Overview of Artificial Intelligence Regulation Report to the Federal Council to provide information for the decision on Switzerland’s approach to regulating AI in the future. In the report fairness and (non-)discrimination were key terms that were mentioned. Adjustments to existing sectoral laws and the responsibility of the competent specialized authorities are necessary particularly in matters relating to discrimination and equity in various sectors (p.11).
init1_name2: Competence Network for AI (CNAI)
init1_justif2: The Competence Network for Artificial Intelligence (CNAI) addresses non-discrimination and fairness regarding AI by providing legal guidance facilitating compliance and promoting best practices to state actors who must adhere to existing law. The CNAI (Competence Network for Artificial Intelligence) facilitates active knowledge exchange through its growing Community of Practice (CoP) which is an organized group for people to share information develop know-how and broaden general AI knowledge. This includes meetings seminars and the 'Data Science and AI for the Public Good' webinar series. The network also runs an internal federal administration group the DS&AI CoP which plans to expand its reach nationally and internationally.

init1_name1: Swiss Federal Commission for Womens Issues
init1_justif1: The Swiss Federal Commission for Women's Affairs' magazine 'Women's Issues' has published articles shedding light on the current situation in Switzerland and raising social legal and political questions: What legal framework exists in Switzerland and Europe to regulate the use of these technologies? What adjustments are needed to prevent the exacerbation of existing social inequalities? For example the magazine's authors highlight a specific case of algorithmic discrimination in hiring in Switzerland and visited the Federal Parliament building to obtain information on the political stance of parliamentarians regarding the topic of AI. 'Women's Issues' is published annually on a current topic.

init1_name1: The AI for Safer Children Initiative
init1_justif1: The initiative supports the state structures that handle child protection outside of formal court proceedings. The initiative is designed to support law enforcement agencies policymakers educators and technology providers in building a coordinated response. The underlying protection mechanisms in the UAE (e.g. the Child Rights Law) rely heavily on state-based non-judicial interventions by the Child Protection Specialist. These specialists handle preventative and therapeutic intervention and take necessary measures in agreement with the custodian before escalating to public prosecution or court. The AI initiative enhances the operational capability of these state mechanisms
init1_name2: The AI for Safer Children Initiative
init1_justif2: The initiative develops and deploys over 90 innovative AI tools for practical and scalable solutions to enhance children's safety and well-being. These tools contributed by more than 50 technology providers include solutions for detecting online child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and facilitating victim identification.This implementation directly secures the child's right to protection from exploitation abuse and Child Pornography which is prohibited under the law. By developing these resources the initiative ensures AI is a 'shield for the most vulnerable' fulfilling the commitment to technologically advanced child protection.
init1_name3: The AI for Safer Children Initiative
init1_justif3: The initiative itself is a 'groundbreaking international collaboration' between the Ministry of Interior of the UAE and UNICRI.Its central mechanism for engagement is the Global Hub on AI for Safer Children which serves as a 'collaborative digital space' connecting 'child protection professionals technologists and law enforcement entities around the world'. This hub has achieved wide international reach incorporating over 1100 members from 120 countries (as of January 2025).

init1_name1: Assessment of the Impact of Personal Data Protection
init1_justif1: The DPIA ensures human oversight in AI by mandating pre-deployment evaluation of high-risk automated systems. Controllers must assess risks when processing involves automated profiling with legal or serious impacts ensuring human review before deployment. The DPIA requires clear explanations risk evaluations and mitigation measures coordinated with a Data Protection Officer (DPO) who provides technical oversight. Controllers must also regularly review results embedding continuous human control and accountability across the AI system’s lifecycle.
