init1_name1: The Office of Communicationss opening of formal enforcement programmes under the Online Safety Act
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The Ofcom Online Safety Act Roadmap to Regulation initiative supports implementation of the framework by operationalising risk management and enforcement of platform duties that directly relate to AI-facilitated misinformation and violence. It guides how the Office of Communications expects services to identify mitigate and report risks from algorithms and automated systems that amplify harmful or illegal content (including violent hateful or misleading material). The roadmap shapes how platforms should use proactive technologies transparency reporting and algorithmic risk assessments to reduce AI-driven harms which are all core to applying the Act’s requirements to real world AI misuse.
init1_name2: Horizon Scanning - AI disinformation and cyber security
init1_justif2: The POST briefing on AI disinformation and cyber security addresses the indicator by providing a government-led evidence-based analysis of how AI systems enable and amplify misinformation including risks of manipulation societal harm and potential links to violence. Produced by the Parliamentary Office of Science and Technology within the UK Parliament it synthesises current research policy developments and threat landscapes including issues such as  AI-deepfakes information warfare and online radicalisation. By informing legislators and supporting parliamentary scrutiny it strengthens the state’s capacity to understand and respond to AI-driven misinformation risks.

init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Information Commissioner's Office Annual Report and Performance Reporting Framework supports Access to Redress and Remedy in the AI context by documenting how individuals can seek remedies through complaints and regulatory oversight. The report explains that helping the public “resolve complaints quickly and efficiently” is a core regulatory function (p. 20) and presents detailed statistics on data protection complaints handled investigations completed and enforcement outcomes (pp. 19 34–36). It also identifies artificial intelligence and biometrics as a regulatory priority and reports on investigations and interventions addressing risks from AI use (pp. 10 15–18).

init1_name1: Protecting children from harms online Codes of Practice (Ofcom)
init1_justif1: The Protection of Children Codes of Practice operationalise children’s rights in AI by requiring platforms to control recommender systems that could surface harmful content to minors (Section 4E pp. 47–52). They mandate highly effective age assurance (Section 4B pp. 21–28) and define “highly effective” with AI/ML-specific safeguards: reliability testing ongoing monitoring and fairness/bias checks on AI age-estimation models (Section 5 paras 5.14–5.15 pp. 87–88). Together these measures make AI systems age-appropriate and reduce algorithmic harms to children.
init1_name2: Online Safety Transparency Reporting Final Transparency Guidance (Ofcom)
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The Final Transparency Guidance makes children’s rights in AI measurable by requiring annual provider reports and Ofcom’s own report enabling systematic oversight of algorithmic risks to children (s.1.3 p.3; s.5 pp.20–22). It tailors notice requirements to the proportion of child users and applicable child-protection duties (ss.3.19–3.22 pp.12–13). Annex A1 lists reportable metrics on content harmful to children mitigation systems and the design/operation of algorithms affecting recommendations (Part 1 items 1–3 8–9 18; pp.27–29).
init1_name3: Ofcom Online Safety Act enforcement investigations
init1_justif3: Ofcom’s investigations enforce the Act’s child-safety duties that directly implicate AI systems. Services must assess and mitigate risks from “the design of functionalities algorithms and features” (s.11(6)(b) pp.11) and deploy highly effective age assurance (s.12(4–8) pp.12–13). Probing suspected breaches compels platforms to fix recommender systems moderation AI and age-checks that could expose children to harm. Ofcom’s enforcement powers (fines directions) are set out in ss.122–127 (pp.145–153). Ofcom launched nine investigations under the Online Safety Act.

init2_name1: ICO statutory Codes of Practice (AI/ADM and EdTech)
init2_justif1: The initiative is the ICO's statutory duty to prioritise children's protection established under Section 91. When regulating AI systems that process children's data the Commissioner must consider that children merit specific protection as they may be less aware of risks consequences and their rights regarding personal data processing. This embeds child-focused oversight into all regulatory decisions including AI guidance enforcement actions and code development ensuring children's vulnerabilities are actively prioritized in practice.
init2_name2: Research and analysis Safety and security risks of generative AI
init2_justif2: The UK Department for Science Innovation & Technology (DSIT) report “Safety and Security Risks of Generative Artificial Intelligence to 2025 (Annex B)” addresses children’s rights and protections by identifying the risk of AI-generated child sexual abuse material and other harmful content. It explicitly recognises that generative AI can enable and scale the creation and distribution of such material increasing the exposure of children to exploitation and abuse.

init1_name1: The National Archives (Transkribus)
init1_justif1: The initiative is a program by The National Archives that involves training automated text-recognition models (Transkribus) to decode historical documents supported by large-scale public participation via Zooniverse. It addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity by enabling systems to learn and accurately process distinctive historical handwriting styles and language variations thereby preserving and making accessible culturally significant archival materials that would otherwise remain unreadable (p. 9).
init1_name2: AI and Data Science Conversion Courses Programme
init1_justif2: This initiative partially addresses workforce diversity rather than cultural/linguistic diversity. The programme provides £10000 scholarships targeting underrepresented groups in AI: women (over 40% of students) Black students (25%) and disabled students (15%). Page 23 of the National AI Strategy confirms these diversity targets. While this addresses demographic representation in the AI workforce it does not specifically target cultural groups linguistic communities or indigenous representation. The programme focuses on gender racial and disability diversity rather than cultural traditions or linguistic diversity.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
imp1_body: Yes an implementation body unit or mechanism currently in operation that was created specifically for AI governance.
init1_name1: AI Energy Council
init1_justif1: The AI Energy Council addresses AI's environmental impact by overseeing energy sustainability of AI infrastructure. The United Nations Educational Scientific and Cultural Organization dimension focuses on promoting energy efficiency of data centres including renewable energy use and reducing carbon footprints. The Council co-chaired by the Technology and Energy Secretaries convenes energy providers the Office of Gas and Electricity Markets the National Energy System Operator and AI companies to forecast AI energy demand ensure AI Growth Zones are powered by clean energy and explore low-carbon solutions like small modular reactors.
init1_name2: Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) Report on AI Data Centres Water Use
init1_justif2: The Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) is a government-led alliance bringing together various actors to ensure the sustainability and green-ness of the UK government's digital services/infrastructure. It recently published this report on AI's water demands.

init1_name1: The Fairness Innovation Challenge
init1_justif1: The government-led activity by the Department for Science Innovation and Technology in collaboration with Innovate UK and supported by the Information Commissioner's Office and Equality and Human Rights Commission is a grant programme (Fairness Innovation Challenge 2023–March 2025). It addresses Fairness and Non-Discrimination by funding projects that develop practical tools and methods to detect assess and mitigate bias in AI systems across sectors such as healthcare finance education and recruitment promoting equitable AI outcomes.
init1_name2: AI tools in recruitment Audit outcomes report (ICO)
init1_justif2: ICO’s audit initiative advances fairness and non-discrimination by requiring recruiters and AI providers to process data fairly monitor and address accuracy/bias issues and ensure any special-category data used for bias monitoring is adequate and accurate (not inferred/estimated) (pp. 6–7 “Recommendation: Fairness”). It mandates transparency/explainability—tell candidates what data is processed the logic of predictions and how data is used for training/testing (p. 6 “Transparency and explainability”)—and to complete/maintain DPIAs (p. 7). The programme issued ~300 recommendations all accepted (p. 3).

init1_name1: An industry guide for addressing technology-facilitatedgender-based violence through Safety by Design
init1_justif1: The “Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence: Safety by Design Industry Guide” led by the Australian Government’s eSafety Commissioner establishes structured design standards and risk management protocols for identifying and mitigating AI-enabled gender-based harms. It requires developers to anticipate and address risks such as harassment deepfakes and abuse that disproportionately affect women and marginalized groups (p. 4). By embedding safety-by-design impact assessments and preventative safeguards into system development it directly advances gender equality by reducing discriminatory and gendered harms in AI-driven digital environments (pp. 5 23).

init1_name1: ICO Regulatory Sandbox
init1_justif1: The ICO Regulatory Sandbox helps organisations design AI systems that comply with UK GDPR including Article 22 on human review of automated decisions. It provides direct support for implementing human oversight explainability and risk mitigation in AI projects. Participating organisations such as Good With Onfido and Tribela worked with the ICO to embed mechanisms for human control and accountability into their AI systems.
init1_name2: ICO Audits and DPIA Framework
init1_justif2: The ICO’s audits and DPIA framework operationalises Human Oversight and Determination by requiring organisations to identify and mitigate risks from high-risk AI and automated decision-making. Under UK GDPR Art. 35 DPIAs must assess necessity proportionality safeguards and rights (including Art. 22 rights to human intervention and contestation). ICO DPIA guidance templates and audits check that AI systems include meaningful human review and fallback procedures before deployment.

init1_name1: AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit
init1_type1: Risk management protocols or fallback procedures
init1_justif1: The 'AI and data protection risk toolkit' launched by the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) helps organisations identify AI-specific risks to individuals' rights and freedoms and provides practical mitigation steps categorized as 'Must' (legal requirements) 'Should' (best practice) and 'Could' (optional measures). Organisations use the toolkit to identify AI risks and incorporate findings into their Data Protection Impact Assessment (requirement under Section 64 of the Data Protection Act 2018) documenting risk assessments and mitigation measures to demonstrate compliance.

init1_name1: Information Commissioners Office Regulating AI The Information Commissioners Offices Strategic Approach
init1_justif1: The Information Commissioner's Office published this strategic report outlining its approach to regulating AI under the UK GDPR. It demonstrates labour protections through enforcement actions including orders to Serco Leisure to cease using facial recognition and fingerprint scanning for employee attendance monitoring (page 15). The ICO applies core UK GDPR principles fairness transparency security and accountability)to workplace AI systems requiring employers to justify biometric surveillance and algorithmic management tools. The report provides guidance on automated decision-making in employment contexts supporting Article 22 protections ensuring human oversight and workers' rights to contest unfair algorithmic determinations.
init1_name2: Information Commissioners Office Enforcement Notice against Serco Leisure for Unlawful Biometric Monitoring of Employees
init1_justif2: The Information Commissioner's Office issued enforcement notices ordering Serco Leisure and seven community leisure trusts to stop using facial recognition and fingerprint scanning to monitor 2000+ employees' attendance at 38 facilities. The ICO found violations of Articles 5(1)(a) 6 and 9 of the UK GDPR ruling that biometric processing was neither necessary nor proportionate given less intrusive alternatives existed. The action enforces workers' data protection rights against algorithmic surveillance demonstrating active implementation of UK GDPR protections in employment contexts.

init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: The Algorithmic Transparency Records Repository is the UK's operational public registry of government algorithmic systems directly fulfilling the indicator's core requirement. Led by the Government Digital Service (GDS) within the Department for Science Innovation and Technology (DSIT) it provides public access to standardised records containing: tool name and purpose description of function responsible agency data used human oversight arrangements and risk assessments. Citizens can search records by organisation sector geography and model capability.  The repository enables proactive transparency allowing civil society and the public to scrutinise government algorithmic tool usage without requiring Freedom of Info requests.

init1_justif1: Crown Commercial Service’s RM6200 Artificial Intelligence Dynamic Purchasing System (AI DPS) operationalises AI procurement by giving public-sector buyers a ready route-to-market to run competitions and award contracts for AI services. The official Find a Tender notice states Crown Commercial Service “has set up a Dynamic Purchasing System for the supply of artificial intelligence services” enabling ongoing supplier participation and buyer call-offs. The Guidelines for AI procurement explicitly point buyers to specialist AI procurement routes and name the Crown Commercial Service Dynamic Purchasing System for AI (RM6200) as an example route-to-market.

init1_justif1: The UK Government’s “Artificial Intelligence resources for the public sector” is a government-led activity for civil service AI upskilling coordinated and delivered through official platforms such as Government Skills Civil Service Learning and the Government Campus. It provides structured AI training pathways guidance and learning materials tailored to public sector roles including different levels from basic awareness to more advanced application. The programme is aimed at civil servants across departments supporting consistent skill development within government.
init1_name2: Civil Service Learning AI Courses
init1_justif2: The Civil Service Learning AI Courses directly implement the AI Playbook's skills development provisions by providing free AI training to all civil servants. Launched in August 2024 by the Cabinet Office and Government Skills the initiative offers courses covering AI fundamentals ethics generative AI large language models machine learning deep learning NLP and computer vision. The courses target all five learner groups identified in the framework (from beginners to senior leaders) and are delivered in multiple formats (video interactive written). This addresses the indicator's requirements for capacity building and training programs for public servants on AI applications and risks.

init1_name1: TechFirst Programme
init1_justif1: TechFirst is a £187 million government programme launched on 9 June 2025 that directly implements the AI Opportunities Action Plan's Recommendation 19 to 'ensure its lifelong skills programme is ready for AI.' It delivers AI upskilling across four strands: TechYouth (£24m) provides AI skills training to 1 million secondary students; TechGrad (£96.8m) offers scholarships in AI and computer science; TechExpert (£48.4m) funds 500 doctoral students in AI research; and TechLocal (£18m) helps SMEs adopt AI. The programme aims to 'train up people of all ages and backgrounds for the tech careers of the future.'
init1_name2: Government-Industry Partnership for AI Skills (75 Million Workers)
init1_justif2: This government-industry partnership launched on 9 June 2025 commits to upskilling 7.5 million UK workers (20% of the workforce) with essential AI skills by 2030. Major tech companies including Google Microsoft IBM Amazon BT Accenture Sage and Salesforce have pledged to make high-quality AI training materials freely available to businesses of all sizes. Training focuses on enabling workers to use AI systems such as chatbots and large language models to boost productivity with sector-specific training developed for industries including healthcare finance and manufacturing.

init1_name1: Implementation Guide for the AI Cyber Security Code of Practice
init1_justif1: The Implementation Guide commissioned by The Department for Science Innovation and Technology provides practical guidance for implementing the 13 security principles across the AI lifecycle. It addresses security obligations through: threat modelling and risk management for AI-specific attacks(Principle 3); safeguards requiring systems to withstand adversarial attacks (Principle 2); incident management and recovery plans (Principle 6); pre-deployment testing procedures (Principle 9); and monitoring for anomalies and security breaches (Principle 12). The Guide offers scenario-driven implementation examples role-specific advice for and mappings to international frameworks to help achieve baseline AI cyber security compliance.
init1_name2: The UKs AI Security Institutes Research Agenda
init1_justif2: The UK government’s AI Security Institute research agenda constitutes a government-led operational programme for the implementation of AI safety and security. Developed by the Department for Science Innovation and Technology and executed through the AI Security Institute it sets out concrete ongoing activities to address AI-related risks. The agenda operationalises state priorities through targeted research programmes on threat identification system evaluation and alignment/control mechanisms.
init1_name3: Research and analysis Safety and security risks of generative AI
init1_justif3: The UK Department for Science Innovation & Technology (DSIT) report “Safety and Security Risks of Generative Artificial Intelligence to 2025 (Annex B)” is a government-led research analysis focused on the safety and security of AI systems particularly in relation to cybersecurity and system vulnerabilities. It examines how generative AI can be exploited to enhance cyberattacks fraud impersonation and other malicious activities while also highlighting risks arising from the integration of AI into critical digital and physical infrastructure.

init1_justif1: The government-led activity by the Government Digital Service is the implementation of the Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard (ATRS) introduced in 2023 and expanded through 2025. It addresses Transparency and Explainability by establishing a standard requiring public sector organisations to document how algorithmic tools function their purpose data inputs and their role in decision-making.

init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The ICO Annual Report and Performance Reporting Framework supports transparency and explainability in AI by publicly reporting on how the regulator handles complaints investigations and regulatory action including cases involving automated decision-making. The Annual Report’s sections on “Complaints and concerns” and “Regulating data protection” describe how individuals raise concerns about data processing and how the ICO assesses compliance including failures to explain or justify automated decisions. By publishing statistics outcomes and regulatory priorities the reporting framework makes the operation and enforcement of transparency and explainability obligations in automated decision-making visible and accountable.

Section 4.1 (pp.29–30) presents policy options to mitigate impacts such as improving environmental data reporting and encouraging more resource-efficient mo

init1_name1: Federal AI Use Case Inventories + public posting
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: This initiative addresses fairness and non-discrimination through mandatory monitoring and evaluation (M&E) mechanisms for federal AI use. OMB Memorandum M-24-10 requires agencies to inventory AI use cases annually and publicly report which systems are “rights-impacting” including risks of inequitable or discriminatory outcomes and how they are managed (Sec. 3(a)(iv) p. 5). For such systems agencies must conduct ongoing monitoring to detect AI-enabled discrimination perform periodic human reviews and mitigate or discontinue use if discriminatory impacts cannot be addressed (Sec. 5(c)(iv)(D)–(F) pp. 19–20; Sec. 5(c)(v)(C) p. 23). These requirements institutionalize continuous oversight of bias and fairness impacts (p. 21).

init1_name1: Healt Data Technology and Interoperability Certification Program Updates Algorithm Transparency and Information Sharing
init1_justif1: The HTI-1 Final Rule advances Human Oversight and Determination by regulating AI/ML-enabled Predictive Decision Support Interventions (DSIs) used in certified health IT. It requires developers to provide plain-language explanations disclose intended use limitations and risks and implement risk-management practices enabling clinicians to independently evaluate contextualize and override AI outputs rather than defer to them. The rule explicitly links transparency to preventing over-reliance and supporting informed human judgment (§170.315(b)(11) pp. 37–46; discussion on independent review and avoiding over-reliance pp. 41–45).
init1_name2: Health Data Technology and Interoperability Certification Program Updates Algorithm Transparency and Information Sharing
init1_type2: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif2: The HTI-1 Final Rule supports Human Oversight and Determination through ongoing monitoring and surveillance under the ONC Health IT Certification Program. Certified health IT with AI/ML-enabled Predictive Decision Support Interventions (DSIs) is subject to post-certification surveillance maintenance of certification and corrective actions ensuring systems continue to operate as decision-support tools rather than replacing human judgment. Monitoring focuses on compliance with transparency risk-management and documentation requirements enabling clinicians to reassess trust and intervene over time (§170.315(b)(11) pp. 37–46; Program surveillance and enforcement discussion pp. 240–247).
init1_name3: Health Data Technology and Interoperability Certification Program Updates Algorithm Transparency and Information Sharing
init1_justif3: The HTI-1 Final Rule reinforces Human Oversight and Determination through administrative enforcement actions that ensure AI remains a support tool subject to human judgment. Non-compliance with transparency risk-management and documentation requirements for AI/ML-enabled Predictive Decision Support Interventions can trigger information-blocking investigations and civil monetary penalties enforced by HHS Office of Inspector General (OIG). This enforcement framework incentivizes organizations to maintain conditions that enable clinicians to independently review question and override AI outputs preserving human agency (§170.315(b)(11) pp. 37–46; Enforcement and Penalties pp. 245–247).

See “Access the 2024 Training Series Acquisition Track” (lines 68–77).

init1_name2: Supercharging Americas AI Workforce

init1_justif1: Agency M-25-21 Compliance Plans operationalize AI safety/security by translating “minimum risk management practices” into agency controls. Example: the HHS Compliance Plan lists “Cybersecurity” as a key domain and commits to speeding AI through the Authority to Operate (ATO) while enabling “develop test and maintain” AI with monitoring capabilities (Sec. “Removing Barriers to the Use of AI” p.4). init1_name1: Recommendations on Algorithmic Transparency
init1_justif1: The document promotes transparency and explainability by requiring agencies to disclose how AI systems work their data sources purposes and limitations (pp. 4–5). It clarifies that people must be informed when decisions involve AI and be given meaningful explanations of system outcomes. It also mandates proactive publication of system details—objectives processes data types and impacts—to ensure understandable and accessible information for the public (pp. 9 12–13).

init1_name1: One Million AI Leaders
init1_justif1: The program led by Ministry of Digital Technologies “One Million Artificial Intelligence Leaders”the educational platforms aistudy.uz and omp.aistudy.uz have been launched. Platforms offer five free online courses aimed at training young people with modern knowledge and skills in the field of AI. The program is scheduled to run until 2027 and aims to train One million young specialists in the field of AI and more than 1000 trainers who will teach computer science teachers in schools and universities. The project will also cover schoolchildren with a national AI competition. The programme doesn’t fully cover formal curriculum reform across all levels it does contribute to labour&skills via large-scale public training.

init1_name1: Seminar on the positive and negative aspects of AI in spreading fake information and disinformation
init1_justif1: The Ministry of Digital Technologies held a capacity-building seminar (May 29 2024) on AI’s role in spreading fake information/disinformation training information services on risks and countermeasures. Deepfakes fabricated images and news and apps that help distinguish false reports supporting prevention and response to AI-facilitated harms were explicitly discussed during the seminar.

init1_name1: Development of National AI Model
init1_justif1: In July 2025 Uzbekistan announced the development of a National AI Language Model emphasizing both technological and cultural localization. Officials noted that current global language models often misinterpret Uzbek names faces and cultural symbols reinforcing stereotypes. The national model aims to ensure accurate representation of Uzbek identity language history and heritage—strengthening cultural and linguistic diversity within AI systems.

init1_name1: Green Data Centers A New Infrastructure Hub
init1_justif1: Government-led initiative include such examples as push for green renewable-powered data centers to support AI workloads: at ICT Week 2024 the Ministry of Digital Technologies and IT Park signed an MoU with DataVolt for a 10 MW data center powered by renewables; the government also announced plans with DataVolt for three green data centers (up to 500 MW by 2032). These actions explicitly tie AI infrastructure to lower-impact energy sources.

init1_name1: Women & AI Forum 2025
init1_justif1: Organized under the initiatives of GAP Women IT Union and IT Ladies with support from the IT Education Association and co-organized by the Ministry of Digital Technologies the forum served as a platform for dialogue experience exchange and presentation of success stories in the digital economy. Discussions centered on the influence of AI on education entrepreneurship and professional growth while participants showcased programs that encourage girls and women to pursue careers in technology.

init1_name1: Parliamentary Discussion of the Draft law On Regulating Relations Arising from the Use of AI
init1_justif1: The government-led draft AI bill implements Human Oversight & Determination by (1) mandating labels for AI-generated content to inform users when outputs are machine-produced and (2)it emphasizes that when making decisions regarding human rights and freedoms one cannot rely solely on the conclusions of information resources and systems created using AI. These provisions were publicly outlined by the Legislative Chamber and ministries during April–May 2025 deliberations and in later adoption reports; the full draft text is not public.

init1_name1: Platform and Guide to using AI in teaching and learning
init1_justif1: The platform is operated by the Vietnam Institute of Educational Sciences (VNIES) under the Ministry of Education and Training and serves as a dedicated portal for AI in education. Relevant sections (as seen in the Guide) include Part I (Introduction to AI in education and AI tools) Part III (AI competency requirements and guidance for learners) and Part IV (AI competency requirements and guidance for teachers) which together aim to build foundational understanding and responsible use of AI in educational settings.

init1_name1: Vietnam Anti-fake news platform
init1_type1: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) reports or platforms
init1_justif1: This website is set up and managed by the Vietnamese government previously the Ministry of Information and Communications currently the Ministry of Culture Sports and Tourism under the Authority of Broadcasting and Electronic Information. This platform allows users to report cases of misinformation and violence on the digital space which covers AI-facilitated misinformation and violence. One of the news featured on the fakenews website explicitly was targeted at AI violence/misinformation see link https://web.archive.org/web/20260128052015/https://tingia.gov.vn/cuc-du-lich.html
init1_name2: Nationwide campaign to combat exploitation of ChatGPT and AI to distort and sabotage Vietnams politics
init1_justif2: These series of articles have been a systematic attempt to prevent use of AI technologies in spreading misinformation to distort Vietnam's politics as part of implementation of Resolution No. 35-NQ/TW dated October 22 2018 of the Politburo on strengthening the protection of the Party's ideological foundation and combating erroneous and hostile viewpoints in the new situation. The Steering Committee 35 (SC35) - a special organization of the Communist Party of Vietnam operating from the central to local levels to protect the Party's ideological foundation and combat erroneous and hostile viewpoints especially in cyberspace - leads this nationwide campaign.
init1_name3: Lao Cai Provincial Police punished 3 cases of using AI images to post false information on social networks
init1_justif3: Lao Cai Provincial Police punished 3 cases of using AI images to post false information on social networks. After verification the Lao Cai Ward Police confirmed that this was fake information and the image was created using AI technology. The account holder N.V.T. (born in 1989 residing in Cam Duong Ward) admitted to posting the image to 'gain likes and views.' The Lao Cai Ward Police have issued a report and a decision to impose an administrative fine of 7.5 million VND on N.V.T. The Lao Cai Provincial Police said that spreading fake news and false information on the internet is particularly dangerous at a time when the locality is focusing intensely on disaster prevention and control.

init1_name1: The Cybersecurity Department recommends the 4T principle when allowing children to participate in the online environment and use AI tools
init1_drive1: N/A. This is an article not a document.
init1_justif1: At the Ministry of Information and Communications' regular November press conference the Deputy Director of the National Cybersecurity Monitoring Center (NCSC) shared some of the risks that artificial intelligence (AI) applications can pose to children: 1) access to inappropriate information; 2) leakage of private and personal information; 3) being lured or enticed into participating in dangerous challenges; 4) acting on AI advice including biased inaccurate harmful or misleading content. The Information Security Department recommends adhering to the '4T principles' which include: compliance intelligence caution and kindness.

init1_justif1: The Au Lac AI Alliance is to develop LLM that can process Vietnamese accurately naturally and in accordance with Vietnamese culture and identity thereby improving public knowledge and promoting national economic development. The activities of the Alliance are based on three principles: Consensus - Respect - Open Community. One of the core functions is Building AI standards and policies aiming to contribute opinions on policies standards and codes related to AI to ensure safe responsible and legally compliant products. This is a gov-led initiative because Professor Dr. Nguyen Xuan Thang Politburo member Director of Ho Chi Minh National Academy of Politics Chairman of the Central Council of Theory was one founding member.

After identifying risks organizations then assign the likelihood of occurrence and analyze the potential negative impact of the identified risks. This facilitates the risk assessment of the AI ??model of interest against the identified risks of the risk identification phase. init1_name1: Draft Law on Employment (amended)
init1_justif1: At parliamentary discussions on 7th May 2025 on the draft Law on Employment (amended) National Assembly deputies proposed having policies to support workers affected by the conversion of automation technology and artificial intelligence (AI) to ensure the ability to adapt and change jobs. 1) Add a separate clause 'have policies to support workers affected by the transformation of automation technology and artificial intelligence'; 2) Mandate gov agencies to update labor market data at least quarterly synthesize and publish periodic labor situation reports every 6 months. The labor market database must be synchronously connected and shared with the population tax insurance and vocational education databases.

init1_name1: Explanation to the public on official news outlets The process of handling traffic violations from AI camera
init1_justif1: The initiative led by the Traffic Police Department (C?c CSGT) under Vietnam’s Ministry of Public Security provides public information about the operation of an AI-based traffic monitoring and enforcement system. Through the explanation of how the system detects violations using surveillance cameras processes data and requires human verification before penalties are issued the authorities disclose key aspects of how AI is used in public service enforcement thus contributes to public disclosure of government AI use by explaining the system’s functioning and role in traffic enforcement.

init1_justif1: On 21/08/2025 Deputy Prime Minister attended the launching ceremony of the “Vietnam AI Academy” program. init1_name1: National Standards on AI and Big Data
init1_justif1: On pages 2 and 8 of TCVN DIN SPEC 92001-2:202x DIN SPEC proposes to distinguish between high- and low-risk AI modules related to safety security privacy and ethics. In this DIN SPEC each AI module is considered either high-risk or low-risk. AI modules related to safety security privacy and ethics are classified as high-risk (potential) modules. On December 29 2023 the Ministry of Science and Technology issued Decision No. 3298/QD-BKHCN announcing this National Standard. This decision takes effect from the date of signing. The General Department of Standards Metrology and Quality is the implementing agency. https://docs.google.com/document/d/1KYcXQNZ3S5iEmTeMFQoMnzGlJTjpU8ELMopSYIQQ63s/edit?usp=sharing

init1_name1: Information Session by Independent Broadcasting Authority Urging ethical use of AI
init1_justif1: The Independent Broadcasting Authority’s initiative promotes the ethical use of AI by advocating for transparency and explainability in AI operations within the broadcasting sector. It encourages clear communication about how AI systems function ensuring stakeholders understand AI decision-making processes. By urging adherence to ethical standards the initiative seeks to build public trust prevent misuse and foster accountability in AI deployment thereby supporting transparency and explainability as core principles in AI governance.

init1_name1: Women in ICT Conference 2025
init1_justif1: The conference provided a platform to deepen awareness of how gender inequalities shape women’s participation in the digital economy. Participants discussed the specific barriers women face in accessing and using digital technologies including limited access to affordable devices and connectivity lower levels of digital skills and gendered online harassment. Through the discussions on cybersecurity speakers highlighted how women and girls are disproportionately exposed to online abuse and privacy violations underscoring the need for gender-sensitive cyber safety policies complaint mechanisms and survivor-centred support services.In relation to AI entrepreneurship and innovation the conference drew attention to the risks of bias.

init1_name1: Digital Skills Program
init1_justif1: The Zimbabwe Ministry of ICT Postal and Courier Services  launched a Digital Skills Programme (launch April 10-11 2025) which explicitly includes AI-related training as part of its upskilling agenda. According to reports the initiative aims to train 1.5 million individuals in “Programming Fundamentals Data Science Fundamentals Android Kotlin Developer Fundamentals and AI Fundamentals.” By integrating AI fundamentals into a large­scale national training effort the project supports both reskilling for those shifting into tech roles and upskilling by building deeper competencies in AI/data science in Zimbabwe’s workforce.
